# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Surface Coatings — Structured Logic

Surface coatings are classified based on **three fundamental criteria**:

### 2.1.1 Classification by **Coating Material Chemistry**

**A. Metallic Coatings** — A metal layer deposited on a different metal substrate.
- Examples: Zinc (Zn), Tin (Sn), Chromium (Cr), Nickel (Ni), Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Aluminum (Al), Copper (Cu).
- **Logic:** Provide **sacrificial protection** (Zn on steel) or **barrier protection** (Cr, Ni).
- **Why:** Sacrificial coatings corrode preferentially to protect the substrate (galvanic series principle).

**B. Non-Metallic (Inorganic) Coatings** — Ceramic or oxide-based layers.
- Examples: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{TiO}_2$, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$, $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$, TiN, TiC, WC.
- **Logic:** High hardness, high-temperature stability, chemical inertness.
- **Why:** Used in cutting tools, turbine blades, biomedical implants.

**C. Organic Coatings** — Polymer or resin-based layers.
- Examples: Paints, lacquers, varnishes, epoxies, polyurethanes, PTFE.
- **Logic:** Barrier protection, aesthetics, chemical resistance, low cost.
- **Why:** Widely used in automotive, marine, aerospace, and architectural sectors.

**D. Conversion Coatings** — Formed *by chemical reaction* of the substrate itself.
- Examples: Anodizing (Al), Phosphating (steel), Chromating (Zn, Al).
- **Logic:** The substrate is *converted* into a protective oxide/phosphate/chromate layer — so the coating is *integrally part of the surface*, not a deposited foreign layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Conversion coatings are *chemically grown* from the substrate, while metallic/organic coatings are *physically deposited* onto the substrate. This distinction is a frequently asked question.

---

### 2.1.2 Classification by **Deposition Technique**

| Method | Sub-category | Coating Type | Typical Thickness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electroplating** | Electrochemical | Metallic (Cr, Ni, Zn) | 5 – 50 µm |
| **Electroless Plating** | Chemical (autocatalytic) | Ni-P, Cu, Au | 5 – 30 µm |
| **Hot Dipping** | Immersion | Zn, Sn, Al (galvanizing) | 25 – 100 µm |
| **Spray Coating** | Thermal/mechanical | Metals, ceramics, polymers | 50 – 500 µm |
| **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** | Vacuum | TiN, CrN, AlTiN | 1 – 10 µm |
| **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** | Chemical reaction | TiC, SiC, DLC | 1 – 20 µm |
| **Sol-Gel** | Chemical solution | Oxides, hybrid coatings | < 1 – 5 µm |
| **Powder Coating** | Electrostatic | Polymer powders | 30 – 150 µm |
| **Anodizing** | Electrochemical conversion | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | 5 – 25 µm |
| **Phosphating** | Chemical conversion | Zinc/Manganese phosphate | 5 – 50 µm |

---

### 2.1.3 Classification by **Functional Purpose**

1. **Protective Coatings** — Anti-corrosion, anti-wear, anti-oxidation.
2. **Decorative Coatings** — Aesthetic enhancement (chromium on automotive parts).
3. **Functional Coatings** — Electrical, magnetic, optical, thermal, biomedical.
4. **Tribological Coatings** — Reduce friction (DLC, MoS₂, PTFE).
5. **Smart/Intelligent Coatings** — Self-healing, anti-fouling, sensor-integrated.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **directly applicable** to numerical problems in KTU ESE and module tests. Memorize units and sign conventions.

### 2.2.1 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Electroplating & Electroless)

**Faraday's First Law:**
$$ m = Z \cdot I \cdot t $$

**Faraday's Second Law:**
$$ Z = \frac{A}{n \cdot F} $$

**Combined (Most Used Form):**
$$ m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Mass of deposited coating | grams (g) |
| $Z$ | Electrochemical equivalent | g/C |
| $A$ | Atomic weight of depositing metal | g/mol |
| $n$ | Valency (electrons transferred) | dimensionless |
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | **96485 C/mol** (≈ 96500 C/mol) |
| $I$ | Current | Ampere (A) |
| $t$ | Time | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that **valency (n)** changes with the ionic state. For example, $\text{Cr}^{6+}$ from $\text{CrO}_3$ has **n = 6**, not 3.

### 2.2.2 Coating Thickness from Mass Deposited

$$ T = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A_s} $$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Coating thickness | cm or µm |
| $m$ | Mass deposited | g |
| $\rho$ | Density of coating material | g/cm³ |
| $A_s$ | Surface area coated | cm² |

### 2.2.3 Current Density Relation

$$ J = \frac{I}{A_s} $$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ | Current density | A/cm² or A/dm² |
| $I$ | Current | A |
| $A_s$ | Cathode (substrate) area | cm² or dm² |

### 2.2.4 Cathode (Plating) Efficiency

$$ \eta_c = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\% $$

> Using Faraday's law for $m_{theoretical}$.

### 2.2.5 PVD Deposition Rate (Basic)

$$ R_{PVD} = \frac{K \cdot I \cdot t}{\rho \cdot A_s} $$

Where $K$ is a process constant dependent on sputtering yield and target-substrate geometry.

### 2.2.6 Corrosion Rate (for Coated Samples)

$$ CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{\rho \cdot A \cdot T} $$

Where $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ (mm/year conversion constant), $W$ = mass loss (g), $T$ = exposure time (hr).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Zn-galvanized steel bodies (corrosion), Cr-plated bumpers (decorative + wear), PTFE coatings (low friction).
- **Aerospace:** MCrAlY bond coats + TBC (thermal barrier) on turbine blades; PVD TiAlN on compressor blades.
- **Cutting Tools:** CVD TiC/Al₂O₃ on carbide inserts; PVD TiN, AlTiN, CrN on HSS drills.
- **Marine:** Cathodic protection + epoxy coatings on ship hulls; Cu-Ni alloy overlays.
- **Biomedical:** Hydroxyapatite (HAp) on Ti implants; DLC on orthopedic joints.
- **Electronics:** ENIG (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold) on PCBs; Au wire bonding pads.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Faraday's Combined Law (Universally Tested in KTU ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU OEMUT833 ESE for the topic of electroplating and metallic coatings.

### Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's First Law

> *"The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte."*

Mathematically:

$$ m \propto Q $$

$$ m \propto I \cdot t $$

Introducing a constant of proportionality $Z$:

$$ m = Z \cdot I \cdot t \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} $$

Where $Z$ is called the **Electrochemical Equivalent** with units **g/C**.

---

### Step 2 — Faraday's Second Law

> *"The mass of substance liberated by a given quantity of electricity is proportional to its chemical equivalent weight."*

Mathematically:

$$ Z \propto A \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z \propto \frac{A}{n} $$

$$ Z = \frac{1}{F} \cdot \frac{A}{n} \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} $$

Where:
- $A$ = Atomic (molar) mass in **g/mol**,
- $n$ = Valency (number of electrons in the electrode reaction),
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol** (approximately **96500 C/mol** in textbooks).

---

### Step 3 — Substitution to Get the Combined Law

Substituting Equation 2 into Equation 1:

$$ m = \left( \frac{1}{F} \cdot \frac{A}{n} \right) \cdot I \cdot t $$

Rearranging:

$$ m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 3 — Combined Faraday's Law)} $$

This is the **master equation** for all electroplating numerical problems.

---

### Step 4 — Derivation of Coating Thickness Formula

Starting from Equation 3 and knowing the physical relationship between mass, density, area, and thickness:

$$ m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A_s \cdot T $$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of the coating metal (g/cm³),
- $V$ = Volume of deposited coating (cm³),
- $A_s$ = Surface area coated (cm²),
- $T$ = Thickness of the coating (cm).

Equating the two expressions for mass:

$$ \rho \cdot A_s \cdot T = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

Solving for coating thickness $T$:

$$ T = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s} $$

Converting to more useful units (T in µm, $A_s$ in dm², $J$ in A/dm²):

$$ T_{\mu m} = \frac{A \cdot J \cdot t \cdot 10^4}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} $$

> Where $t$ is in seconds and $J$ is in A/dm².

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Numerical on Faraday's law + thickness calculation is a **guaranteed Part B sub-question**.

### Problem:

A steel component of surface area **$200 \, \text{cm}^2$** is to be electroplated with **nickel** using a current of **$10 \, \text{A}$** for **$45$ minutes**. Calculate:
1. The mass of nickel deposited.
2. The thickness of the nickel coating.
3. The cathode current density.

**Given Data:**
- Atomic mass of Ni, $A = 58.71 \, \text{g/mol}$
- Valency, $n = 2$ (since $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni}$)
- Density of Ni, $\rho = 8.9 \, \text{g/cm}^3$
- Faraday constant, $F = 96500 \, \text{C/mol}$

---

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Calculate Time in Seconds:**
$$ t = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \, \text{s} $$

**Step 2 — Apply Combined Faraday's Law:**

$$ m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

$$ m = \frac{58.71 \times 10 \times 2700}{2 \times 96500} $$

$$ m = \frac{1585170}{193000} $$

$$ m = 8.21 \, \text{g} $$

**Result:** Mass of Ni deposited ≈ **8.21 g**

---

**Step 3 — Calculate Cathode Current Density:**

$$ J = \frac{I}{A_s} = \frac{10 \, \text{A}}{200 \, \text{cm}^2} $$

$$ J = 0.05 \, \text{A/cm}^2 $$

**Equivalently:** $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2$ (since $1 \, \text{dm}^2 = 100 \, \text{cm}^2$).

---

**Step 4 — Calculate Coating Thickness:**

$$ T = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A_s} = \frac{8.21}{8.9 \times 200} $$

$$ T = \frac{8.21}{1780} $$

$$ T = 4.61 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{cm} $$

Converting to micrometers:

$$ T = 4.61 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4 \, \mu m = 46.1 \, \mu m $$

**Result:** Coating thickness ≈ **46.1 µm**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electroplating vs. Electroless Plating

> This is the **single most-tested comparative question** in KTU ESE.

| Parameter | Electroplating | Electroless Plating |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | External DC power source | Autocatalytic chemical reduction |
| **Current Required?** | **Yes** (external current) | **No** (no external current) |
| **Thickness Uniformity** | Poor on complex geometries (edges accumulate) | **Excellent** — uniform even on intricate shapes |
| **Equipment Cost** | Higher (rectifier, anode, bus bars) | Lower (only tank + heater) |
| **Bath Stability** | Moderate | Requires careful pH & stabilizer control |
| **Typical Examples** | Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn, Au | Ni-P, Ni-B, Cu, Au, Ag |
| **Deposit Purity** | High | High (but with P/B co-deposition) |
| **Adhesion** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Good | **Superior** (amorphous Ni-P) |
| **Cost per Unit Area** | Lower | Higher |
| **Application** | Decorative + functional | Engineering (precision, uniform thickness) |

---

## 3.4 Comparison — PVD vs. CVD

| Parameter | PVD | CVD |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Temperature** | Lower (200–500 °C) | Higher (600–1100 °C) |
| **Substrate Distortion** | Minimal | Possible (thermal stress) |
| **Coating Density** | Good | **Excellent (near-theoretical)** |
| **Coating Adhesion** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Thickness Control** | Precise | Precise |
| **Equipment Cost** | High (vacuum) | High (vacuum + gas handling) |
| **Coating Examples** | TiN, CrN, AlTiN, DLC | TiC, SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, DLC |
| **Coverage of Complex Shapes** | Line-of-sight | **Conformal (all surfaces)** |
| **Deposition Rate** | Slower | Moderate to fast |

## 4.2 Electroplating Process Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC Power Supply Rectifier] -->|+ Anode Lead| AN[Anode Coating Metal]
    DC -->|- Cathode Lead| CA[Cathode Substrate Workpiece]
    AN -->|Ion Exchange| EL[Electrolyte Salt Solution]
    CA -->|Deposition| EL
    EL -->|Circulation Filtration| PUMP[Pump and Filter Unit]
    PUMP -->|Recycle| EL
    HEAT[Heater and Temperature Controller] -->|Maintain T| EL
```

**Process Flow Logic:**
1. **DC source** drives conventional current from anode (+) to cathode (–).
2. At the **anode**: Metal dissolves → $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$.
3. At the **cathode (substrate)**: Metal ions deposit → $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}$.
4. **Electrolyte** carries ions and maintains conductivity.
5. **Pump + filter** removes particulates and ensures uniform composition.
6. **Heater** maintains optimum bath temperature (e.g., 45–60 °C for Ni plating).

---

```mermaid
    A[Application Requirement] --> Q1{Function Needed}
    Q1 -->|Corrosion Protection| B1[Metallic Coating Zn Al or Organic Epoxy]
    Q1 -->|Wear Resistance| B2[Hard Ceramic TiN Al2O3 or Conversion Anodizing]
    Q1 -->|Decorative| B3[Cr Plating Lacquer Enamel]
    Q1 -->|High Temperature| B4[MCrAlY TBC Ceramic Coating]
    Q1 -->|Electrical| B5[Cu Sn Ag Au ENIG]
    Q1 -->|Biomedical| B6[HAp Ti DLC]

    B1 --> S1[Substrate and Environment Check]
    B2 --> S1
    B3 --> S1
    B4 --> S1
    B5 --> S1
    B6 --> S1
    S1 --> OUT[Final Coating Selection]
```

## 4.4 Coating Thickness Distribution — Electroplating vs. Electroless (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUB[Complex Shaped Substrate] --> EP[Electroplating]
    SUB --> ELP[Electroless Plating]

    EP --> E1[Sharp Edges: High Current Density - Thicker Coating]
    EP --> E2[Recesses: Low Current Density - Thinner Coating]
    EP --> E3[Non Uniform Thickness]

    ELP --> L1[All Surfaces: Uniform Autocatalytic Deposition]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between electroplating and electroless plating with respect to (i) driving force, (ii) thickness uniformity, and (iii) equipment complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Electroplating | Electroless Plating |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | External DC current supply | Autocatalytic chemical reduction (no external current) |
| **Thickness Uniformity** | Poor on complex shapes; edges receive higher current | Excellent; uniform even on intricate geometries |
| **Equipment Complexity** | Higher (rectifier, anode, bus bars, cathode connections) | Lower (immersion tank, heater, pH controller) |

> *(1 mark per correct row, full table or equivalent points acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any three differences between Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Process Temperature:** PVD operates at lower temperatures (200–500 °C); CVD requires higher temperatures (600–1100 °C).
2. **Coating Conformality:** PVD is line-of-sight (poor on hidden surfaces); CVD is conformal (uniform on all exposed surfaces).
3. **Chemical Reaction:** PVD is purely physical (sputtering/evaporation); CVD involves chemical reaction of precursor gases at the substrate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of surface coatings** based on coating material chemistry with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **sacrificial and barrier protection** mechanisms in metallic coatings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **process of electroplating** with a neat schematic. Derive Faraday's laws of electrolysis and calculate the **mass and thickness** of nickel deposited when a current of **$12 \, \text{A}$** is passed for **$1.5$ hours** through a nickel sulphate solution on a steel substrate of area **$250 \, \text{cm}^2$**. Given: $A_{\text{Ni}} = 58.71$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.9 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, $F = 96500 \, \text{C/mol}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A:

**(a) Classification of Surface Coatings (7 marks):**

Surface coatings are classified into four major categories based on **coating material chemistry**:

**1. Metallic Coatings (2 marks):**
A layer of metal deposited on a substrate of different composition.
- **Examples:** Zinc (galvanizing), Chromium (decorative), Nickel (engineering), Tin (food cans), Aluminum (high-temperature protection).
- **Protection Mechanism:**
  - **Sacrificial Protection:** The coating metal is **more anodic** than the substrate in the galvanic series and corrodes preferentially. Example: Zinc on steel — even if scratched, Zn continues to protect Fe cathodically.
  - **Barrier Protection:** The coating acts as a physical barrier preventing the electrolyte from reaching the substrate. Example: Chromium on steel — purely barrier (noble coating).
  - **Galvanic Series Logic:** Sacrificial coatings must lie **above** the substrate in the galvanic series (e.g., Zn above Fe).

**2. Non-Metallic Inorganic Coatings (2 marks):**
Ceramic, oxide, carbide, or nitride layers.
- **Examples:** $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (anodized Al), TiN (cutting tools), TiC, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$, DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon).
- **Properties:** Extreme hardness, high-temperature stability, chemical inertness.
- **Application:** Cutting tool inserts, turbine blades, biomedical implants.

**3. Organic Coatings (2 marks):**
Polymer, resin, or elastomer-based layers.
- **Examples:** Paints, lacquers, varnishes, enamels, epoxies, polyurethanes, PTFE.
- **Properties:** Excellent barrier, flexibility, chemical resistance, low cost.
- **Application:** Automotive bodies, marine structures, pipelines, household appliances.

**4. Conversion Coatings (1 mark):**
Formed *by chemical/electrochemical conversion of the substrate surface itself*.
- **Examples:** Anodizing (Al → $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$), Phosphating (steel → zinc/manganese phosphate), Chromating (Zn → chromate film).
- **Key Feature:** Coating is *integrally part of the substrate*, not a foreign layer.

---

**(b) Electroplating Process + Faraday's Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Electroplating Process Description (3 marks):**

Electroplating is an **electrochemical deposition process** in which a metal coating is deposited on a conductive substrate (cathode) by passing a direct current through an electrolyte solution containing ions of the coating metal.

**Setup:**
- **Cathode (–):** Substrate (steel component to be coated).
- **Anode (+):** Coating metal (Ni, Cr, Zn, etc.) — either soluble (consumable) or inert.
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous salt solution (e.g., $\text{NiSO}_4$ for Ni plating).
- **DC Source:** Rectifier providing stable voltage/current.

**Reactions:**
- At anode: $\text{Ni} \rightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (oxidation/dissolution)
- At cathode: $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni}$ (reduction/deposition)

**Parameters Controlled:** Current density (1–5 A/dm²), pH, temperature (45–60 °C), bath composition, agitation.

---

**Derivation of Faraday's Combined Law (2 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.1:

$$ m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

$$ T = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s} $$

---

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

Given: $I = 12 \, \text{A}$, $t = 1.5 \times 3600 = 5400 \, \text{s}$, $A_s = 250 \, \text{cm}^2$, $A = 58.71$, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.9 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, $F = 96500$.

**Mass of Nickel:**

$$ m = \frac{58.71 \times 12 \times 5400}{2 \times 96500} $$

$$ m = \frac{3804408}{193000} $$

$$ m = 19.71 \, \text{g} $$

**Thickness of Coating:**

$$ T = \frac{19.71}{8.9 \times 250} = \frac{19.71}{2225} $$

$$ T = 8.86 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{cm} = 88.6 \, \mu m $$

**Final Answers:** $m \approx 19.71 \, \text{g}$ and $T \approx 88.6 \, \mu m$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Module Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare **electroplating and electroless plating** in detail. Explain why electroless nickel is preferred for components with **complex geometry**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PVD and CVD processes** with their working principles. List any four differences and mention **two industrial applications** of each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B:

**(a) Electroplating vs. Electroless Plating (7 marks):**

Already covered in Section 3.3 comparison matrix. Key points to write:

**Electroplating (3 marks):**
- Uses **external DC power source**; substrate is cathode.
- Thickness depends on **current density distribution** — uneven on complex parts.
- **Edges, corners** get thicker coating due to high local current density.
- **Recesses, blind holes** get thinner coating.
- Requires **rectifier, anode, electrical connections**.

**Electroless Plating (2 marks):**
- Uses **autocatalytic chemical reduction** — no external current.
- Reaction: $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni}$ (electrons supplied by reducing agent like $\text{NaH}_2\text{PO}_2$).
- **Deposition is uniform** on **all exposed surfaces**, regardless of geometry.
- Ideal for complex parts like **valves, pipes, deep recesses**.

**Why Electroless for Complex Geometry (2 marks):**
- Uniformity is governed by **chemical kinetics**, not current distribution.
- **Same thickness** on flat surfaces, corners, and recesses.
- No "dog-boning" or "edge buildup" effects.
- **Hardness:** Electroless Ni-P can be hardened to 1000 HV by heat treatment.
- Example: **Hydraulic valves, gears, aerospace connectors, oil field equipment**.

---

**(b) PVD and CVD (7 marks):**

**PVD — Physical Vapor Deposition (3 marks):**

PVD is a vacuum-based process where a target material is **physically converted into vapor** and deposited onto the substrate surface.

**Main Variants:**
- **Evaporation:** Target is heated (electron beam or resistance) until it vaporizes.
- **Sputtering:** Inert gas ions (Ar⁺) bombard the target, knocking atoms off.
- **Ion Plating:** Hybrid — evaporation + ion bombardment for better adhesion.

**Working Principle:** Vapor atoms travel through vacuum (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa) and condense on the cooler substrate, forming a thin, adherent film (1–10 µm).

**CVD — Chemical Vapor Deposition (2 marks):**

CVD involves **chemical reaction of volatile precursor gases** on a heated substrate to form a solid coating.

**Working Principle:**
- Precursor gases (e.g., $\text{TiCl}_4$, $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{N}_2$) flow into a reaction chamber.
- At high substrate temperature (600–1100 °C), they react: $\text{TiCl}_4 + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{TiC} + 4\text{HCl}$.
- Solid TiC deposits on substrate; gaseous HCl is exhausted.

**Four Differences (1 mark) + Industrial Applications (1 mark):**

| PVD Applications | CVD Applications |
|---|---|
| TiN coating on drills & end mills | TiC/TiN on carbide cutting inserts |
| CrN on plastic molding dies | $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ on turbine blades |
| AlTiN on high-speed machining tools | DLC on automotive components |
| Decorative coatings (watch cases, faucet finishes) | Polycrystalline Si for semiconductors |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mistake in Thickness Calculation:** Forgetting to convert cm → µm. Always multiply the thickness in cm by $10^4$ to get µm. A wrong unit can cost **1–2 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Valency Confusion:** For Cr plating using $\text{CrO}_3$ bath, students often use $n = 3$ instead of **n = 6**. Always verify from the electrolyte's ionic state.
> 3. **Confusing PVD/CVD:** PVD is **physical**, lower temperature, line-of-sight; CVD is **chemical**, higher temperature, conformal. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Sacrificial vs. Barrier Mix-up:** Zn on steel = **sacrificial** (Zn corrodes); Cr on steel = **barrier** (Cr is noble). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Time Conversion:** Always convert minutes or hours into seconds before substituting in Faraday's equation. A common 1-mark loss in numericals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision Sheet — Surface Coating Types**

- ✅ Surface coatings are classified into **4 major categories**: Metallic, Non-Metallic Inorganic, Organic, and Conversion coatings.
- ✅ **Metallic coatings** offer **sacrificial protection** (Zn on steel) or **barrier protection** (Cr on steel).
- ✅ **Conversion coatings** are *chemically grown* from the substrate; all others are *physically/chemically deposited* on the substrate.
- ✅ **Electroplating** uses **external DC current**; **Electroless plating** uses **autocatalytic chemical reduction**.
- ✅ **Electroless plating** gives **uniform thickness** on complex geometries; electroplating does not.
- ✅ **PVD** = Physical, lower T, line-of-sight; **CVD** = Chemical, higher T, conformal.
- ✅ **Faraday's Combined Law:** $m = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — the **master equation** for all electroplating numericals.
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96485 \, \text{C/mol}$ (use **96500** in KTU answers).
- ✅ **Coating thickness formula:** $T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A_s}$ or directly $T = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s}$.
- ✅ **Anodizing** converts Al to $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}$; **Phosphating** forms zinc/manganese phosphate on steel.
- ✅ **Galvanic series** determines sacrificial behavior — coating metal must be **more anodic**.
- ✅ **Salt spray test (ASTM B117)** is the standard for corrosion resistance of coatings.
- ✅ **Cutting tool coatings:** TiN (general), AlTiN (high-speed), CrN (corrosion-resistant), DLC (low friction).
- ✅ **Thermal Barrier Coatings (TBC)** use **MCrAlY** bond coat + **YSZ** ($\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3$-stabilized $\text{ZrO}_2$) top coat for turbine blades.
- ✅ Common **valencies**: Cu²⁺ (n=2), Ni²⁺ (n=2), Cr⁶⁺ (n=6 from $\text{CrO}_3$), Zn²⁺ (n=2), Ag⁺ (n=1), Au³⁺ (n=3).
- ✅ **Organic coatings** are **barrier-only**, not sacrificial; they fail if breached.
- ✅ KTU examiners love: "*Differentiate PVD and CVD*" and "*Explain electroplating with Faraday's derivation*".
- ✅ Memorize **ASTM standards** — ASTM B571 (adhesion), ASTM B487 (thickness), ASTM B117 (corrosion).
- ✅ **Coating thickness range:** PVD/CVD (1–10 µm), Electroplating (5–50 µm), Hot dip (25–100 µm), Spray (50–500 µm).
- ✅ For **decorative + functional** dual-purpose coatings, **chromium** is the most common choice (automotive trim).
- ✅ **Electroless Ni-P** is **amorphous** → superior corrosion resistance; can be crystallized by heat treatment for high hardness (~1000 HV).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ceramic and Organic Methods of Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ceramic and Organic Methods of Coating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Surface Coating Technology

**Surface coating** is a fundamental branch of surface engineering wherein a layer of material is deposited onto the surface of a substrate (base material) to enhance its functional, decorative, or protective properties without altering the bulk characteristics of the parent material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Ceramic and Organic Methods of Coating refer to the deposition of inorganic ceramic compounds (oxides, nitrides, carbides) and organic polymeric films on engineering substrates to provide wear resistance, corrosion protection, thermal insulation, and aesthetic appeal.*

---

## 1.2 Classification of Coating Methods

The coating universe is broadly bifurcated into **ceramic (inorganic)** and **organic** families based on the chemistry of the deposited film:

| Category | Material Class | Operating Temperature | Typical Thickness |
|----------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Ceramic Coating** | Oxides, Nitrides, Carbides, Borides of metals/semi-metals | **400 °C – 12,000 °C** (process temp) | **1 µm – 500 µm** |
| **Organic Coating** | Polymers, Resins, Epoxies, Polyurethanes | **Room Temp – 250 °C** (curing temp) | **5 µm – 200 µm** |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🍕 The Pizza Coating Analogy
Think of an engineering component as a **plain pizza base (substrate)**. The toppings you add (cheese, sauce, vegetables) are the **coating materials**. 

- **Ceramic coatings** = **Hard, baked toppings like a crispy cheese crust**. They require *very high oven temperatures*, form an *extremely hard, chemically inert, and heat-resistant* layer. Just as a baked crust protects the base from getting soggy, ceramic coatings shield the metal from wear, heat, and corrosion.
- **Organic coatings** = **Cold toppings like fresh cream or chocolate sauce**. They are *applied at room temperature*, are *flexible, glossy, and decorative*, but offer less thermal protection. Like a paint layer on a wall, organic coatings primarily guard against *moisture, mild chemicals, and provide aesthetic finish*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Ceramic coatings are chosen when the component operates in **extreme environments** (turbine blades, cutting tools, rocket nozzles), whereas organic coatings are preferred for **everyday applications** (automotive bodies, home appliances, marine hulls, structural steel).

---

## 1.4 Why Coat? – The 5 Pillars of Functional Necessity

A component is coated to satisfy one or more of the following engineering demands:

1. **Wear Resistance** – Protection against abrasive, adhesive, and erosive wear (e.g., $\text{TiN}$, $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on cutting tools).
2. **Corrosion Resistance** – Barrier against chemical attack (e.g., zinc-rich primers on steel).
3. **Thermal Insulation / Barrier** – Reduction of heat transfer into substrate (e.g., $\text{ZrO}_2$ on turbine blades – Thermal Barrier Coatings, TBCs).
4. **Electrical Insulation / Conductivity** – Dielectric layers on copper or conductive tracks on insulators.
5. **Aesthetic & Decorative Finish** – Gloss, color, texture (e.g., automotive polyurethane topcoat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> Questions in OEMUT833 frequently ask: *"Differentiate between ceramic and organic coatings"* or *"Why ceramic coatings are preferred over organic coatings for high-temperature applications?"* – Always link your answer to the **bonding type**: ceramic = *ionic/covalent* (strong, brittle, refractory), organic = *van der Waals/covalent in chains* (weak, flexible, low-temp).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ceramic Coating Methods – Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Thermal Spray Coating

**Principle:** A ceramic (or metallic) feedstock (powder, wire, or rod) is heated to a *molten or semi-molten state* and accelerated by a gas stream toward a prepared substrate, where it flattens, cools rapidly, and adheres as a lamellar splat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamellar Splat Formation:** Molten droplets strike the substrate at velocities of **50 m/s – 1200 m/s**, spread into thin discs (5–15 µm thick), and stack like *fallen leaves* to build up the coating thickness. This anisotropic lamellar microstructure defines the mechanical behavior of all thermally sprayed coatings.

#### Sub-Classification of Thermal Spray (High-Yield for KTU)

| Technique | Heat Source | Flame Temperature | Particle Velocity | Bond Strength |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Flame Spray (FS)** | Oxy-acetylene | ~3,100 °C | ~50 m/s | Medium |
| **Arc Spray** | Electric Arc | ~6,000 °C | ~100 m/s | Medium-High |
| **Plasma Spray** | DC Plasma Arc | **12,000 – 16,000 °C** | **200 – 600 m/s** | High |
| **HVOF (High Velocity Oxy-Fuel)** | Combustion + Jet Kero/O₂/H₂ | ~3,000 °C | **600 – 1200 m/s** | **Very High** |
| **Detonation Gun Spray** | Detonation Wave (CH₄ + O₂) | ~4,500 °C | **800 – 1200 m/s** | **Highest** |

**Why HVOF & D-Gun dominate for cermets (WC-Co, Cr₃C₂-NiCr):**
The high kinetic energy of particles *deforms them plastically* on impact, ensuring excellent **mechanical interlocking** and **low porosity (< 1%)**, even without full melting. This preserves the carbide structure.

---

### 2.1.2 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

**Principle:** Volatile precursors (e.g., $\text{TiCl}_4$, $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{SiH}_4$) react chemically **in the gas phase or on a heated substrate** to form a non-volatile solid ceramic film, with by-products evacuated as gases.

**General Reaction Template:**
$$\text{Reactant gases (vapor)} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{Heat (800-1100°C)}} \text{Solid ceramic film} + \text{Volatile by-products}$$

**Example – TiC Coating on Cutting Tool:**
$$\text{TiCl}_4(g) + \text{CH}_4(g) \xrightarrow{\,950-1050\,°\text{C}\,} \text{TiC}(s) + 4\text{HCl}(g)$$

**Example – CVD of Diamond Film:**
$$\text{CH}_4(g) \xrightarrow{\,\text{H}_2 \, / \, \text{plasma} \,} \text{C (diamond)}(s) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$$

**Variants of CVD:**
- **APCVD** – Atmospheric Pressure CVD
- **LPCVD** – Low Pressure CVD (better uniformity)
- **PECVD** – Plasma Enhanced CVD (lower temperature, ~300 °C)
- **MOCVD** – Metal-Organic CVD (uses metallo-organic precursors)
- **HFCVD** – Hot Filament CVD (for diamond)

---

### 2.1.3 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)

**Principle:** The coating material is **physically vaporized** from a solid source (target) and transported through a low-pressure environment to condense on the substrate. **No chemical reaction** in the gas phase (in its purest form).

**Core Sub-Processes:**

#### (a) Vacuum Evaporation (Thermal Evaporation)
- Source material is **heated by resistance, electron beam, or induction** in a high vacuum ($\sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ torr).
- Atoms travel in straight lines (line-of-sight) and condense on the cooler substrate.

#### (b) Sputter Deposition
- A plasma (usually Ar⁺ ions) bombards the **target (cathode)**, ejecting atoms via momentum transfer.
- Ejected atoms deposit on the substrate.
- **DC Sputtering** for conductive targets; **RF Sputtering** for insulating ceramics like $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

#### (c) Ion Plating
- Hybrid of evaporation + sputtering.
- Substrate is **biased negatively**, attracting ions → enhanced adhesion, denser coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Spot:** PVD operates at **lower temperatures (200–500 °C)** than CVD, making it ideal for **tool steels and temperature-sensitive substrates**. PVD coatings are typically **2–5 µm** thick, while CVD can produce **5–25 µm**.

---

### 2.1.4 Sol-Gel Process

**Principle:** A colloidal suspension (**sol**) of ceramic precursors (metal alkoxides like $\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5\text{)}_4$ – TEOS) undergoes hydrolysis and polycondensation to form a **gel network**, which is then dried and heat-treated to yield a ceramic film.

**Stepwise Chemistry of $\text{SiO}_2$ Sol-Gel:**
$$\text{Si(OR)}_4 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\text{ROH} \quad \text{(Hydrolysis)}$$
$$\text{n Si(OH)}_4 \rightarrow (\text{Si-O-Si})_n + 2n\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Polycondensation)}$$

**Process Sequence:** Sol → Dip/Spin Coat → Gelation → Drying → Calcination → Ceramic Film.

---

### 2.1.5 Spray Pyrolysis

**Principle:** A solution (often metal salts like $\text{SnCl}_4$) is atomized into a spray and directed onto a **heated substrate**. The solvent evaporates and the solute undergoes **thermal decomposition** to form the desired ceramic oxide film.

$$\text{SnCl}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \xrightarrow{\,400-500\,°\text{C}\,} \text{SnO}_2(s) + 4\text{HCl}(g)$$

Common products: **FTO ($\text{SnO}_2$:F), ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** for solar cells and transparent electrodes.

---

## 2.2 Organic Coating Methods – Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Liquid Paint Coating (Conventional Spray Painting)

**Principle:** Pigmented liquid resin (alkyd, epoxy, polyurethane, acrylic) is atomized through a spray gun and deposited on the substrate. Subsequent **curing** (air-drying, stoving at 80–180 °C, or UV-curing) produces a continuous polymer film.

**Film Formation Mechanism:**
- **Solvent Evaporation** + **Oxidative Cross-linking** (for alkyds)
- **Polymerization** (for 2-pack epoxies: resin + hardener)
- **Chain Entanglement** (for thermoplastics like acrylic lacquers)

### 2.2.2 Powder Coating

**Principle:** A dry, free-flowing powder (typically epoxy, polyester, or hybrid) is applied electrostatically and then **cured at 160–200 °C** in an oven, where it melts, flows, and cross-links into a continuous film.

**Steps:**
1. **Electrostatic Spray** (gun charges powder to **–30 to –100 kV**, grounded part attracts powder).
2. **Fusion & Flow-out** in convection/IR oven.
3. **Cross-linking** to final thermoset film.

**Advantages:** Zero VOC emissions, thicker films (60–120 µm), excellent edge coverage.

### 2.2.3 Electrodeposition / Electrocoating (E-Coat)

**Principle:** The component is immersed in a **dilute water-borne paint bath** and acts as one electrode. Applying a DC voltage (50–400 V) drives charged paint particles to migrate and deposit on the part.

- **Anodic E-coat** – Part is **anode (+)**; common, automotive primers.
- **Cathodic E-coat** – Part is **cathode (–)**; superior corrosion resistance, dominant in automotive bodies.

**Film Thickness Control (KTU High-Yield Equation):**
$$\text{DFT} \propto \frac{V \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_0}{\kappa}$$
where $V$ = applied voltage, $\varepsilon$ = dielectric constant, $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space, $\kappa$ = paint bath conductivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's Law Equivalent:** Coating growth self-limits because deposited film is insulating → current drops → no further deposition beyond ~25 µm. This guarantees **uniform thickness even on complex shapes** – the hallmark advantage of E-coat.

### 2.2.4 Dip & Spin Coating

- **Dip Coating:** Substrate is withdrawn at controlled velocity from a liquid tank → thin film formed by gravity drainage & solvent evaporation.
- **Spin Coating:** Liquid dropped on a rapidly rotating substrate (1000–6000 rpm); centrifugal force spreads it to a uniform thin film. Dominant in **semiconductor photoresist deposition**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Splat Thickness (Thermal Spray)** | $t_s \approx \frac{D_p}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p V_p}{3\sigma}}$ | $D_p$: droplet dia, $\rho_p$: density, $V_p$: velocity, $\sigma$: surface tension |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate (PVD – Hertz-Knudsen)** | $\dot{m} = A \cdot p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{2\pi R T}}$ | kg/(m²·s); $p$: vapor pressure, $M$: molar mass, $R$: gas constant, $T$: source temperature (K) |
| 3 | **Arrhenius for CVD Growth Rate** | $r = r_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$: activation energy (kJ/mol) |
| 4 | **Sticking Coefficient (PVD/CVD)** | $S_c = \frac{\text{atoms incorporated}}{\text{atoms arriving}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le S_c \le 1$ |
| 5 | **Sol-Gel Hydrolysis Ratio** | $h = \frac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{M(OR)}_n]}$ | $h < 1$: linear polymer, $h > 1$: branched/cluster |
| 6 | **E-Coat Film Thickness (Empirical)** | $d = \frac{A \cdot V \cdot t}{\kappa \cdot A_{\text{part}}}$ | $V$: voltage, $t$: time, $A_{\text{part}}$: coated area |
| 7 | **Spin Coating Film Thickness** | $t_f = \frac{k \cdot \eta^{1/3}}{\omega^{1/2}}$ | $\eta$: viscosity, $\omega$: angular velocity (rpm), $k$: constant |
| 8 | **Porosity of Thermal Spray** | $P_\% = \frac{\rho_{\text{theoretical}} - \rho_{\text{measured}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | HVOF: $< 1\%$, Plasma: $2\text{–}5\%$, Flame: $5\text{–}15\%$ |
| 9 | **Bond Strength (ASTM C633)** | $\sigma_b = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{peg}}}$ | MPa; minimum acceptable for engineering: **> 35 MPa** |
| 10 | **Hardness Equivalence (Vickers → Approx Rockwell)** | $\text{HRC} \approx \frac{\text{HV} - 20}{10}$ | Rough conversion for tool coatings |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Map

| Industry Sector | Preferred Ceramic Coating | Preferred Organic Coating |
|----------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Aerospace (Turbines)** | $\text{ZrO}_2$ TBC, MCrAlY bond coat | Polyurethane topcoat on airframe |
| **Automotive Engine** | DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon) on piston rings | E-coat (cathodic) primer + acrylic topcoat |
| **Cutting Tools** | $\text{TiN}, \text{TiAlN}, \text{CrN}$ (PVD) | None |
| **Marine** | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$/Zn composite (thermal spray) | Epoxy + polyurethane anti-fouling |
| **Electronics** | $\text{SiO}_2$, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ (CVD/PECVD) | Photoresist, parylene (medical) |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Hydroxyapatite (HA) | Parylene C, PMMA bone cement |
| **Architectural** | Anodized $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | PVDF, polyester powder coat |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hertz-Knudsen Equation for PVD Evaporation Rate

The **Hertz-Knudsen equation** is the foundational model for vapor flux from a liquid/solid source into a vacuum. KTU frequently tests the *molecular-kinetic theory* behind it.

### Step 1 – Assumption of Ideal Gas at Source Surface
At equilibrium, vapor just above the source surface behaves as an ideal gas with **Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution**.

The mean molecular velocity normal to the surface:
$$\bar{v}_n = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

### Step 2 – Mass Flux Definition
Mass flux (kg per m² per second) from the surface is:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \bar{v}_n$$

Using ideal gas law: $\rho = \frac{p \cdot M}{RT}$, therefore:
$$\dot{m} = \frac{p M}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

### Step 3 – Simplify the Expression
$$\dot{m} = p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{RT}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}} = p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8M}{\pi RT}}$$

Regrouping with the universal gas constant $R$:
$$\boxed{\dot{m} = A \cdot p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{2\pi RT}}}$$

The **Hertz-Knudsen equation** is the mass evaporation rate per unit area, with $A$ being a sticking/condensation coefficient ($\le 1$) and $p$ being the equilibrium vapor pressure at temperature $T$.

### Step 4 – Engineering Interpretation
- For **high deposition rate** → need **high vapor pressure** → requires **high source temperature**.
- For **low atomic mass $M$** elements → vapor flux is higher.
- This explains why **Al (M = 27)** evaporates easily, but **W (M = 184)** needs e-beam evaporation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sol-Gel Hydrolysis & Polycondensation Stoichiometry

Consider the formation of $\text{SiO}_2$ film from TEOS (Tetraethyl Orthosilicate).

### Step 1 – Hydrolysis Step
One mole of TEOS reacts with water:
$$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_3(\text{OH}) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

For **complete hydrolysis**, all four –OR groups are replaced:
$$\text{Si(OR)}_4 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\text{ROH}$$

### Step 2 – Polycondensation Step
Two silanol groups combine:
$$\equiv\text{Si-OH} + \text{HO-Si}\equiv \rightarrow \equiv\text{Si-O-Si}\equiv + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Or alcohol elimination:
$$\equiv\text{Si-OH} + \text{RO-Si}\equiv \rightarrow \equiv\text{Si-O-Si}\equiv + \text{ROH}$$

For $n$ monomers:
$$n\,\text{Si(OH)}_4 \rightarrow (\text{SiO}_2)_n + 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 3 – Net Mass Balance
Overall: $\text{Si(OR)}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\text{ROH}$

So **1 mole TEOS + 2 moles water → 1 mole $\text{SiO}_2$ + 4 moles ethanol**.

### Step 4 – Stoichiometric Water Ratio
$$h = \frac{n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{n_{\text{TEOS}}} = 2 \text{ (stoichiometric)}$$

- $h \ll 2$: hydrolysis slow → **linear polymer chain** → brittle film.
- $h \gg 2$: rapid hydrolysis → **branched clusters** → colloidal gel.
- $h = 2$ + acid catalyst: **dense, monolithic** glass-like film.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of CVD Growth Rate

### Step 1 – Reaction Rate Statement
For a surface-reaction-limited CVD process, the deposition rate $r$ (µm/min) follows Arrhenius kinetics:
$$r = r_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $r_0$ = pre-exponential factor (depends on precursor concentration)
- $E_a$ = apparent activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = substrate temperature (K)

### Step 2 – Linearize for Experimental Determination
Taking natural log:
$$\ln(r) = \ln(r_0) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Plotting $\ln(r)$ vs $\frac{1}{T}$ gives a straight line with:
- **Slope** = $-\frac{E_a}{R}$ → enables $E_a$ determination.
- **Intercept** = $\ln(r_0)$.

### Step 3 – Numerical Example (TiC CVD)
Given: Growth rate at $1000\,°\text{C} = 0.5\,\mu\text{m/min}$, at $950\,°\text{C} = 0.2\,\mu\text{m/min}$.

Using the ratio:
$$\frac{r_2}{r_1} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

$$2.5 = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1223} - \frac{1}{1273}\right)\right]$$

Compute the reciprocal difference:
$$\frac{1}{1223} - \frac{1}{1273} = \frac{1273 - 1223}{1223 \times 1273} = \frac{50}{1{,}556{,}879} = 3.21 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

Therefore:
$$\ln(2.5) = \frac{E_a}{8.314} \cdot 3.21 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$0.916 = E_a \cdot 3.86 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\boxed{E_a \approx 237\,\text{kJ/mol}}$$

This high activation energy confirms a **surface-reaction-limited regime**, typical for halide-based CVD of TiC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Spin-Coating Film Thickness (Meyerhofer Model)

### Step 1 – Stages of Spin Coating
1. **Dispense:** Liquid deposited on substrate.
2. **Spin-up:** Centrifugal spreading (thickness drops sharply).
3. **Spin-off:** Balance of centrifugal vs. viscous forces.
4. **Evaporation:** Solvent loss dominates → thickness stabilizes.

### Step 2 – Spin-off Stage (Balance of Forces)
Inertial force per unit volume = $\rho \, \omega^2 \, r$.
Viscous force per unit volume = $\eta \, \frac{\partial^2 v_r}{\partial z^2}$.
Equating (lubrication approximation):
$$\rho \omega^2 r \sim \eta \frac{V}{h^2}$$

Solving for average radial velocity and integrating, the film thickness evolves as:
$$h(t) = \frac{h_0}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{4\rho\omega^2 h_0^2}{3\eta}\,t}}$$

### Step 3 – Final Thickness (Empirical)
After evaporation completes (time $t_f$):
$$\boxed{t_f = C \cdot \eta^{1/3} \cdot \omega^{-1/2}}$$

Where $C$ depends on solvent evaporation rate and solids content. **Key insight:** Higher spin speed → thinner film; higher viscosity → thicker film.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Ceramic vs. Organic)

| Parameter | Ceramic Coating | Organic Coating |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Bonding** | Ionic / Covalent (strong) | Covalent chains + van der Waals |
| **Hardness** | **1,000 – 4,000 HV** | **2 – 10 HV (pencil 2H–6H)** |
| **Max Service Temperature** | **600 – 1,200 °C** | **150 – 250 °C** |
| **Deposition Temperature** | 200 – 1,100 °C | RT – 200 °C |
| **Thickness Range** | 1 – 500 µm | 5 – 200 µm |
| **Porosity** | 0 – 15 % | Pinhole-free |
| **Flexibility** | Brittle | Flexible (impact > 60 cm @ 1 kg) |
| **Cost (per m²)** | High (₹₹₹₹) | Low (₹₹) |
| **Repairability** | Difficult | Easy (recoat) |
| **Typical Application** | Aero, tooling, dies | Automotive, appliances |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Generic Ceramic Coating Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate Selection<br>Steel WC Inconel Ti] --> B[Surface Preparation<br>Grit Blasting Degreasing]
    B --> C[Masking<br>Plugging Fixtures]
    C --> D{Choose Coating Route}
    D -->|Thermal| E[Thermal Spray<br>Plasma HVOF Arc]
    D -->|Vapor Phase| F[CVD Reactor<br>850-1050 C]
    D -->|Vapor Phase| G[PVD Chamber<br>Vacuum 10 to 5 torr]
    D -->|Wet Chemical| H[Sol Gel Process]
    D -->|Spray Pyrolysis| I[Spray Pyrolysis Unit]
    E --> J[Coating Buildup<br>50 to 500 um]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Post Processing<br>Sealing Polishing Heat Treat]
    K --> L[Quality Inspection<br>Adhesion Porosity Thickness Hardness]
    L --> M[Finished Component]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: CVD Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GasPanel[Gas Panel]
        G1[Precursor 1<br>TiCl4]
        G2[Precursor 2<br>CH4 NH3]
        G3[Carrier Gas<br>Ar N2 H2]
    end
    GasPanel --> MFC[Mass Flow Controllers<br>0 to 500 sccm]
    MFC --> Mixer[Gas Mixer<br>and Preheater]
    Mixer --> Reactor[Hot Wall CVD Reactor<br>850 to 1050 C]
    Reactor --> Substrate[Substrate Holder<br>Induction Heated]
    Substrate --> Film[Ceramic Film Growth<br>5 to 25 um]
    Film --> Exhaust[Exhaust Scrubber<br>HCl H2 Removal]
    subgraph Control[Control Unit]
        TC1[Reactor TC]
        TC2[Substrate TC]
        PR[Pressure Gauge<br>10 to 760 torr]
    end
    TC1 -.-> Reactor
    TC2 -.-> Substrate
    PR -.-> Reactor
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: PVD Sputtering System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Vacuum[Pump Stack<br>Rotary plus Diffusion<br>10 to 6 torr] --> Chamber[Vacuum Chamber<br>Stainless Steel 304]
    Ar[Ar Gas Inlet<br>99.999 percent] --> Chamber
    Target[Target Cathode<br>Ti Al Cr] --> Chamber
    Sub[Pulse DC or RF<br>Bias 300 to 800 W] --> Target
    Substrate[Substrate Holder<br>Rotating Heated] --> Chamber
    Bias[Substrate Bias<br>minus 50 to minus 200 V] --> Substrate
    Plasma[Glow Discharge Plasma<br>Ar plus ions] --> Target
    Plasma --> Sputter[Atom Ejection via<br>Momentum Transfer]
    Sputter --> Depo[Film Deposition<br>2 to 5 um]
    Depo --> Coat[Hard Ceramic Coating<br>TiN TiAlN CrN]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Organic Coating Line (Automotive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Body in White<br>Steel Stamped] --> B[Pre Treatment<br>Phosphating Rinse]
    B --> C[Cathodic E Coat<br>280 V 2 min 30 C]
    C --> D[UF Rinse 1]
    D --> E[UF Rinse 2]
    E --> F[Sealer PVC Application<br>Anti Chip]
    F --> G[Primer Surfacer<br>30 to 40 um]
    G --> H[Basecoat<br>Color 15 to 25 um]
    H --> I[Clearcoat<br>35 to 45 um]
    I --> J[Oven Cure<br>140 to 160 C 20 min]
    J --> K[Polishing Inspection]
    K --> L[Final Vehicle]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Selection of Coating Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Coating Requirement] --> Q1{Service Temperature?}
    Q1 -->|>500 C| Ceramic[Go to Ceramic Route]
    Q1 -->|<250 C| Q2{Thickness Required?}
    Q2 -->|>50 um| Powder[Powder Coating]
    Q2 -->|<50 um| Q3{Substrate Shape}
    Q3 -->|Complex Hollow| ECoat[Electrocoating]
    Q3 -->|Flat Small| Q4{Volume Production?}
    Q4 -->|High| Spray[Automatic Spray]
    Q4 -->|Low| Manual[Manual Spray]
    Ceramic --> Q5{Load Bearing Wear?}
    Q5 -->|Yes Tribology| PVD[PVD TiN DLC]
    Q5 -->|Yes Thermal| TBC[Plasma ZrO2 TBC]
    Q5 -->|Corrosion High Temp| SprayC[HVOF or Plasma]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between PVD and CVD techniques of ceramic coating with respect to process temperature, deposition mechanism, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | PVD | CVD |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Mechanism** | Physical vaporization (no chemical reaction) | Chemical reaction of precursors |
| **Process Temp.** | **200 – 500 °C** (low) | **800 – 1100 °C** (high) |
| **Film Thickness** | 2 – 5 µm | 5 – 25 µm |
| **Adhesion** | Good (line-of-sight) | Excellent (conformal) |
| **Coating Uniformity** | Line-of-sight only | Conformal (covers complex shapes) |
| **Applications** | Cutting tools (TiN), decorative | Semiconductor SiO₂, tool inserts |

> Marks distribution: 1 mark for mechanism, 1 for temperature contrast, 1 for application contrast.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What are the main constituents of a typical organic coating formulation? Explain the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A liquid organic coating formulation contains:

1. **Resin/Binder (30 – 60 %)** – Polyurethane, epoxy, alkyd; forms the continuous film, provides adhesion, flexibility, and chemical resistance.
2. **Pigments (10 – 30 %)** – $\text{TiO}_2$, carbon black, iron oxides; provide color, opacity, UV protection, corrosion inhibition.
3. **Solvents (20 – 50 %)** – Xylene, toluene, MEK; reduce viscosity for application; evaporate during curing.
4. **Additives (1 – 5 %)** – Surfactants, flow agents, anti-skinning agents, biocides; modify rheology, drying, and durability.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Thermal Spray & CVD Ceramic Coatings `[14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Plasma Spray process** for ceramic coating in detail with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of primary and secondary gas, and explain why ceramic coatings can be deposited on metallic substrates without melting the substrate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable chemical reaction, describe the **CVD process for TiC coating on cemented carbide inserts**. List four advantages and two limitations of CVD over PVD.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Plasma Spray Process (7 Marks):**

1. **Plasma Generation:** A high-frequency arc (~250 V, ~500–800 A DC) is struck between a **thoriated tungsten cathode** and a **water-cooled copper anode (nozzle)**. Argon (primary gas) is ionized to form a high-enthalpy plasma jet. Hydrogen or helium (secondary gas) is added to **raise the enthalpy and thermal conductivity** of the plasma.

2. **Plasma Jet Characteristics:**
$$T_{\text{plasma}} = 12{,}000 \text{ to } 16{,}000 \, °\text{C}$$
$$V_{\text{jet}} = 1{,}000 \text{ to } 4{,}000 \, \text{m/s}$$

3. **Powder Injection:** Ceramic powder (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{ZrO}_2$, 10–90 µm size) is injected into the plasma plume via an **Ar carrier gas** through a port either **internal** (into the nozzle) or **external** (downstream). Particles melt in milliseconds and are accelerated toward the substrate.

4. **Splat Formation:** Molten droplets impact the **grit-blasted substrate** (Ra ≈ 5–10 µm) at velocities of 200–600 m/s. They flatten into **lamellar splats (5–15 µm thick)** and freeze within microseconds, building up the coating layer-by-layer (50–500 µm).

5. **Why Substrate Does Not Melt:** The exposure time of any substrate point to the plasma is short (each pass < 10 ms), and the **heat-affected zone is shallow** (< 50 µm). Active cooling jets (air or CO₂) keep substrate bulk temperature **below 200 °C**.

**Block Schematic:**
```
[Powder Feeder] → [Ar Carrier] → [Internal Injection Port]
                                       ↓
[Power Supply 500 A DC] → [Arc] → [Nozzle Anode] → [Plasma Jet 12,000°C]
                                                          ↓
[Substrate on Manipulator] ← [Molten Sprays Impact] ← [Flatten into Splats]
                                       ↓
                              [Coating Layer Growth]
```

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**CVD of TiC on Cemented Carbide (7 Marks):**

**Reaction:**
$$\text{TiCl}_4(g) + \text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2(g) \xrightarrow{\,950-1050\,°\text{C}\,} \text{TiC}(s) + 4\text{HCl}(g) + \text{H}_2(g)_{\text{residual}}$$

**Process Steps:**
1. **Substrate Preparation:** Cemented carbide (WC-Co) inserts cleaned, degreased, and seeded (optional) with nucleation sites.
2. **Reactor Loading:** Parts placed in a **hot-wall CVD reactor** (Inconel retort).
3. **Vacuum & Heating:** Chamber pumped to ~50 torr, heated to **950–1050 °C**.
4. **Gas Flow:** $\text{TiCl}_4$ (bubbler with H₂ carrier), $\text{CH}_4$, and excess $\text{H}_2$ introduced at controlled mass flow rates (MFC-controlled).
5. **Reaction:** Gases diffuse to substrate, adsorb, react heterogeneously → TiC nucleates and grows.
6. **Cooldown:** Slow cool in inert atmosphere to avoid thermal shock.

**Advantages of CVD over PVD (any 4):**
1. **Conformal coverage** – uniform on complex 3-D geometries (non-line-of-sight).
2. **Higher deposition rates** (typically 5–25 µm/h vs. 1–3 µm/h for PVD).
3. **Better adhesion** due to metallurgical bonding at high temperature.
4. **Thicker coatings** possible (up to 25 µm) without residual stress cracking.
5. **Better throwing power** in holes, recesses, and internal surfaces.

**Limitations (any 2):**
1. **Very high process temperature (900–1100 °C)** – unsuitable for hardened tool steels (> HRC 60 risk of softening).
2. **Toxic and corrosive by-products** (HCl, Cl₂) require scrubbing.
3. **Grain coarsening** of substrate at CVD temperatures reduces mechanical properties.

---

### Question B: Organic Coatings & Surface Preparation `[14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **cathodic electrodeposition (e-coat) process** with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages and limitations, and explain why it is the dominant primer process in automotive industry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **sol-gel ceramic coating**? Describe the step-by-step sol-gel process for depositing a silica ($\text{SiO}_2$) film on a metallic substrate. List four advantages of sol-gel over CVD.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Cathodic E-Coat (7 Marks):**

**Principle:** The component (automotive body) is dipped in a water-borne paint bath (8–12 % solids) and connected as the **cathode (–)**. The **stainless steel anode (+)** is placed in the tank walls or immersed tube electrodes. Applying a DC voltage of **250–400 V** causes positively charged epoxy-amine resin micelles to migrate and deposit on the cathodic body.

**Key Reactions at Cathode:**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2(g) + 2\text{OH}^-$$
$$\text{R-NH}_3^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Resin coagulation)}$$

The deposited film is **insoluble** (pH rises near cathode due to OH⁻ generation), so further deposition self-limits at ~20–30 µm.

**Block Schematic:**
```
DC Rectifier 280 V → Cathode Lead → [Automotive Body Immersed]
DC Rectifier Anode → Stainless Anode Plates → [Paint Tank]
Paint Recirculation + Filtration + Cooling ← [Ultra Filter]
UF Permeate → Rinse Tanks → Reuse
```

**Advantages:**
1. **100 % coverage** of cavities, box-sections, sharp edges.
2. **Uniform film thickness** regardless of part geometry.
3. **Excellent corrosion resistance** due to amine-cured epoxy chemistry.
4. **Environment-friendly** – water-based, low VOC.
5. **High throughput** – entire body coated in ~2 min.

**Limitations:**
1. High capital cost (tank, rectifier, UF system).
2. Color restriction (only dark shades feasible as primer).
3. Sensitivity to bath chemistry (pH, conductivity, MEQ).

**Why Dominant in Automotive:**
- Eliminates weld rust in box-sections (critical for 10-year anti-corrosion warranty).
- Single-coat uniform primer on the entire body shell including hidden areas.
- Compatible with subsequent powder primer surfacer and topcoat systems.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Sol-Gel Process (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** A wet-chemical process in which a **colloidal suspension (sol)** of ceramic precursors is converted into a **continuous gel network**, then dried and calcined to form a dense ceramic film.

**Step-by-Step Process for $\text{SiO}_2$ on Steel:**

1. **Sol Preparation:** Mix TEOS ($\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4$) with ethanol, water, and a few drops of HCl or $\text{HNO}_3$ as catalyst. Typical molar ratio TEOS:EtOH:H₂O = 1:4:4. Stir for 1 hour at **60 °C** → clear sol.

2. **Hydrolysis:** $\text{Si(OR)}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\text{ROH}$ (acid catalysis preferred for linear chains).

3. **Polycondensation:** Silanols condense → **Si–O–Si** bridges → oligomeric network.

4. **Dip Coating:** Pre-cleaned steel substrate (acetone + ethanol wash) is withdrawn from the sol at a controlled speed of **5–25 cm/min**. A thin liquid film clings to the surface.

5. **Drying:** Gel film aged at room temperature for 1 hour, then at **100 °C for 30 min** to remove residual solvent and water.

6. **Calcination / Heat Treatment:** Heated to **400–600 °C** for 1 hour in air → organic groups burn out, **Si–O–Si network densifies** into glassy $\text{SiO}_2$ film.

7. **Resulting Film:** **0.1 – 1 µm thick**, amorphous, dense, adherent.

**Advantages of Sol-Gel over CVD (any 4):**
1. **Much lower processing temperature** (400–600 °C vs. 800–1100 °C).
2. **Simple equipment** – beaker, dip-coater, oven. No vacuum or reactor.
3. **Excellent compositional control** at molecular level.
4. **Large-area and complex-shape coating** easily possible (non-line-of-sight).
5. **Low cost** for thin films and small batch production.
6. **No toxic precursor gases** (compared to $\text{TiCl}_4$, $\text{SiH}_4$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse sol-gel with spray pyrolysis** – sol-gel is a *wet-chemical dip/spin coat*, spray pyrolysis uses *aerosol decomposition on hot substrate*.
> 2. **CVD substrate temperature is HIGH** (~1000 °C) – do not write "low temperature" – that is **PVD**.
> 3. **Cathodic vs. Anodic E-coat** – the part is **cathode (–)** in cathodic e-coat, **anode (+)** in anodic. Examiners deduct marks if polarity is reversed.
> 4. **HVOF vs. Plasma Spray** – HVOF has *cooler flame but higher particle velocity*; plasma has *higher temperature but lower velocity*. Many students invert this.
> 5. **Units!** Always write temperature in **°C** and thickness in **µm** (not mm) in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullet Summary):**

- **Ceramic Coatings** = inorganic (oxides/nitrides/carbides), high-temperature processing, hard, refractory, ionic/covalent bonding.
- **Organic Coatings** = polymer/resin-based, room/low-temp cure, flexible, decorative + corrosion protection, van der Waals/covalent chain bonding.
- **Thermal Spray** family: **Flame < Arc < Plasma < HVOF < Detonation Gun** in terms of particle velocity and bond strength.
- **Plasma spray temperature**: 12,000–16,000 °C, but substrate stays < 200 °C due to active cooling.
- **CVD** uses **chemical reaction** in vapor phase; **PVD** uses **physical vaporization** – no chemical reaction.
- **CVD temp (800–1100 °C) >> PVD temp (200–500 °C)**; CVD is conformal, PVD is line-of-sight.
- **TiC CVD reaction:** $\text{TiCl}_4 + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{TiC} + 4\text{HCl}$ at ~1000 °C.
- **Hertz-Knudsen equation:** $\dot{m} = A p \sqrt{M/(2\pi RT)}$ governs PVD evaporation rate.
- **Sol-gel net reaction:** $\text{Si(OR)}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\text{ROH}$.
- **Cathodic e-coat** uses negatively biased part, water-borne epoxy-amine bath, 250–400 V DC, self-limiting film ~25 µm, dominant in automotive primer.
- **Powder coating** uses electrostatic spray at **–30 to –100 kV**, cured at 160–200 °C, zero VOC, 60–120 µm thick.
- **Spin coating** thickness $t_f \propto \eta^{1/3}/\omega^{1/2}$ – higher rpm → thinner film.
- **Hardness**: ceramic coatings >1,000 HV; organic coatings 2–10 HV (pencil hardness 2H–6H).
- **Service temperature**: ceramic up to 1,200 °C; organic limited to 150–250 °C.
- **Always mention process temperature, film thickness range, and typical application** when describing any coating technique – this is the KTU board examiner's mantra for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economics of Coating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Economics of Coating** is a branch of **industrial engineering and manufacturing economics** that quantitatively evaluates the total cost of ownership, profitability, and feasibility of applying protective, decorative, or functional surface coatings to engineering components. It encompasses the systematic analysis of **capital expenditure (CapEx)**, **operational expenditure (OpEx)**, **depreciation, labor wages, energy consumption, consumables, maintenance, and overheads** associated with coating processes such as electroplating, anodizing, powder coating, PVD/CVD, thermal spraying, and painting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Economics of coating refers to the financial assessment of coating operations, balancing the cost of installation, operation, and maintenance against the value added to the product through improved surface properties (corrosion resistance, wear resistance, aesthetic appeal, etc.).*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Plain English)

Imagine you are deciding whether to get your car's body painted with a cheap local paint job for ₹5,000 or a premium ceramic coating for ₹25,000.

- The **cheap paint** wears off in 1 year, needs redoing, and may allow rust to form.
- The **premium coating** lasts 5 years, protects against rust, and increases resale value.

**Economics of coating is exactly this decision-making** — but applied to industrial parts, where costs run into crores and the consequences of poor coating (machine failure, product rejection, warranty claims) can be catastrophic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU context:** Every KTU board exam question on this topic tests whether you can calculate **cost per coated piece**, **payback period**, or **break-even point** for a coating plant. This is a highly numerical, problem-oriented module.

## 1.3 Core Cost Components in Coating Economics

| Cost Type | Definition | Example (Electroplating) |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Costs independent of production volume | Equipment, building, installation |
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Costs proportional to production | Chemicals, electricity, labor per piece |
| **Direct Cost** | Attributable to specific job | Anode material, electrolyte |
| **Indirect Cost (Overhead)** | Shared across operations | Factory rent, supervisor salary |
| **Capital Recovery Cost** | Depreciation + interest on investment | Annualized equipment cost |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** Coating thickness is universally measured in **micrometers (μm)** and cost is normalized to **cost per square meter (₹/m²)** of coated area.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Coating Economics

- **Coating cost per unit area:** $\text{Cost/m}^2$ in ₹/m²
- **Depreciation rate:** usually **straight-line method** for KTU problems
- **Payback period:** in years
- **Return on Investment (ROI):** in %
- **Break-even production volume:** pieces/year
- **Cost per piece (Cp):** total cost ÷ number of pieces coated per year

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Coating Economics

The economic analysis of a coating system follows a **systematic five-step framework** that KTU examiners love to test:

### Step 1: Capital Investment Estimation
- Initial cost of coating equipment (electroplating tank, spray gun, vacuum chamber, etc.)
- Cost of building, ventilation, effluent treatment plant (ETP)
- Cost of jigs, fixtures, and tooling
- Working capital (raw materials, spares, salary advance)

### Step 2: Operating Cost Calculation
The annual operating cost includes:
- **Direct labor** (operators, helpers)
- **Electricity** (rectifiers, heaters, blowers)
- **Consumables** (chemicals, gases, anodes, powder)
- **Maintenance** (5–10% of capital cost per year)
- **Overheads** (rent, insurance, supervision)

### Step 3: Depreciation Calculation
Depreciation accounts for the **wear, tear, and obsolescence** of equipment. KTU standardly uses:

**Straight Line Method (SLM):**
$$D = \frac{C - S}{n}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Initial capital cost (₹)
- $S$ = Salvage value (₹)
- $n$ = Useful life in years
- $D$ = Annual depreciation (₹/year)

### Step 4: Cost Per Piece Calculation
$$\text{Cost per piece (Cp)} = \frac{\text{Annual Fixed Cost} + \text{Annual Variable Cost}}{\text{Annual Production (pieces)}}$$

### Step 5: Profitability Analysis
- **Payback Period** = Capital Investment ÷ Annual Net Cash Inflow
- **ROI** = (Net Profit ÷ Capital Investment) × 100
- **Break-even Volume** = Fixed Cost ÷ (Selling Price − Variable Cost per piece)

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Rationale |
|------|----------------------|
| Capital estimation | Determines loan requirement and insurance base |
| Operating cost | Recurring expense that decides competitive pricing |
| Depreciation | Tax shield + replacement planning |
| Cost per piece | Benchmarking across coating vendors |
| Profitability | Go/No-go decision for new coating line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Industries like **automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors), aerospace (HAL), shipbuilding (Cochin Shipyard), and electronics (Samsung, Foxconn)** use these exact models to decide between in-house coating vs outsourcing. For instance, anodizing of iPhone aluminum shells requires cost calculations to the nearest ₹/piece for millions of units.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $D$=depreciation, $C$=cost, $S$=salvage, $n$=life | ₹/year | Straight-line depreciation |
| 2 | $Cp = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{N}$ | $C_{annual}$=annual cost, $N$=pieces/year | ₹/piece | Cost per coated piece |
| 3 | $\text{Cost/m}^2 = \dfrac{Cp}{A}$ | $A$=surface area of one piece | ₹/m² | Normalized cost |
| 4 | $A = \pi d L$ | $d$=diameter, $L$=length | m² | Cylindrical surface area |
| 5 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{I}{R}$ | $I$=investment, $R$=annual return | years | Investment recovery time |
| 6 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{NP}{I} \times 100$ | $NP$=net profit | % | Profitability ratio |
| 7 | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - V}$ | $FC$=fixed cost, $P$=price, $V$=variable cost | units | Break-even quantity |
| 8 | $E = P \times t \times C_e$ | $P$=power, $t$=time, $C_e$=electricity rate | ₹ | Energy cost |
| 9 | $I_{eff} = I + I \times r \times t$ | $r$=interest rate, $t$=time | ₹ | Effective investment with interest |
| 10 | $N = \dfrac{H \times 60}{T_c}$ | $H$=working hours, $T_c$=cycle time | pieces/year | Annual production capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Most 14-mark questions on Economics of Coating require you to compute **depreciation + annual cost + cost per piece + payback period** in a single structured answer. Memorize the 10 formulas above as a single integrated calculation pipeline.

## 2.4 Important Boundary Conditions (KTU Examiner Favorites)

- **Working hours assumed:** 8 hrs/day × 300 days = **2400 hrs/year** (unless stated otherwise)
- **Working days assumed:** 25 days/month × 12 = **300 days/year**
- **Cycle time ($T_c$):** Total time per piece = loading + coating + drying + unloading
- **Machine utilization:** Never 100%; use 80% as default in absence of data
- **Inflation/interest:** KTU problems assume **constant rupee value** unless otherwise stated

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Numerical Problem: Electroplating Plant Economics

> **Worked Example (Module 1, High-Yield):**
> *An electroplating unit coats cylindrical shafts of diameter 50 mm and length 300 mm. The plant operates 8 hours/day, 25 days/month. Cycle time is 6 minutes per piece. Capital cost is ₹15,00,000, salvage value ₹1,50,000, life 10 years. Annual fixed overheads = ₹2,00,000. Variable cost per piece = ₹12. Selling price = ₹40. Electricity = ₹4 per kWh, rectifier draws 5000 W at 50% duty cycle. Calculate: (a) Annual production, (b) Annual depreciation, (c) Total annual cost, (d) Cost per piece, (e) Payback period if net annual profit is ₹3,00,000.*

### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Surface area of one cylindrical shaft**

$$A = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.3 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = 0.0471 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (approx)}$$

> *Logic:* Diameter converted from 50 mm to 0.05 m and length 300 mm to 0.3 m because SI units of area are m², which is the industry standard for coating cost normalization.

**Step 2: Annual Production Capacity**

Working hours per year = $8 \times 25 \times 12 = 2400$ hours

Cycle time $T_c$ = 6 minutes = 0.1 hours

Pieces per hour = $1 / 0.1 = 10$ pieces/hour

$$N = 2400 \times 10 = 24{,}000 \text{ pieces/year}$$

> *Logic:* The cycle time includes loading, plating, rinsing, drying, and unloading. Multiplying pieces/hour by total hours gives annual throughput.

**Step 3: Annual Depreciation (SLM)**

$$D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{10}$$

$$D = \frac{13{,}50{,}000}{10} = 1{,}35{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

> *Logic:* The capital cost of ₹15 lakh is reduced by the salvage value ₹1.5 lakh, then divided equally over the 10-year life. This is the standard **Straight Line Method** required by KTU.

**Step 4: Annual Variable Cost**

$$VC_{annual} = N \times V_c = 24{,}000 \times 12 = 2{,}88{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

> *Logic:* Variable cost scales linearly with production. 12 ₹/piece includes chemicals, consumable anodes, and direct labor per piece.

**Step 5: Annual Electricity Cost**

Rectifier runs at 50% duty cycle → effective power = $5000 \times 0.5 = 2500$ W = 2.5 kW

Annual energy = $2.5 \text{ kW} \times 2400 \text{ h} = 6000$ kWh

$$E_{cost} = 6000 \times 4 = 24{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

> *Logic:* Duty cycle < 100% because the rectifier is not loaded continuously — plating and idle periods alternate.

**Step 6: Total Annual Cost**

$$TC = D + \text{Overheads} + VC_{annual} + E_{cost}$$

$$TC = 1{,}35{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}000 + 24{,}000$$

$$TC = 6{,}47{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

> *Logic:* Sum of all annual cash outflows. Depreciation is included as a non-cash but book-keeping expense.

**Step 7: Cost per Piece**

$$C_p = \frac{TC}{N} = \frac{6{,}47{,}000}{24{,}000}$$

$$C_p \approx 26.96 \text{ ₹/piece}$$

**Cost per m²:**

$$C_{m^2} = \frac{C_p}{A} = \frac{26.96}{0.0471} \approx 572.4 \text{ ₹/m}^2$$

> *Logic:* Dividing per-piece cost by surface area gives a normalized metric used to compare different coating processes (e.g., powder coating vs electroplating).

**Step 8: Payback Period**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{I}{\text{Net Annual Profit}} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

> *Logic:* Payback = how many years of profit are needed to recover the initial ₹15 lakh investment.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Computed Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Annual Production ($N$) | 24,000 pieces | Capacity planning |
| Depreciation ($D$) | ₹1,35,000/year | Tax & replacement |
| Annual Variable Cost | ₹2,88,000/year | Direct production expense |
| Annual Electricity | ₹24,000/year | Utility cost |
| Total Annual Cost | ₹6,47,000/year | Cash outflow |
| Cost per Piece | ₹26.96 | Pricing benchmark |
| Cost per m² | ₹572.4 | Process comparison |
| Payback Period | 5 years | Investment recovery |

### 3.3 Break-Even Volume Derivation

If the company wants to find the production volume where total cost = total revenue:

Let $P$ = selling price = ₹40/piece, $V$ = variable cost per piece = ₹12 + (electricity per piece)
Electricity per piece = $24{,}000 / 24{,}000 = ₹1$ → Total $V = ₹13$

Fixed cost $FC = D + \text{Overheads} = 1{,}35{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}35{,}000$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - V} = \frac{3{,}35{,}000}{40 - 13} = \frac{3{,}35{,}000}{27} \approx 12{,}407 \text{ pieces}$$

> *Logic:* At ~12,407 pieces, the company neither makes profit nor loss. Since actual production is 24,000 pieces, the plant is operating **safely above break-even**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU loves this question:** It tests **five distinct economic concepts** (depreciation, variable cost, electricity cost, per-piece cost, payback) in **one integrated problem** — exactly matching the 14-mark weightage with internal choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
