\pi r^{2} h = \pi (0.05)^{2}(0.05) = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{vol}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times 10{,}000 = 3.927 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{mass}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times 10{,}000 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}
$$

> **Conversion:** 3.08 × 10⁻⁵ kg/s = **110.9 g/h** — a typical roughing MRR for die-sinking EDM.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Process Outcomes

This is the single most important KTU summary table — examiners frequently ask parameter-influence questions (6–7 marks).

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish (Ra) | Effect on Tool Wear |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Peak current *I* | ↑↑ Increases | ↑↑ Worsens (deeper craters) | ↑ Increases |
| ↑ Pulse on-time *tᵢ* | ↑↑ Increases | ↑↑ Worsens | ↑ Increases |
| ↑ Pulse off-time *tₒ* | ↓ Decreases (less sparking) | Improves (debris flushed) | ↓ Reduces |
| ↑ Capacitance *C* | ↑ Increases (larger spark) | ↑ Worsens | ↑ Increases |
| ↓ Open-circuit voltage *V_s* | ↓ Decreases | Improves (smaller gap) | ↓ Reduces |
| ↑ Dielectric flushing | ↑ Slight increase | Improves | ↓ Reduces |
| Reversed polarity (workpiece = −) | ↓ Decreases MRR | Improves | ↓↓ Drastically reduces tool wear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EDM System Block Diagram (High-Detailed Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePS["DC Power Supply V_s"]
    nodeR["Charging Resistor R"]
    nodeC["Capacitor C"]
    nodeG["Spark Gap Tool to Workpiece"]
    nodeW["Workpiece Anode"]
    nodeT["Tool Cathode"]
    nodeD["Dielectric Fluid Reservoir"]
    nodeP["Dielectric Pump"]
    nodeF["Filter and Cooler"]
    nodeS["Servo Feed System"]
    nodeCN["CNC Control Unit"]
    nodeV["Voltage Comparator and Gap Sensor"]

    nodePS --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeW
    nodeG --> nodeT
    nodeG --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeCN --> nodeS
    nodeCN --> nodePS
    nodeD --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The upper **left-to-right branch** (PS → R → C → Gap) is the classical *R-C relaxation circuit*.
- The lower **closed loop** (Dielectric → Pump → Filter → Gap) represents *flushing and cooling*.
- The right-side **feedback loop** (Gap → Voltage Comparator → Servo → Tool) is the *automatic gap control* that maintains the optimal overcut.

## 4.2 R-C Relaxation Circuit — Equivalent Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVS["DC Source V_s"]
    nodeSW["Switching Element"]
    nodeRES["Resistor R"]
    nodeCAP["Capacitor C"]
    nodeGP["Spark Gap V_b breakdown"]
    nodeWRK["Workpiece"]

    nodeVS --> nodeSW
    nodeSW --> nodeRES
    nodeRES --> nodeCAP
    nodeCAP --> nodeGP
    nodeGP --> nodeWRK
    nodeWRK --> nodeVS
```

**Operating cycle:**
1. Switch closes → capacitor *C* charges through *R* toward *V_s*.
2. Voltage at *C* reaches *V_b* → dielectric ionizes → spark fires → *C* discharges through gap.
3. Voltage collapses → dielectric de-ionizes → cycle repeats.

## 4.3 EDM Process Decision Flow (For Selecting Parameters)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start: Choose Operation Type"]
    nodeQ1{"Required Surface Finish Ra less than 1 micrometer?"}
    nodeFin["Finishing Mode: Low I, Short t_i, High f"]
    nodeQ2{"Material Hardness greater than HRC 55?"}
    nodeEDM["EDM is Suitable Process"]
    nodeQ3{"Cavity Depth greater than 50 mm?"}
    nodeWire["Consider Wire EDM"]
    nodeParam["Adjust I t_i C based on MRR and Ra Targets"]
    nodeEnd["End: Run EDM with Flushing and Servo Control"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeFin
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeEDM
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeParam
    nodeEDM --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeWire
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeParam
    nodeFin --> nodeParam
    nodeWire --> nodeParam
    nodeParam --> nodeEnd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *List the four stages of a single EDM spark discharge and state the function of the dielectric fluid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ionization of dielectric** (0.5 mark): When gap voltage exceeds the dielectric strength, the insulating fluid ionizes into a plasma channel.
2. **Spark discharge** (0.5 mark): A high-density current pulse (~10⁴–10⁶ A/cm²) flows through the plasma, heating the workpiece surface to 8,000–20,000 °C.
3. **Material removal by melting and vaporization** (1 mark): A micro-crater forms on the workpiece; ~90 % material is melted, ~10 % vaporized.
4. **Dielectric quenching and flushing** (0.5 mark): Pulse ends, plasma collapses, fresh dielectric removes the resolidified debris and re-establishes insulation.

**Function of dielectric fluid (0.5 mark):** Acts as an insulator between the electrodes, becomes an ionizable medium during the spark, flushes away debris, and cools the workpiece and tool.

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between a relaxation (RC) generator and a transistorized pulse generator used in EDM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | RC Generator | Transistorized Generator |
|---|---|---|
| Spark energy control (1 mark) | Energy depends on C and V_b; cannot be varied independently of frequency | Pulse on/off times independently controllable via electronic switching |
| Frequency (1 mark) | 0.5 – 10 kHz, coupled to spark energy | 1 – 500 kHz, independent of spark energy |
| Application (1 mark) | Simple conventional EDM, finishing | Modern CNC EDM, high MRR, mirror finish possible |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1(A) — *(Module 3 Choice — Path A)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of Electrical Discharge Machining with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the dielectric fluid and the servo control system. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An RC relaxation EDM generator has R = 200 Ω, C = 2 µF, supply voltage V_s = 120 V, and breakdown voltage V_b = 70 V. Calculate (i) the charging time per cycle, and (ii) the pulse frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle (3 marks):** EDM removes metal by **controlled, repetitive electrical sparks** in a dielectric medium. The tool and workpiece are separated by a small gap (0.01–0.5 mm). The pulse generator creates high-frequency voltage pulses. When the gap voltage exceeds the dielectric breakdown strength, a spark jumps across, melting and vaporizing a tiny volume of the workpiece. The molten material is flushed away by the dielectric, the plasma collapses during the off-time, and the cycle repeats at frequencies up to 500 kHz. There is no mechanical contact between tool and workpiece.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. Briefly describe: DC source → R → C → gap → comparator → servo feedback → tool feed.

**Role of dielectric fluid (1 mark):** It *insulates* during the off-time, becomes a *conductive plasma* during discharge, *cools* the electrodes, and *flushes* away the eroded particles.

**Servo control (1 mark):** The comparator senses the gap voltage; if it is *too high* (open circuit, no sparking), the servo advances the tool; if it is *too low* (short circuit), it retracts. This maintains a constant optimum spark gap and prevents arcing.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** R = 200 Ω, C = 2 µF = 2 × 10⁻⁶ F, V_s = 120 V, V_b = 70 V.

**(i) Charging time t_c (4 marks):**

$$
t_c = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_s - V_b}\right)
$$

$$
RC = 200 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
V_s - V_b = 120 - 70 = 50 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\frac{V_s}{V_s - V_b} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.4
$$

$$
\ln(2.4) = 0.8755
$$

$$
t_c = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.8755 = 3.502 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_c \approx 350.2 \;\mu\text{s}\,}
$$

**(ii) Pulse frequency f (3 marks):**

$$
f = \frac{1}{t_c} = \frac{1}{3.502 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2855 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f \approx 2.86 \;\text{kHz}\,}
$$

This lies within the typical 0.5–10 kHz range of an RC relaxation generator — **verified**.

---

### Question B1(B) — *(Module 3 Choice — Path B, Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
> **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a relaxation-type (RC) EDM generator. Derive the expression for the charging time and the frequency of pulses. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Define Material Removal Rate in EDM. An EDM operation produces hemispherical craters of radius 0.08 mm and depth 0.08 mm at a pulse frequency of 5 kHz on a steel workpiece (ρ = 7.85 g/cm³). Calculate the volumetric and mass MRR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (2 marks):** The RC relaxation generator consists of:
- A **DC voltage source (V_s)**
- A **charging resistor R** in series
- A **capacitor C** in parallel with the **spark gap** (tool–workpiece gap)
- The gap acts as a *voltage-controlled switch* that closes (breaks down) when the voltage across C reaches the breakdown value V_b.

**Working (2 marks):** Capacitor charges through R from 0 V toward V_s according to V_c(t) = V_s(1 − e^(−t/RC)). When V_c reaches V_b, the dielectric breaks down, the gap becomes a low-resistance plasma, and the capacitor discharges almost instantaneously (in microseconds) through the gap, eroding a small amount of metal. The voltage collapses, the dielectric recovers its insulating property, and the cycle repeats automatically — a self-sustaining *relaxation oscillation*.

**Derivation of charging time t_c (2 marks):**

Starting from V_c(t) = V_s(1 − e^(−t/RC)) and setting V_c = V_b at t = t_c:

$$
V_b = V_s(1 - e^{-t_c/RC}) \;\Rightarrow\; t_c = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_s - V_b}\right)
$$

**Frequency (1 mark):**

$$
f = \frac{1}{t_c} = \frac{1}{RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_s - V_b}\right)}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Definition of MRR (2 marks):** Material Removal Rate in EDM is defined as the *volume (or mass) of workpiece material removed per unit time* during the spark erosion process. It is typically expressed in **mm³/min** or **g/min**. MRR depends on discharge current, pulse on-time, frequency, and the thermophysical properties of the workpiece material.

**Numerical calculation (5 marks):** Given: r = 0.08 mm, h = 0.08 mm, f = 5 kHz = 5000 Hz, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³ = 7.85 × 10⁻³ g/mm³.

*Volume per crater:*

$$
V_{\text{crater}} = \pi r^{2} h = \pi (0.08)^{2}(0.08)
$$

$$
V_{\text{crater}} = \pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.08 = \pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V_{\text{crater}} \approx 1.6085 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

*Volumetric MRR:*

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{vol}} = V_{\text{crater}} \times f = 1.6085 \times 10^{-3} \times 5000
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MRR}_{\text{vol}} \approx 8.04 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 482.6 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}\,}
$$

*Mass MRR:*

$$
\text{MRR}_{\text{mass}} = \text{MRR}_{\text{vol}} \times \rho = 8.04 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MRR}_{\text{mass}} \approx 0.0631 \;\text{g/s} \approx 3.79 \;\text{g/min}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. **Forgetting the Ln function** in the charging-time derivation. Many students write *t_c = RC(V_s − V_b)* — this is **wrong**; Ln is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Conflating the roles of R and C.** *R* controls *frequency*, *C* controls *spark energy*. Swapping them costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong polarity convention.** In die-sinking EDM the *workpiece is the anode*; in wire-EDM the *workpiece is the cathode* (to protect the wire). A common 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in MRR.** Mixing mm³/s with g/min mid-calculation. Convert to a single system at the start.
> 5. **Writing "EDM is a mechanical process."** EDM is a *thermal/electro-thermal* process. This single sentence can cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing arcing with sparking.** *Arcing* = uncontrolled continuous discharge (damaging). *Sparking* = controlled pulsed discharge (productive). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Re-read 24 hours before the KTU exam.**

### Core Concepts
- EDM is a **non-contact, thermo-electric** material removal process.
- Material is removed by **melting (~90 %) and vaporization (~10 %)** at temperatures of **8,000 – 20,000 °C**.
- The four stages of every spark: **ionization → discharge → material removal → quenching**.
- **No mechanical hardness limit** for the workpiece — even tungsten carbide, ceramics, and Inconel are easily machined.

### Equipment Essentials
- **Six subsystems**: pulse generator, servo feed, tool holder, dielectric system, work tank, control unit.
- **Dielectric functions**: insulation, ionization medium, coolant, debris flusher.
- **Polarity in die-sinking**: Tool = (−), Workpiece = (+).
- **Polarity in wire-EDM**: Workpiece = (−), Wire = (+) — *reversed to reduce wire wear*.

### Generator & R-C Circuit
- **RC generator** is *self-excited* relaxation oscillator; pulse energy depends only on *C* and *V_b*.
- **Transistorized generator** allows *independent* control of on-time, off-time, and current → preferred for modern CNC EDM.
- **Key formula**: $t_c = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_s - V_b}\right)$
- **Energy per spark**: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_b^{2} - V_1^{2}) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} C V_b^{2}$
- **Frequency**: $f = \dfrac{1}{RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_s - V_b}\right)}$
- *R* controls **frequency**; *C* controls **spark energy**.

### MRR
- $\text{MRR}_{\text{vol}} = \pi r^{2} h f$ (hemispherical crater model)
- MRR ↑ with ↑ I, ↑ tᵢ, ↑ C; MRR ↓ with ↑ tₒ (more flushing time, less sparking).

### Surface Finish
- Empirical relation: $R_a \propto I^{1.1} \cdot t_i^{0.4}$
- **Finishing**: low current, short on-time → Ra ≈ 0.4 µm.
- **Roughing**: high current, long on-time → Ra ≈ 4 – 10 µm.

### Applications (Memorize 4)
1. Die & mold manufacturing.
2. Hard/brittle/exotic alloys.
3. Wire-EDM for 2-D profiles.
4. Broken tool / drill removal.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Don't say "EDM cuts metal" — it *erodes* metal thermally.
- Don't confuse *arc* with *spark*.
- Don't forget the Ln in the charging time formula.
- Don't swap R and C roles in viva questions.
- Don't state wrong polarity for wire-EDM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Beam Machining (EBM) - Principle of Working, Equipment, Material Removal Rate, Process Parameters, Performance Characterization and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Machining (LBM) - Principle of Working, Equipment, Material Removal Rate, Process Parameters, Performance Characterization and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Beam Machining (LBM)** is a non-contact, thermal-type advanced material removal process that utilizes a highly focused, coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of light (amplified via *Stimulated Emission of Radiation*) to remove material through **melting, vaporization, and ablation**. The photon energy of the laser beam is absorbed by the workpiece surface, where it is converted into intense localized heat, raising the temperature above the material's melting or vaporization point. The process falls under the category of **Energy Beam Machining (EBM)** processes, alongside Electron Beam Machining (EBM-h), Plasma Arc Machining (PAM), and Water Jet Machining (WJM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LBM is classified as a **thermal, non-contact, no tool wear** process. The energy source is a photon beam, NOT a mechanical tool. This is the most fundamental conceptual distinction students must internalize before the examination.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a magnifying glass under bright sunlight and focusing the sun's rays onto a single spot on a piece of dry paper. Within seconds, the paper **smokes, scorches, and burns a hole** at that focal point. 

LBM works on the **exact same principle** — but instead of sunlight (which is dispersed, multi-colored, and incoherent), we use a **LASER** (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation):
- **Coherent** → all light waves move in lock-step (in phase).
- **Monochromatic** → single, pure wavelength (single color).
- **Collimated** → rays travel parallel without spreading.

By focusing this disciplined light through a special lens, we deliver an extraordinarily high energy density (often **10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm²**) onto a tiny spot. The metal simply has nowhere to dump this energy except by melting and evaporating — resulting in clean, precise material removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The L.A.S.E.R. Acronym (Likely 2-Mark Question):**
> **L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation
> Invented by **Theodore Maiman** in **1960** (first operational laser, ruby laser).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Laser Power | $P$ | $10$ – $10^4$ | W |
| Beam Diameter (focused) | $d_b$ | $0.1$ – $1.0$ | mm |
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) | $\lambda$ | $0.4$ – $10.6$ | µm |
| Power Density | $I$ | $10^5$ – $10^7$ | W/mm² |
| Specific Energy | $E_s$ | $10^3$ – $10^5$ | J/mm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule:** For a material to be machined by LBM, the absorbed power density must exceed the threshold power density. Metals typically require **$10^6$ to $10^7$ W/mm²**; ceramics and non-metals require **$10^5$ to $10^6$ W/mm²**.

## 1.4 Comparison with Other Thermal Machining Processes

| Process | Energy Source | Atmosphere | Vacuum? | Key Limitation |
|--------|---------------|------------|---------|----------------|
| **LBM** | Photons | Air / Inert gas | No | Limited to absorptive materials |
| EBM (Electron) | Electrons | Vacuum | Yes | Expensive vacuum chamber |
| PAM (Plasma) | Ionized gas plasma | Gas | No | Lower precision |
| AJM (Abrasive Jet) | Abrasive + Gas | Air | No | Soft materials only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LBM is preferred over EBM for many applications:** LBM can operate in **ambient atmosphere**, making it far more convenient and cheaper than Electron Beam Machining, which requires a high-vacuum chamber. However, LBM cannot match the precision of EBM for micro-machining of dense metals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Working — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The LBM process follows a rigorously defined energy-transfer chain. Let us break it down into six structured steps:

1. **Generation of Laser Beam:** Electrical/optical pumping excites atoms in the lasing medium (e.g., CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber) to a higher energy state. *Stimulated emission* produces photons that are amplified within an optical resonator (two parallel mirrors) until a powerful, coherent beam emerges.

2. **Beam Transmission:** The beam is routed through a beam delivery system — either **mirrors and lenses** (for CO₂) or **optical fibers** (for Nd:YAG and fiber lasers) — to the focusing head.

3. **Beam Focusing:** A focusing lens (often made of ZnSe for CO₂) concentrates the beam to a focal spot of diameter $d_b$ at the workpiece surface. Spot size is governed by:
$$d_b = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f}{D}$$
where $f$ is focal length of lens and $D$ is unfocused beam diameter.

4. **Energy Absorption:** When the laser beam strikes the workpiece, photons penetrate a thin surface layer (typically $0.1$ to $1$ µm) and are absorbed by free electrons in metals or by molecular bonds in non-metals. **Absorptivity ($\alpha$)** is critical — highly reflective metals (e.g., Cu, Al) absorb poorly at room temperature, so they may be pre-coated or pre-heated.

5. **Thermal Conversion:** The absorbed photon energy is converted into lattice vibrational energy (heat). The local temperature rises at a rate of $10^6$ to $10^8$ K/s — a near-instantaneous thermal spike.

6. **Material Removal Mechanisms (in order of energy density):**
   - **Heating** (below melting point)
   - **Melting** (most common in cutting/drilling)
   - **Vaporization** (in drilling of deep micro-holes)
   - **Ablation** (cold removal by breaking chemical bonds — used in Excimer lasers for polymers, ceramics)
   - **Chemical Degradation** (e.g., wood cutting via pyrolysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Students often confuse the dominant removal mechanism. **For metals → Melting + Vaporization (thermal). For non-metals (polymer, ceramic) → Ablation (photo-chemical).** Remember this distinction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Focused Beam Diameter** | $d_b = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f}{D}$ | Minimum spot size; $f$ = focal length, $D$ = beam dia |
| 2 | **Power Density (Intensity)** | $I = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_b^2}$ | W/mm²; governs machining capability |
| 3 | **MRR — Drilling (Cylindrical)** | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, f$ | mm³/s; $d$ = hole dia, $f$ = feed |
| 4 | **MRR — Cutting (Kerf)** | $\text{MRR} = v \, \cdot \, t \, \cdot \, w$ | mm³/s; $v$ = cut speed, $t$ = thickness, $w$ = kerf width |
| 5 | **Specific Energy** | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{P}{v \, t \, w}$ | J/mm³; key performance metric |
| 6 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | $\text{HAZ} \propto \dfrac{\kappa \, \Delta t}{v}$ | $ \kappa$ = thermal diffusivity |
| 7 | **Kerf Width (Empirical)** | $w = w_0 + k\sqrt{t}$ | $w_0$ = minimum kerf, $k$ = empirical const |
| 8 | **Energy per Unit Volume** | $E_v = \dfrac{\eta \, P}{v \, A_c}$ | $\eta$ = coupling efficiency |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Rule Applied:** All absolute value / magnitude notations in formulas use \vert or \mid to avoid breaking table syntax. Ensure formulas are studied in their rendered (LaTeX) form.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LBM is the backbone of modern **precision manufacturing**. Specific applications include:
- **Aerospace** — Drilling thousands of micro-holes in turbine blades (for film cooling).
- **Automotive** — Trimming and profiling of tailored blanks.
- **Electronics** — Drilling via-holes in printed circuit boards (PCBs).
- **Medical Devices** — Cutting stents and manufacturing hypodermic needles.
- **Textile & Leather** — Cutting intricate patterns without fraying (since the heat seals the edges).
- **Dies & Molds** — Engraving hardened tool steel (HRC > 60) that cannot be cut by conventional tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LBM is unbeatable for super-hard materials:** The energy density of a focused laser exceeds the strength of *any* material — diamond, tungsten carbide, or even ceramics. There is no mechanical force involved, so hardness is irrelevant.

## 2.4 Types of Industrial Lasers (Must-Know Table)

| Laser Type | Active Medium | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Best For |
|------------|---------------|----------------------|----------|
| **CO₂ Laser** | CO₂ + N₂ + He gas | $10.6$ µm | Non-metals (wood, plastic, leather), thick metal cutting |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | Neodymium-doped YAG crystal | $1.06$ µm | Metals (drilling, welding), can be fiber-coupled |
| **Fiber Laser** | Doped optical fiber | $1.07$ µm | High-efficiency metal cutting, marking |
| **Excimer Laser** | Reactive gas (e.g., ArF) | $0.193$ µm (UV) | Micro-machining, ablation of polymers/ceramics |
| **Ruby Laser** | Chromium in Al₂O₃ | $0.694$ µm (red) | First laser, mostly historical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** CO₂ laser has $\lambda = 10.6$ µm (far-infrared). Nd:YAG has $\lambda = 1.06$ µm (near-infrared). Since metals reflect infrared less than far-infrared, **Nd:YAG is generally better for metals**, while **CO₂ is excellent for non-metals**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Focused Spot Size

**Starting Premise:** A Gaussian laser beam of diameter $D$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is focused by a lens of focal length $f$. We wish to find the diameter $d_b$ of the focused spot at the focal plane.

**Step 1 — Airy Disc Foundation:**
For a perfect circular lens focusing a parallel, coherent beam, the diffraction-limited spot radius is given by the Airy criterion. The half-angle of the converging cone is:
$$\theta = \dfrac{D/2}{f} = \dfrac{D}{2f}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Diffraction Limit:**
The first minimum of the Airy pattern occurs at angle:
$$\sin \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$$

For small angles (typical in lasers): $\sin \theta \approx \theta$.
$$\dfrac{D}{2f} = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Spot Diameter:**
Rearranging the equation to express the focused spot diameter $d_b$:
$$d_b = 1.22 \, \lambda \, \cdot \, \dfrac{f}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Interpretation:**
- If $\lambda = 10.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m (CO₂), $f = 50$ mm, $D = 10$ mm:
$$d_b = 1.22 \times 10.6 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$d_b = 1.22 \times 10.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 6.466 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 64.66 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To get a smaller spot, reduce $f$ (use a shorter focal length lens) or increase $D$ (use a beam expander). The wavelength $\lambda$ is fixed by the laser type. This is why beam expanders are common in industrial LBM setups.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Density at Focused Spot

**Starting Premise:** A laser of power $P$ is focused to a spot of diameter $d_b$. Find the power density $I$.

**Step 1 — Area of Focused Spot (assumed circular):**
$$A_b = \dfrac{\pi \, d_b^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Power Density Definition:**
Power density is power per unit area:
$$I = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{P}{\pi d_b^2 / 4}$$

**Step 3 — Final Form:**
$$\boxed{I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \, d_b^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
A $P = 1000$ W CO₂ laser is focused to a spot of $d_b = 0.2$ mm.
$$I = \dfrac{4 \times 1000}{\pi \times (0.2)^2} = \dfrac{4000}{0.1257} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 3.18 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

This is well above the threshold for steel machining.

## 3.3 Derivation: Material Removal Rate for Laser Drilling

**Starting Premise:** A laser drills a cylindrical hole of diameter $d$ to a depth $L$ at a constant feed rate $f$ (mm/s). Derive the MRR.

**Step 1 — Volume of One Hole:**
$$V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, L$$

**Step 2 — Time Taken:**
$$T = \dfrac{L}{f}$$

**Step 3 — MRR Definition (Volume per unit time):**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V}{T} = \dfrac{\pi d^2 L / 4}{L / f}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**
$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, f}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Numerically):**
Drilling a $d = 0.5$ mm hole at $f = 10$ mm/s in steel:
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.5)^2 \times 10 = \dfrac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.25 \times 10 = 1.963 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Material Removal Rate for Laser Cutting

**Starting Premise:** A laser cuts along a straight path of length $L$ through a sheet of thickness $t$ with kerf width $w$ at cutting speed $v$ (mm/s). Derive MRR.

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Cut:**
The cut creates a rectangular channel (approximately) of width $w$ and depth $t$:
$$A_c = w \times t$$

**Step 2 — Time to Cut Length L:**
$$T = \dfrac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Volume Removed:**
$$V = A_c \times L = w \, t \, L$$

**Step 4 — MRR:**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{V}{T} = \dfrac{w \, t \, L}{L / v}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification:**
$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = v \, \cdot \, t \, \cdot \, w}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Specific Energy (Most Important Performance Metric)

**Starting Premise:** Specific energy $E_s$ is defined as the energy required to remove a unit volume of material. Derive the formula in terms of laser power $P$ and MRR.

**Step 1 — Definition:**
$$E_s = \dfrac{\text{Energy Supplied}}{\text{Volume Removed}} = \dfrac{P \cdot T}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Express in Terms of MRR:**
Since $\text{MRR} = V/T$, we have $T = V/\text{MRR}$. Substituting:
$$E_s = \dfrac{P \cdot V / \text{MRR}}{V} = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}}$$

**Step 3 — For Cutting Case (Substitute MRR = v·t·w):**
$$\boxed{E_s = \dfrac{P}{v \, t \, w}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**
$E_s$ has units of J/mm³. It represents the energy cost of machining. **Lower $E_s$ means higher process efficiency.** Materials with high thermal conductivity (e.g., copper) require higher $E_s$ because heat dissipates quickly away from the cut zone.

**Step 5 — Typical Values:**

| Material | $E_s$ (J/mm³) |
|----------|---------------|
| Plastics | $2$ – $5$ |
| Steel | $10$ – $30$ |
| Titanium | $30$ – $50$ |
| Ceramics | $50$ – $200$ |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on MRR | Effect on Kerf Width | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Quality |
|------------------------|---------------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Laser Power ($P$)** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ May worsen |
| **Cutting Speed ($v$)** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Improves (up to a limit) |
| **Material Thickness ($t$)** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Degrades |
| **Focal Point Position** | Variable | Variable | Variable | Critical — must be optimized |
| **Assist Gas Pressure** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Improves (blows away debris) |
| **Pulse Frequency** | ↑ Increases (up to limit) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Improves (for pulsed) |
| **Beam Mode (TEM₀₀ vs TEM₀₁*)** | ↓ for TEM₀₁ | ↑ for TEM₀₁ | ↑ for TEM₀₁ | ↓ TEM₀₀ is best |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** TEM₀₀ (Transverse Electromagnetic Mode 00) is the **Gaussian** mode, which gives the most concentrated, round beam. This is the *preferred* mode for high-quality machining. Multi-mode beams (TEM₀₁*) distribute energy over a larger area and are used for **welding**, not cutting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LBM Process Block Diagram (Component-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the full functional architecture of an industrial LBM setup — from electrical energy input to the final machined feature on the workpiece.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PS[Power Supply Module]
        PSU1[High Voltage DC Power Supply]
        PSU2[Capacitor Bank for Pulsing]
    end

    subgraph LS[Laser Source Module]
        LM1[Lasing Medium CO2 or NdYAG or Fiber]
        LM2[Optical Resonator Two Mirrors]
        LM3[Cooling System Water or Air]
    end

    subgraph BD[Beam Delivery System]
        BD1[Beam Expander Increases Diameter D]
        BD2[Mirror M1 Deflects Beam]
        BD3[Mirror M2 Deflects Beam]
        BD4[Optical Fiber for NdYAG and Fiber Lasers]
    end

    subgraph FH[Focusing Head]
        FH1[Focusing Lens ZnSe for CO2]
        FH2[Focal Point Position Adjustable]
        FH3[Nozzle for Assist Gas]
    end

    subgraph AS[Assist Gas System]
        AS1[Gas Cylinder O2 or N2 or Ar]
        AS2[Pressure Regulator]
        AS3[Gas Flow Meter]
    end

    subgraph WP[Workpiece Station]
        WP1[CNC XY Table]
        WP2[Workpiece Clamping Fixture]
        WP3[Exhaust and Fume Extraction]
    end

    subgraph CTL[Control System]
        CTL1[CNC Controller]
        CTL2[Process Parameter Monitor]
        CTL3[Safety Interlocks and Enclosure]
    end

    PSU1 --> LM1
    PSU2 --> LM1
    LM1 --> LM2
    LM3 -.chills.-> LM1
    LM2 --> BD1
    BD1 --> BD2
    BD2 --> BD3
    BD3 --> FH1
    BD4 --> FH1
    FH1 --> FH2
    FH2 --> WP2
    AS1 --> AS2
    AS2 --> AS3
    AS3 --> FH3
    FH3 --> WP2
    WP1 --> WP2
    CTL1 --> WP1
    CTL2 --> LM1
    CTL3 --> FH1
    WP2 --> WP3
```

## 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

### **A. Power Supply Module**
Converts standard AC mains (230 V, 50 Hz) into **high-voltage DC** (often $10$–$50$ kV) needed to excite the lasing medium. The capacitor bank stores energy for **pulsed operation** (Q-switching).

### **B. Laser Source Module**
- **Lasing Medium:** The "gain medium" where stimulated emission occurs. For CO₂ lasers, this is a gas mixture of CO₂, N₂, and He.
- **Optical Resonator:** Two mirrors facing each other — one fully reflective, one partially transmissive (output coupler). Photons bounce back and forth, triggering more stimulated emission, amplifying the beam.
- **Cooling System:** Critical because only ~10–20% of electrical energy becomes laser light; the rest is heat that must be removed (often by chilled water).

### **C. Beam Delivery System**
- **Beam Expander:** Increases beam diameter $D$ to reduce the final spot size (recall $d_b = 1.22\lambda f / D$).
- **Mirrors (M1, M2):** For CO₂, mirrors are made of **Mo (molybdenum)** or **Au-coated copper** since glass is opaque to 10.6 µm.
- **Optical Fiber:** For Nd:YAG and fiber lasers, the beam is transmitted through a flexible fiber — this is a major advantage for robotic arms.

### **D. Focusing Head**
- **Focusing Lens:** ZnSe lens for CO₂, fused silica for Nd:YAG. The lens converges the beam to a focal spot of ~0.1–0.5 mm.
- **Focal Point Position:** The focal point may be placed **on the surface**, **above the surface**, or **below the surface** — each position gives a different kerf and HAZ.
- **Nozzle:** Coaxial with the beam, delivers assist gas to the cutting zone.

### **E. Assist Gas System**
- **Oxygen (O₂):** Exothermic reaction with steel → increases cutting efficiency. Used for **thick** mild steel.
- **Nitrogen (N₂):** Inert — produces clean, oxide-free cut for **stainless steel and titanium**.
- **Argon (Ar):** For reactive metals (e.g., titanium) where oxidation must be avoided.
- Gas pressure typically $1$ – $20$ bar.

### **F. Workpiece Station**
- **CNC Table:** Moves the workpiece in X-Y (or X-Y-Z) under the stationary beam, or moves the beam via a gantry.
- **Fixture:** Holds the workpiece rigidly; vacuum chucks or mechanical clamps are common.

### **G. Control System**
- **CNC Controller:** Reads G-code and synchronizes table motion with laser on/off and power modulation.
- **Process Monitor:** Sensors for power, gas pressure, temperature, and focus position.
- **Safety Interlocks:** Class IV laser enclosures, E-stop circuits, laser-warning signs, and protective eyewear with the correct **Optical Density (OD)** rating for the laser's wavelength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Safety Note:** Industrial LBM systems are **Class IV lasers** (most hazardous). They can cause instant eye damage, skin burns, and ignite materials. The entire beam path must be enclosed, and operators must wear wavelength-specific goggles.

## 4.3 Energy Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Electrical Energy Input] -->|10 to 20 percent efficient| E2[Laser Beam Photon Energy]
    E2 -->|Focused| E3[Workpiece Surface Absorption]
    E3 -->|Converted to heat| E4[Melting and Vaporization]
    E4 -->|Material ejected| E5[Removed Material plus Vapour]
    E1 -.wasted as heat.-> E6[Cooling System Loss]
    E1 -.wasted as fluorescence.-> E7[Optical Losses]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Set A]**
**Define Laser Beam Machining. List any four characteristics of the laser beam that make it suitable for material processing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Laser Beam Machining (LBM) is a non-contact, thermal material removal process in which a highly focused, coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of light is used to remove material by melting and vaporization.

**Four characteristics of laser beam suitable for material processing:**
1. **Coherence** — All photons are in phase, allowing the beam to be focused to a diffraction-limited spot, achieving very high power density.
2. **Monochromaticity** — Single wavelength allows wavelength-selective absorption by the workpiece and predictable optical system design.
3. **Collimation** — Parallel rays do not diverge significantly, so the beam retains its intensity over long distances, enabling precise delivery.
4. **High Power Density** — Focused laser delivers $10^6$ to $10^7$ W/mm², sufficient to melt and vaporize any material, including super-hard alloys and ceramics.

(Any other valid characteristics like high directionality, controlled pulse duration, no tool wear — also accepted.)

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]**
**Differentiate between Laser Beam Machining (LBM) and Electron Beam Machining (EBM) in any six aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Aspect | LBM | EBM |
|---|--------|-----|-----|
| 1 | Energy source | Photon beam (light) | Electron beam |
| 2 | Medium of travel | Air / gas / fiber | Vacuum (mandatory) |
| 3 | Vacuum chamber required? | No | Yes (essential) |
| 4 | Beam deflection | Mirrors / fiber | Electromagnetic coils |
| 5 | Power efficiency | 10 – 20 % | 70 – 80 % |
| 6 | Suitable for non-conductors? | Yes (wood, plastic) | No (electrons scatter in non-conductors) |
| 7 | X-ray hazard | None | Significant (requires shielding) |
| 8 | Cost | Lower | Higher (vacuum + shielding) |

(Any six of the above for 3 marks.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of working and the main equipment** used in Laser Beam Machining. List the **three main types of industrial lasers** with their typical wavelengths and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-wave CO₂ laser of **$P = 1500$ W** is focused to a spot diameter of **$d_b = 0.3$ mm** to cut a steel plate of thickness **$t = 4$ mm**. The cutting speed is **$v = 25$ mm/s** and the kerf width achieved is **$w = 0.5$ mm**. Calculate:
   (i) Power density at the focal spot
   (ii) Material Removal Rate (MRR)
   (iii) Specific Energy for the process
   **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principle and Equipment:**

*Principle:* A laser is generated by *stimulated emission* of radiation from a pumped lasing medium placed in an optical resonator. The emerging beam is directed through beam delivery optics and focused by a lens onto the workpiece. At the focal point, the power density is so high ($10^6$ W/mm²) that the material is heated, melted, and partially vaporized, removing material. An assist gas (O₂ or N₂) blows away the molten/vaporized material from the kerf.

*Main Equipment (six components):*
1. **Laser source** — Generates the coherent beam (lasing medium + resonator + pump).
2. **Power supply** — Provides high-voltage DC for pumping.
3. **Cooling system** — Removes waste heat from the laser source.
4. **Beam delivery system** — Mirrors / optical fibers route the beam.
5. **Focusing head** — Lens + nozzle for assist gas.
6. **CNC work table** — Moves the workpiece precisely.
7. **Control system & safety enclosure** — CNC + interlocks.

*Three Main Industrial Lasers:*

| Laser | Wavelength | Application |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| CO₂ laser | 10.6 µm | Cutting non-metals, thick metal plates |
| Nd:YAG laser | 1.06 µm | Drilling, welding, marking of metals |
| Excimer laser | 0.193 µm (UV) | Micro-machining, polymer ablation |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Power Density at Focal Spot:**
$$I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \, d_b^2} = \dfrac{4 \times 1500}{\pi \times (0.3)^2}$$

$$I = \dfrac{6000}{0.2827} = 21{,}220.6 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 2.12 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Result:** $I \approx 2.12 \times 10^4$ W/mm² — this is well above the threshold for steel machining ($\sim 10^6$ W/mm² is in continuous regime, but lower values are usable for cutting).

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**
$$\text{MRR} = v \times t \times w = 25 \times 4 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Result:** MRR = 50 mm³/s.

**(iii) Specific Energy:**
$$E_s = \dfrac{P}{v \, t \, w} = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{1500}{50} = 30 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Result:** $E_s = 30$ J/mm³. This is consistent with the typical specific energy range for steel (10 – 30 J/mm³), confirming the calculation is physically reasonable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **process parameters** affecting Laser Beam Machining performance. Explain **performance characterization metrics** such as Material Removal Rate, Specific Energy, Heat Affected Zone, and Kerf Taper. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed Nd:YAG laser of **average power $P = 400$ W**, **pulse energy $E_p = 5$ J**, and **pulse frequency $f_p = 80$ Hz** is used to drill holes of diameter **$d = 0.6$ mm** in an aluminium sheet. The feed rate (drilling speed) is **$f = 5$ mm/s**. Calculate:
   (i) Pulse duration (assuming the average power equals peak power times duty cycle)
   (ii) Material Removal Rate
   **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Process Parameters & Performance Characterization:**

*Process Parameters:*
1. **Laser Power ($P$):** Higher power → higher MRR but larger HAZ.
2. **Cutting Speed ($v$):** Higher speed → lower HAZ, narrower kerf, but incomplete cut if too high.
3. **Focal Point Position:** On-surface, above, or below; determines kerf shape and HAZ.
4. **Beam Mode:** TEM₀₀ (Gaussian) preferred for quality; multi-mode for welding.
5. **Pulse Parameters:** Frequency, pulse duration, peak power (for pulsed lasers).
6. **Assist Gas:** Type (O₂/N₂/Ar) and pressure; controls chemistry and debris removal.
7. **Workpiece Properties:** Absorptivity, thermal conductivity, thickness, reflectivity.

*Performance Characterization Metrics:*

| Metric | Formula | Significance |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| **MRR** | $v \cdot t \cdot w$ | Productivity — volume removed per second |
| **Specific Energy** | $P/\text{MRR}$ | Energy efficiency — lower is better |
| **HAZ** | Region adjacent to cut with microstructural change | Lower is better (mechanical degradation) |
| **Kerf Taper** | $(w_{\text{top}} - w_{\text{bottom}})/t$ | Should be minimized for accuracy |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | Empirical vs speed | Striation pattern |
| **Dross Formation** | Visual inspection | Indicates incomplete vaporization |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Pulse Duration:**

Given:
- Average Power $P = 400$ W
- Pulse Energy $E_p = 5$ J
- Pulse Frequency $f_p = 80$ Hz

**Step 1 — Verify consistency:**
$$P = E_p \times f_p = 5 \times 80 = 400 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

This confirms the given data is consistent (no separate calculation needed for verification).

**Step 2 — Pulse Duration:**
For a pulsed laser, the average power is related to peak power $P_{\text{peak}}$ and duty cycle:
$$P = P_{\text{peak}} \times \tau \times f_p$$
where $\tau$ is pulse duration.

*Assuming the average power equals the peak power times the duty cycle (a common simplified assumption):*
$$P = P_{\text{peak}} \cdot (\tau \cdot f_p)$$

The duty cycle is $\tau \cdot f_p$, and we are told to assume average power equals peak power times duty cycle directly:
$$P = P_{\text{peak}} \times \tau \times f_p$$

Solving for pulse duration $\tau$ (assuming $P_{\text{peak}} = P$ at the simplest level, i.e., peak equals the per-pulse instantaneous power calculated as $E_p / \tau$):
$$P = \dfrac{E_p}{\tau} \times \tau \times f_p \Rightarrow P = E_p \times f_p$$

In the *standard KTU interpretation* where the average power is the relevant process power:
$$\tau = \dfrac{E_p}{P_{\text{peak}}}$$

If we take the simplification that the per-second energy delivered equals the per-pulse energy times the number of pulses (which is $E_p \times f_p = P$), the pulse duration for a typical Q-switched Nd:YAG is in microseconds. Given the level of the problem, the **expected answer is:**

$$\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{f_p} \cdot \text{(duty cycle)}$$

For a typical industrial Q-switched Nd:YAG, the duty cycle is ~ 0.1% to 1%, giving:
$$\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{80} \times 0.005 = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 62.5 \, \mu\text{s}$$

*Alternative simple interpretation (most KTU-marked):* If the question assumes pulse duration is the time during which one pulse delivers its energy, then:
$$\tau = \dfrac{E_p}{P_{\text{peak}}}$$

With the common assumption $P_{\text{peak}} \approx 10 \times P_{\text{avg}} = 4000$ W:
$$\tau = \dfrac{5}{4000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.25 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Without explicit peak power or duty cycle, the question is **ambiguous**. For 7-mark full credit, students should clearly state their assumption. The cleanest path: state $\tau = 1/f_p = 12.5$ ms is the *period*, and assume a typical duty cycle of 5–10% → pulse duration ~ **0.6 to 1.25 ms**. This will earn full credit with explicit assumptions shown.

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 \, f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.6)^2 \times 5$$

$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.36 \times 5 = 0.7854 \times 1.8 = 1.414 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Result:** MRR $\approx$ **1.41 mm³/s**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common places students lose marks in LBM questions are:
> 1. **Confusing TEM₀₀ with TEM₀₁\*** — TEM₀₀ is the *fundamental Gaussian mode* (best for cutting). TEM₀₁\* is multi-mode (welding).
> 2. **Forgetting that LBM does NOT need a vacuum** (unlike EBM). Examiners explicitly test this contrast.
> 3. **Mixing up MRR formulas for drilling vs cutting** — Drilling uses $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2 f$, Cutting uses $v \cdot t \cdot w$.
> 4. **Not stating units** in numerical answers. Always write W/mm², mm³/s, J/mm³.
> 5. **Forgetting the assist gas function** — it both removes molten debris AND provides exothermic energy (in O₂ cutting of steel).
> 6. **Saying "laser is monochromatic, so it's a single color"** — that's correct, but you should also note it means **wavelength-selective absorption** is possible.

### **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] LBM = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
- [ ] Invented by **Theodore Maiman, 1960** (ruby laser).
- [ ] LBM is a **thermal, non-contact, no tool wear** process.
- [ ] Operates in **air** (no vacuum needed — advantage over EBM).
- [ ] Energy density: **$10^5$ to $10^7$ W/mm²** at focal spot.
- [ ] Power density: $I = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_b^2}$.
- [ ] Focused spot size: $d_b = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda f}{D}$.
- [ ] MRR (drilling): $\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 f$.
- [ ] MRR (cutting): $v \cdot t \cdot w$.
- [ ] Specific Energy: $E_s = P / \text{MMR}$ — lower is better.
- [ ] CO₂ laser: $\lambda = 10.6$ µm, for non-metals + thick metal.
- [ ] Nd:YAG laser: $\lambda = 1.06$ µm, for metals (drilling, welding).
- [ ] Fiber laser: $\lambda = 1.07$ µm, high-efficiency metal cutting.
- [ ] Excimer laser: $\lambda = 0.193$ µm (UV), for polymer/ceramic ablation.
- [ ] Assist gases: **O₂** (carbon steel, exothermic), **N₂** (stainless steel, clean cut), **Ar** (titanium, no oxidation).
- [ ] TEM₀₀ = Gaussian (best for cutting), TEM₀₁\* = Multi-mode (welding).
- [ ] HAZ: Heat Affected Zone — minimized by high cutting speed.
- [ ] Kerf width: $w = w_0 + k\sqrt{t}$ — empirical.
- [ ] Kerf taper: top wider than bottom if focus is above surface.
- [ ] Applications: drilling micro-holes in turbine blades, cutting PCBs, trimming tailored blanks, engraving dies, stent cutting.
- [ ] Safety: **Class IV laser** — eye damage, skin burns, fire hazard.
- [ ] Compared to EBM: LBM works in air, no X-ray hazard, lower power efficiency, can machine non-conductors.
- [ ] Compared to AWJM: LBM has smaller kerf, less taper, but produces HAZ.
- [ ] Limitation: Highly reflective materials (Cu, Al) absorb poorly at room temperature — need pre-heating or coatings.
- [ ] Theoretically can machine **any material** because energy density exceeds all material strengths.
- [ ] The lasing medium must be cooled — only **10–20%** of input energy becomes laser light.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** When asked to "list the equipment" or "draw the block diagram," always remember to include the **Cooling System** and **Assist Gas System** — many students forget these and lose 1–2 marks. The 7-mark question typically expects 6 distinct equipment items plus the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Machining - Principle of Working, Equipment, Material Removal Rate, Process Parameters, Performance Characterization and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Plasma Arc Machining (PAM)** is a non-conventional, electro-thermal material removal process in which a high-velocity, constricted jet of ionized gas (the *plasma*) is used to melt and displace the workpiece material. The plasma is generated by passing an inert or semi-inert gas through a high-intensity electric arc struck between a non-consumable tungsten electrode and the conductive workpiece (or between the electrode and a constricting nozzle, in the transferred-arc variant). The process exploits temperatures of the order of **20,000 K to 30,000 K** — well above the melting point of any known engineering material — making it suitable for cutting, profiling, and grooving of all electrically conductive metals regardless of their hardness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorise:**
> *"Plasma Arc Machining is a thermal cutting process that uses a high-velocity jet of ionized plasma (the 4th state of matter) at temperatures of 20,000–30,000 K to melt and remove electrically conductive metals by fusion, with the molten material being swept away by the high-kinetic-energy plasma stream."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning Fire-Hose"

Imagine standing in a torrential rainstorm holding a regular garden hose. The water pressure is high, but the water is cold and harmless. Now imagine swapping that garden hose with a **fire-hose whose water has been superheated to ten times the temperature of the Sun's surface**, and you are spraying it onto a steel wall. The water doesn't just soak the wall — it **vaporizes** the steel on contact and blows the molten droplets away in a bright, hissing stream.

That is essentially what **Plasma Arc Machining** does to a metal workpiece:
- The **"superheated water"** = the ionized gas (plasma) at **≈ 28,000 K**.
- The **"fire-hose nozzle"** = the constricting copper nozzle of the plasma torch.
- The **"steel wall"** = any electrically conductive metal (steel, aluminium, copper, titanium).
- The **"pump creating pressure"** = the power source + gas flow system delivering energy at rates of **6–400 kW**.

The plasma is *not* a flame and *not* a spark — it is the **4th state of matter**: a soup of free electrons, positive ions, and neutral atoms that conducts electricity and behaves almost like a fluid in a magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Plasma"?** The term was coined by **Irving Langmuir (1928)** because the oscillating behaviour of ionized gas reminded him of blood plasma carrying charged corpuscles.

## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| 1955 | First commercial plasma torch patented (Union Carbide / Linde) |
| 1960s | Introduction of transferred-arc plasma for metal cutting |
| 1970s | High-definition (HD) plasma and water-injection plasma introduced |
| 1980s | Integration with CNC gantry tables for profile cutting |
| 2000s+ | **High-Definition Plasma** rivals laser cutting in tolerance on thin sheets |

Today, PAM is the **workhorse of the metal-fabrication industry** for plate thicknesses from **0.5 mm to 150 mm**, especially where Oxy-Fuel cutting fails (stainless steel, aluminium, copper).

## 1.4 Why PAM is Categorised Under Non-Conventional Machining

Unlike conventional machining, PAM does **not** rely on mechanical hardness differential between tool and workpiece. Instead, it relies on **thermal energy density**. Hence it belongs to the family of **Thermal-Type Non-Conventional Machining Processes** along with:
- Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)
- Laser Beam Machining (LBM)
- Electron Beam Machining (EBM)
- Water Jet Machining (WJM)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** PAM + LBM + EBM + EDM = "**PLED**" Thermal NCM Processes.

---

## 1.5 Plasma — The Fourth State of Matter

When sufficient energy is added to a gas, electrons are stripped from their parent atoms, producing a quasi-neutral cloud of **ions, electrons, and neutral particles**. This is the **plasma state**.

$$ \text{Solid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Liquid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat}} \text{Gas} \xrightarrow{\text{Ionization}} \text{Plasma} $$

The degree of ionization $\alpha$ is given by the **Saha Ionization Equation**:

$$ \frac{n_i}{n_n} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{21} \frac{T^{3/2}}{n_i} \, e^{-E_i / k_B T} $$

where $E_i$ is the ionization energy of the gas, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is temperature, and $n_i$, $n_n$ are number densities of ions and neutrals.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recap — What PAM IS and IS NOT**
> - ✅ **IS**: Thermal, uses ionized gas (plasma), cuts any conductive metal.
> - ❌ **IS NOT**: A chemical process, a mechanical process, restricted to ferrous metals, dependent on oxygen combustion.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle

The operating principle of PAM can be broken down into **seven sequential stages**:

1. **Gas Selection & Injection**
   A suitable plasma-forming gas (**Argon, Nitrogen, Hydrogen, or a mixture**) is fed through the torch under controlled pressure (typically **0.5–1.5 MPa**).

2. **Pilot Arc Initiation**
   A low-current **pilot arc** (≈ 15–40 A) is struck between the tungsten electrode and the copper nozzle using a high-frequency (HF) or high-voltage (HV) starting circuit. This ionizes the gas locally.

3. **Plasma Column Formation**
   The pilot arc's heat ionizes the bulk of the incoming gas. The conductive ionized channel constricts due to the **magnetic pinch effect**, producing a narrow, high-energy plasma jet.

4. **Transferred Arc Establishment**
   Once the pilot arc approaches the workpiece (in *transferred-arc mode*), the arc transfers from nozzle-to-electrode to electrode-to-workpiece, dramatically increasing the available energy density at the cut zone.

5. **Material Melting & Vapourization**
   The workpiece material under the plasma jet is heated beyond its melting (and partially its boiling) point. Local temperatures reach **20,000 K to 30,000 K** at the plasma core.

6. **Material Ejection (Kerf Formation)**
   The high-velocity plasma stream (exhaust velocities up to **Mach 3** ≈ 1000 m/s) sweeps the molten metal downward through the kerf, producing the characteristic **dross-free cut** in oxygen-free plasma systems.

7. **Solidification**
   The molten material is ejected as fine droplets that re-solidify as they leave the kerf, with the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** immediately adjacent to the cut edge.

## 2.2 The "WHY" and "HOW" — Thermodynamic Logic

| Stage | WHY? | HOW? |
|-------|------|------|
| Gas injection | Provide ionizable medium | Pressurized gas supply |
| Pilot arc | Initiate ionization without workpiece contact | HF/HV starter, 15–40 A |
| Constriction | Focus energy to small area | Magnetic pinch + nozzle geometry |
| Transferred arc | Maximize energy into workpiece | Workpiece acts as anode |
| Melting | Material removal mechanism | Conduction + convection heat transfer |
| Ejection | Clear kerf, prevent re-solidification | High gas momentum |
| Solidification | Forms HAZ | Residual heat dissipation |

## 2.3 Types of Plasma Arc Machining

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks **comparison questions** between transferred and non-transferred arc.

### 2.3.1 Non-Transferred Arc (NTA) Plasma
- Arc is between **electrode and nozzle only**.
- Workpiece is **not** part of the electrical circuit.
- Lower energy density → used for **welding, surfacing, powder spraying**.
- Operating voltage: **80–200 V**.

### 2.3.2 Transferred Arc (TA) Plasma
- Arc is between **electrode and workpiece**.
- Workpiece is the **anode**, electrode is the **cathode**.
- Very high energy density → used for **cutting and profiling**.
- Operating voltage: **100–400 V**, currents **30–1000 A**.

### 2.3.3 Comparison Table (HIGH-YIELD)

| Parameter | Non-Transferred Arc | Transferred Arc |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------| 
| Arc path | Electrode ↔ Nozzle | Electrode ↔ Workpiece |
| Energy efficiency | **20–40 %** | **60–85 %** |
| Power range | 5–50 kW | 30–400 kW |
| Standoff distance | Fixed (short) | Variable (1–10 mm) |
| Typical use | Welding, spraying | **Cutting**, profiling |
| Cut quality | Low (HAZ large) | High (kerf narrow) |

---

## 2.4 Equipment Architecture

A complete PAM system consists of **six subsystems**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           PLASMA ARC MACHINING SYSTEM                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  1. Power Source (DC Rectifier)                      │
│     • Constant-current, drooping V-I characteristic  │
│     • Open-circuit voltage: 200–400 V                │
│     • Current range: 30–1000 A                       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  2. Plasma Torch (Cutting Head)                      │
│     • Tungsten electrode (cathode, 2 % thoriated)    │
│     • Copper constricting nozzle (anode in NTA)      │
│     • Water-cooled (in high-power systems)           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  3. Gas Supply & Control                             │
│     • Plasma gas (Ar, N2, H2)                        │
│     • Shield gas (CO2, N2) — protects nozzle         │
│     • Pressure regulators, flow meters, solenoids    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  4. Cooling System                                   │
│     • Recirculating chilled water for torch          │
│     • Critical above 100 A to prevent nozzle melt    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  5. Workpiece Handling & Drive                       │
│     • CNC gantry or cantilever table                 │
│     • Traverse speed: 50–8000 mm/min                 │
│     • Vertical height control (THC)                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  6. Control Console                                  │
│     • PLC / CNC with HMI                             │
│     • Arc-voltage feedback (for THC)                 │
│     • Parameter presets per material/thickness       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.4.1 Plasma Torch — Internal Anatomy (Detailed)

The torch is the heart of the system. From top to bottom:

1. **Electrode Holder** — Holds the tungsten electrode; water-cooled.
2. **Tungsten Electrode** — Cathode; typically 2 % thoriated for low electron work function.
3. **Plasma Gas Inlet** — Tangential injection → creates **swirl flow** that constricts and stabilizes the arc.
4. **Swirl Ring / Gas Distributor** — Generates vortex flow pattern.
5. **Copper Constricting Nozzle** — Narrows arc cross-section → raises energy density. Made of OFHC copper; water-cooled.
6. **Shield Gas Cup** — Outer ring supplies shield gas to protect nozzle from oxidation and improve cut quality.
7. **Nozzle-to-Electrode Gap** — Critical dimension (typically 1–3 mm); determines arc voltage and plasma velocity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **plasma gas** with the **shield gas**. The plasma gas forms the cutting jet; the shield gas *only* protects the nozzle and improves cut-edge quality — it does NOT participate in cutting.

---

## 2.5 Plasma-Forming Gases — Roles & Selection

| Gas | Ionization Energy (eV) | Thermal Conductivity | Primary Role |
|-----|------------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Argon (Ar)** | 15.76 | Low | Inert carrier, easy arc start |
| **Nitrogen (N₂)** | 14.53 | Medium | Higher energy, for stainless & mild steel |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | 13.60 | Very High | Adds reducing chemistry, boosts energy |
| **Oxygen (O₂)** | 13.62 | High | Mild steel cutting (oxidizing cut) |
| **Compressed Air** | — | Medium | Low-cost, limited life |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is hydrogen added to argon for cutting stainless steel?"*
> **Answer:** Hydrogen raises the **thermal conductivity** and **arc voltage**, increasing the heat input per unit length. The reducing environment also prevents oxide formation, yielding a clean, oxide-free cut edge.

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you must know for Module-3 PAM numericals.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Material Removal Rate (cutting) | $MRR = v \cdot t \cdot w$ | mm³/min | $v$ = traverse speed (mm/min), $t$ = thickness (mm), $w$ = kerf width (mm) |
| 2 | Material Removal Rate (drilling/piercing) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot f$ | mm³/min | $D$ = hole dia, $f$ = feed (mm/min) |
| 3 | Heat Input per unit length | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/mm or kJ/m | $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current |
| 4 | Power in plasma | $P = V \cdot I$ | W or kW | |
| 5 | Power utilization efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{work}}{V \cdot I}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.5 – 0.7 |
| 6 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Lower is better |
| 7 | Kerf taper angle | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{(w_t - w_b)}{2t}$ | degrees | $w_t$ = top kerf, $w_b$ = bottom kerf |
| 8 | Heat-Affected Zone width (empirical) | $HAZ \propto \dfrac{I^{2}}{v}$ | mm | Higher current & slow speed → wider HAZ |
| 9 | Arc pressure (approx.) | $P_{arc} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I^{2}}{4\pi^{2} r^{2}}$ | Pa | Magnetic pinch pressure |
| 10 | Plasma exit velocity (1-D gas dynamic) | $v_{exit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma R T_0}{(\gamma - 1) M}} \cdot \sqrt{1 - (p_e/p_0)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ | m/s | $T_0$ = stagnation temperature, $M$ = molar mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 1** ($MRR = v \cdot t \cdot w$) is the **single most-tested formula** in KTU exams on PAM — memorize it cold.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship-building yards**: Cutting 30–50 mm steel plates for hull sections.
- **Pressure-vessel fabrication**: Profile cutting of dished ends.
- **Automotive industry**: Prototype body-panel trimming on stainless steel.
- **Aerospace**: Machining titanium and Inconel sheets (where EDM is too slow).
- **Scrap yards**: Cutting heavy structural sections.
- **Job-shop fabricators**: Versatile alternative to laser for thicker plates.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Plasma Cutting

### Problem Statement
Derive the MRR formula for a straight-line plasma cut on a plate of thickness $t$ cut at traverse speed $v$ producing a kerf of width $w$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the volume removed per unit time.**
When the torch moves a distance $dx$ in time $dt$, it removes a slab of material.

$$
\text{Volume of one slab} = w \cdot t \cdot dx
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $dx / dt = v$.**
The traverse speed $v$ relates incremental length to time.

$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dx = v \cdot dt
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the volume equation.**

$$
\text{Volume in time } dt = w \cdot t \cdot v \cdot dt
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate volume with respect to time to obtain MRR.**

$$
MRR = \frac{d(\text{Volume})}{dt} = w \cdot t \cdot v
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{MRR = v \cdot t \cdot w \;\; [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}]}
$$

where
- $v$ = traverse speed in mm/min
- $t$ = plate thickness in mm
- $w$ = kerf width in mm

---

### NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (Fully Worked-Out)

**Question:** A plasma arc cuts a 12 mm thick mild-steel plate at a traverse speed of 1500 mm/min. The measured kerf width is 2.5 mm. Determine:
(a) The Material Removal Rate.
(b) The Specific Energy Consumption if the arc voltage is 110 V and current is 200 A.
(c) The heat input per unit cut length.

**Given:**
- $t = 12$ mm, $\;v = 1500$ mm/min, $\;w = 2.5$ mm
- $V = 110$ V, $\;I = 200$ A

#### (a) MRR Calculation

$$
MRR = v \cdot t \cdot w = 1500 \times 12 \times 2.5
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 45{,}000 \; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min}}
$$

#### (b) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

First compute the arc power:

$$
P = V \cdot I = 110 \times 200 = 22{,}000 \text{ W} = 22 \text{ kW}
$$

Convert MRR to mm³/s:

$$
MRR = \frac{45{,}000}{60} = 750 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Now compute SEC:

$$
SEC = \frac{P}{MRR} = \frac{22{,}000}{750}
$$

$$
\boxed{SEC = 29.33 \text{ J/mm}^{3}}
$$

#### (c) Heat Input per Unit Length

$$
H = \frac{V \cdot I}{v} = \frac{110 \times 200}{1500} = \frac{22{,}000}{1500}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 14.67 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Magnetic Pinch Pressure in a Constricted Arc

The constriction of the arc inside the nozzle produces a radially inward magnetic force. We derive the resulting pressure on the arc core.

**Step 1 — Current-carrying conductor in its own magnetic field.**
For a long cylindrical arc of radius $r$ carrying current $I$ uniformly distributed, the self-induced magnetic field at radius $x$ is:

$$
B(x) = \frac{\mu_0 I_{enc}}{2\pi x}
$$

where $I_{enc} = I \cdot \dfrac{x^{2}}{r^{2}}$ (since current density is uniform).

**Step 2 — Substitute $I_{enc}$:**

$$
B(x) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r^{2}} \cdot x
$$

**Step 3 — Magnetic pressure at radius $x$:**
The radial pressure (Lorentz force per unit volume, integrated) on the plasma column boundary is:

$$
P_{arc} = \frac{1}{2\mu_0}\left(\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\right)^{2} = \frac{\mu_0 I^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{arc} = \frac{\mu_0 I^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}} \;\; [\text{Pa}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation shows that **pinch pressure increases with the square of current** and **inversely with the square of arc radius** — the principle behind why smaller nozzle bores deliver higher energy density.

---

## 3.3 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Effect of Process Parameters on Performance

The following matrix maps every input parameter to the performance outputs a KTU examiner may test.

| Process Parameter ↑ (increases) | MRR | Kerf Width | HAZ | Surface Roughness | Top-Bottom Squareness |
|---------------------------------|-----|------------|-----|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Arc Current (I)** | ↑↑ Strong | ↑ Slight | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ (better) |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | ↑ Moderate | ↑ Moderate | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Traverse Speed (v)** | ↓↓ Strong | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ (taper increases) |
| **Gas Flow Rate (Q)** | ↑ Slight | ↓ (narrower kerf) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Standoff Distance** | ↓ after optimum | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | ↑ Slight | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Shield Gas Pressure** | Slight | Slight | Slight | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Plate Thickness** | ↓ (limited by power) | ↑ (must allow kerf clearance) | Limited | ↓ at high t | ↓ |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the matrix for exam answers:** "As traverse speed *increases*, MRR *decreases* because the heat input per unit length $H = VI/v$ falls below the threshold needed to maintain melting through the plate."

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 3: Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Width — Empirical Correlation

**Given empirical model** (widely accepted):

$$
HAZ = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot \sqrt{t}}
$$

where $k$ is a material-dependent constant (typically $0.05 < k < 0.2$ for steels).

**Derivation logic:**
1. Heat input per unit length: $H = VI/v$.
2. The thermal diffusion depth scales as $\sqrt{\alpha \tau}$ where $\tau$ is interaction time.
3. Interaction time at the cut zone: $\tau \approx w/v$.
4. So penetration depth (and HAZ) $\propto \sqrt{\alpha w / v}$.
5. Combining with $H$, the empirical form above is recovered.

**Example Numerical:**
A 10 mm stainless-steel plate is cut with $V = 130$ V, $I = 250$ A, $v = 1200$ mm/min, $k = 0.12$. Estimate HAZ.

$$
HAZ = \frac{0.12 \times 130 \times 250}{1200 \times \sqrt{10}} = \frac{3900}{1200 \times 3.162} = \frac{3900}{3794.7}
$$

$$
\boxed{HAZ \approx 1.03 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — Comparison with Other NCM Processes

$$
SEC = \frac{\text{Energy supplied}}{\text{Volume removed}} = \frac{P}{MRR}
$$

**Typical SEC values (memorize for comparison questions):**

| Process | Typical SEC (J/mm³) |
|---------|---------------------|
| Oxy-fuel cutting | 5 – 15 |
| **Plasma Arc Machining** | **20 – 50** |
| Laser Beam Machining | 8 – 25 |
| EDM | 50 – 200 |
| AJM | 100 – 400 |
| USM | 50 – 150 |

> [!NOTE]
> PAM has a **moderate SEC** — higher than laser for thin sheets, but far lower than EDM or AJM.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — Effect of Parameter Change

**Problem:**
A fabricator finds that the kerf width in his plasma cut increases from 2.0 mm to 2.8 mm when the current is raised from 200 A to 280 A. Traverse speed remains 1500 mm/min on a 10 mm plate. Find:
(a) The change in MRR.
(b) The change in heat input per unit length.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Initial MRR:
$$
MRR_1 = 1500 \times 10 \times 2.0 = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

Final MRR:
$$
MRR_2 = 1500 \times 10 \times 2.8 = 42{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

Change:
$$
\Delta MRR = 42{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min} \;(\uparrow 40\%)
$$

**Part (b):** Assume voltage scales linearly with current at $V = 80 + 0.15 I$ (typical droop).

$V_1 = 80 + 0.15(200) = 110$ V
$V_2 = 80 + 0.15(280) = 122$ V

$$
H_1 = \frac{110 \times 200}{1500} = 14.67 \text{ J/mm}
$$

$$
H_2 = \frac{122 \times 280}{1500} = 22.77 \text{ J/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H = +8.10 \text{ J/mm} \;(\uparrow 55\%)}
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a PAM System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete functional architecture** of a Plasma Arc Machining system — from input energy to final cut quality.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> B[DC Rectifier Power Source]
    B -->|Constant Current 30 to 1000A| C[Plasma Torch Assembly]
    D[Compressed Gas Cylinders Ar N2 H2] --> E[Gas Pressure Regulators and Flow Meters]
    E -->|Plasma Gas| C
    E -->|Shield Gas| F[Shield Gas Nozzle Cup]
    G[Chilled Water Cooling Unit] -->|Coolant Inlet| C
    C -->|Hot Coolant Return| G
    C -->|Constricted Plasma Jet 28000K| H[Workpiece Anode Plate]
    H -->|Return Current Path| B
    I[CNC Controller with HMI] -->|Arc Voltage Feedback THC| C
    I -->|XY Traverse Commands| J[Servo Driven Gantry Table]
    J -->|Linear Motion| H
    K[Operator Panel] --> I
    H --> L[Cut Plate with Kerf and Dross]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **A → B → C → H** is the **electrical power chain** (DC transferred arc).
- **D → E → C / F** is the **gas distribution chain** (plasma + shield).
- **G ↔ C** is the **thermal management chain** (water cooling loop).
- **I → J → H** is the **motion control chain** (CNC positioning + THC).
- **C → H** is the **material-removal interface** where the plasma jet meets the workpiece.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Plasma Torch (Sectioned View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Electrode Holder Water Cooled] --> A2[Tungsten Cathode 2 percent Thoriated]
    A2 --> A3[Plasma Gas Inlet Tangential Swirl]
    A3 --> A4[Swirl Ring Vortex Generator]
    A4 --> A5[Copper Constricting Nozzle OFHC]
    A5 --> A6[Nozzle to Electrode Gap 1 to 3 mm]
    A6 --> A7[Shield Gas Cup Outer Ring]
    A7 --> A8[Plasma Jet Exits toward Workpiece]
    A8 --> A9[Workpiece Anode Cut Zone]
```

### Annotation of Key Stages
| Stage | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Electrode Holder | Mechanical support + electrical contact |
| Tungsten Cathode | Emits electrons (thermionic emission) |
| Gas Inlet (tangential) | Creates **swirl flow** for arc stabilization |
| Swirl Ring | Distributes gas uniformly around electrode |
| Constricting Nozzle | Geometrically narrows arc column |
| Nozzle-Electrode Gap | Sets arc voltage & plasma velocity |
| Shield Gas Cup | Protects nozzle from oxidation |
| Plasma Jet | Final cutting stream (≈ Mach 3) |
| Workpiece | Anode; receives arc + is melted |

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Sequence of Operations in PAM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Sequence Operator Initiates CNC Program] --> S2[Gas Pre flow for 2 seconds Purge Torch]
    S2 --> S3[Coolant Flow Verification Interlock]
    S3 --> S4[Power Source Turn On Open Circuit Voltage 300V]
    S4 --> S5[HF or HV Pilot Arc Ignition Electrode to Nozzle]
    S5 --> S6[Plasma Gas Ionization Localized]
    S6 --> S7[Torch Approaches Workpiece Standoff 3 to 8 mm]
    S7 --> S8[Arc Transfers to Workpiece Transferred Arc Established]
    S8 --> S9[CNC XY Motion Initiated Cut Starts]
    S9 --> S10[Arc Voltage Monitored Height Control Maintained]
    S10 --> S11{Cut Complete}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Power Off Arc Extinguished]
    S12 --> S13[Gas Post flow for 5 seconds Cool Torch]
    S13 --> S14[System Ready for Next Cut]
    S11 -->|No| S10
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flow mirrors the **OEM start-up sequence** specified in Hypertherm / ESAB / Kjellberg manuals — useful for KTU design questions.

---

## 4.4 Energy & Material Flow Sankey-Style Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Electrical Power 100 percent VI] --> P2[Arc at Workpiece 60 to 85 percent]
    P1 --> P3[Nozzle Heat Loss 10 to 20 percent]
    P1 --> P4[Radiation and Convection Losses 5 to 15 percent]
    P2 --> P5[Material Melting and Vapourization]
    P2 --> P6[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    P5 --> P7[Kerf Removal MRR Output]
    P6 --> P8[Unwanted Thermal Damage]
```

### Energy Budget Insight
| Energy Channel | Typical Share |
|----------------|---------------|
| Useful (melting + ejection) | **60–85 %** |
| Nozzle + cooling losses | 10–20 % |
| Radiation + convection | 5–15 % |

A **well-designed** PAM system has nozzle losses below 15 %; a **poorly tuned** one (worn nozzle, wrong standoff) may lose **> 35 %** to nozzle heating.
