# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define sintering. List the three stages of solid-state sintering with one key feature of each.

**Model Answer:**
Sintering is the thermal process of bonding and densifying a powder compact into a coherent, predominantly solid mass at temperatures *below* the melting point, driven by the reduction in total surface free energy.

Three stages:
1. **Initial stage (0–5% shrinkage):** Neck formation between particles; mechanisms are surface diffusion and evaporation–condensation; pores are still open and interconnected.
2. **Intermediate stage (5–15% shrinkage):** Cylindrical interconnected pore channels shrink; densification via grain-boundary and volume diffusion; significant shrinkage occurs.
3. **Final stage (< 8% porosity):** Pores become isolated and spherical; densification is slow, controlled by volume diffusion along grain boundaries; grain growth may dominate.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **dry pressing** and **cold isostatic pressing (CIP)**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dry Pressing | Cold Isostatic Pressing |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of pressure | Uniaxial | Hydrostatic (equal all directions) |
| Pressure range | 20–200 MPa | 100–400 MPa |
| Mold type | Rigid metal die | Flexible rubber mold |
| Green density | Non-uniform (friction at walls) | Uniform |
| Shape complexity | Low (simple prismatic) | Higher (near-net complex shapes) |
| Cost & cycle | Low | Higher |
| Typical parts | Refractory bricks, ferrite cores | Spark plug insulators, turbine preforms |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Sintering Mechanisms + Hot Compaction

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the thermodynamic driving force of sintering. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **neck-growth model of Kuczynski** for solid-state sintering and derive the relation between neck radius and time for **volume diffusion** as the dominant mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** process with a schematic diagram. Compare HIP with **Hot Pressing** and **Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS)** in terms of pressure, temperature, cycle time, and shape capability.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Driving Force:**
Every powder compact has a large total surface area. Surface atoms have higher free energy than bulk atoms. The total surface energy $E_s = \gamma_s \cdot A_{\text{total}}$ acts as the driving force. As the compact sinters, $A_{\text{total}}$ decreases (replaced by lower-energy grain-boundary surfaces), releasing free energy:

$$\Delta G_{\text{sinter}} = \gamma_s \, dA_{\text{surface}} - \gamma_{gb} \, dA_{gb}$$

Since $\gamma_{gb} < 2\gamma_s$, $\Delta G$ is negative → sintering is spontaneous.

**Laplace pressure** at a curved neck surface produces a chemical-potential gradient that drives vacancy flow from neck to grain boundary (or equivalently, atomic flow from grain boundary to neck).

**Sketch (drawn in exam):** Two spheres of radius $R$ in contact, with a neck of radius $x$ and neck radius of curvature $\rho = x^2/4R$.

**Derivation of $x^4 \propto t$:**
(Provide derivation similar to Section 3.1 of these notes.)

1. Geometric relation: $\rho = x^2/(4R)$ (small neck approximation).
2. Stress at neck: $\sigma \approx -8\gamma_s R / x^2$.
3. Vacancy flux (Fick's law): $J_v = (D_v \sigma \Omega)/(kT \delta)$.
4. Volume conservation: rate of neck growth = volume of material deposited.
5. Integration yields $\boxed{(x/R)^4 = (64 D_v \gamma_s \Omega / 3 kT R^3) \cdot t}$ — the characteristic 4th-power (or 5th-power in some texts) law for volume-diffusion-controlled initial-stage sintering.

**Conclusion:** Higher temperature (larger $D_v$), finer particle size (smaller $R$), and higher surface energy accelerate neck growth.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**HIP Process Description:**
In Hot Isostatic Pressing, the ceramic part (either pre-sintered to closed porosity, or sealed in a hermetic can) is placed in a pressure vessel. The vessel is heated to **1200–2000 °C** while an inert gas (**Ar at 100–200 MPa**) is applied uniformly on all sides. The isostatic pressure drives the elimination of residual porosity by plastic flow, creep, and diffusion. After the cycle, the part is cooled and removed. If the can was used, it is removed by machining or chemical etching. Modern post-sinter HIP can be done **without a can** once the surface pores have sealed.

**Schematic (drawn in exam):** Pressure vessel with heating elements, Ar inlet, part placed centrally.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Hot Pressing | HIP | SPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure | Uniaxial, 20–50 MPa | Isostatic, 100–200 MPa (Ar) | 30–80 MPa, pulsed DC |
| Temperature | 1500–2000 °C | 1200–2000 °C | 1000–1800 °C |
| Cycle time | Several hours | Several hours | Minutes |
| Shape capability | Simple (uniaxial die) | Complex near-net | Simple to moderate |
| Density achievable | >99% | >99.5% (near theoretical) | >99% with fine grains |
| Tooling | Graphite die | None (or sacrificial can) | Graphite die |
| Application | SiC, AlN substrates | Turbine blades, implants | Nanoceramics, FGMs |

**Conclusion:** HIP is the premium process for critical applications requiring maximum density and reliability; SPS is best when nanoscale grain retention and rapid cycle are required; HP is a cost-effective intermediate solution for simpler shapes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Powder Preparation + Consolidation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **mechanical** and **chemical** routes of ceramic powder preparation. Discuss the **sol-gel** process in detail, listing its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a diagram, explain the **slip casting** process. State the relevant equation for cast thickness vs. time, and discuss the variables affecting the cast quality.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Mechanical Routes (top-down):**
- Ball milling, attrition milling, jet milling.
- Break down coarser particles into finer ones.
- Wide particle-size distribution, possible contamination from media.
- Cheap, scalable, suitable for oxides, carbides, nitrides.

**Chemical Routes (bottom-up):**
- Solid-state reaction, precipitation, sol-gel, hydrothermal, spray pyrolysis.
- Build the powder from atomic/molecular precursors.
- Narrow size distribution, high purity, sub-micron to nano scale.
- More expensive, lower throughput.

**Sol-Gel Process in Detail:**
- **Step 1 – Hydrolysis:** Metal alkoxide (e.g., Si(OC₂H₅)₄) reacts with water:
  $$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$
- **Step 2 – Condensation / Polymerisation:** Si–OH groups link to form Si–O–Si bridges, releasing water:
  $$2\text{Si(OH)}_4 \rightarrow (\text{SiO}_2)_x + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- The product is a **gel** — a 3D network of solid with trapped solvent.
- **Step 3 – Drying:** Solvent removed to give **xerogel** (or **aerogel** under supercritical drying).
- **Step 4 – Calcination:** Heated at 500–1000 °C to obtain the final oxide powder (5–100 nm).

**Advantages:**
- Ultra-fine, high-purity, narrow size-distribution powders.
- Lower processing temperatures than solid-state reaction.
- Excellent chemical homogeneity.

**Limitations:**
- High cost of metal alkoxide precursors.
- Long processing time.
- Health hazards of organic solvents.
- Difficult to scale up to tonnes/day.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Slip Casting Process:**
A slip (a stable colloidal suspension of ceramic powder in water with dispersant, binder, and deflocculant) is poured into a porous plaster mold. The plaster absorbs water by capillary suction, causing ceramic particles to deposit against the mold wall as a consolidated layer. Once the desired thickness is reached, excess slip is drained (drain casting) or the mold is inverted (solid casting). The cast piece is trimmed, demolded when dry enough, and then dried and sintered.

**Sketch (drawn in exam):** Plaster mold, slip inside, water being absorbed outward, consolidated layer growing inward.

**Equation for Cast Thickness:**

$$h^2 = \frac{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot k_m \cdot t}{\eta \cdot \alpha}$$

or in simpler form:

$$h^2 = K \cdot t$$

where $K$ depends on mold permeability ($k_m$), slip viscosity ($\eta$), and solids volume fraction.

**Variables Affecting Cast Quality:**

1. **Slip viscosity** – higher viscosity → slower cast, denser layer.
2. **Solids loading** – too low → porous cast; too high → poor flow, defects.
3. **Mold permeability** – higher → faster casting but lower density.
4. **Particle size** – finer → slower casting, denser cast, more shrinkage.
5. **Dispersant type and amount** – affects slip stability; flocculation leads to non-uniform casts.
6. **Drying rate** – must be slow to prevent cracking from differential shrinkage.
7. **pH of slip** – affects zeta-potential and stability.

**Conclusion:** Slip casting is a low-cost, low-pressure process ideal for traditional ceramics (tableware, sanitary ware) and large simple shapes, but is being replaced by CIM and tape casting for high-tech applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Particulate Ceramics questions:**
> 1. **Confusing surface diffusion with grain-boundary diffusion.** Many students say "sintering happens by surface diffusion" — this is wrong; surface diffusion *grows necks but does not densify*. Use the correct mechanism: **volume diffusion + GB diffusion = densification**.
> 2. **Mixing up the sintering stages.** Saying "Stage 3 = grain growth" is incorrect; grain growth occurs throughout but dominates Stage 3 along with slow densification.
> 3. **Forgetting "below melting point."** A huge deduction if you imply the ceramic melts during sintering.
> 4. **Wrong temperature scale.** Always use Kelvin in Arrhenius equations. Celsius gives wrong answers.
> 5. **In slip casting / tape casting questions:** drawing diagrams without labeling **mold, slip, water absorption direction, and consolidated layer** costs easy marks.
> 6. **HIP vs HP confusion:** students forget HIP is *isostatic* (gas) and HP is *uniaxial* (die).
> 7. **Finishing questions:** students write "ceramics cannot be machined" — instant 1–2 mark cut. The correct statement is "ceramics require **diamond-based or non-contact** finishing due to extreme hardness and brittleness."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-glance KTU 2024 revision sheet — particulate ceramics:**

### Powder Preparation
- ☐ Mechanical (top-down) = milling/crushing; chemical (bottom-up) = sol-gel, precipitation, hydrothermal.
- ☐ Fine, narrow-distribution, high-purity, un-agglomerated powders are *non-negotiable* for dense ceramics.
- ☐ Sol-gel → nano powders (5–100 nm); ball mill → micron powders (0.5–50 µm).

### Consolidation
- ☐ Uniaxial pressing = simple shapes; CIP = uniform density; slip casting = traditional pottery; tape casting = thin sheets; CIM = complex small parts; extrusion = long sections.
- ☐ Slip casting follows **$h^2 \propto t$**.
- ☐ CIM feedstock: ~50–60 vol% powder, 40–50 vol% binder; debinding is the slowest step.

### Drying
- ☐ Two stages: constant-rate (surface-controlled) and falling-rate (diffusion-controlled).
- ☐ Cracking occurs when surface tensile stress > wet green strength.
- ☐ Thicker parts need slower drying (∝ 1/L²).

### Sintering
- ☐ Below $T_m$; driven by surface energy reduction.
- ☐ Three stages: Initial (neck), Intermediate (channels), Final (closed pores).
- ☐ Only volume + GB diffusion = densification; surface diffusion + evaporation = non-densifying.
- ☐ Liquid-phase sintering is **3 steps**: rearrangement → solution–precipitation → final solid-state.
- ☐ Densification and grain growth are *competing*; keep grains small.
- ☐ Arrhenius: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$; **use Kelvin**.

### Hot Compaction
- ☐ HP = uniaxial die, simple shapes.
- ☐ HIP = isostatic gas pressure, complex shapes, near-theoretical density.
- ☐ SPS = pulsed DC, rapid, retains nano grains.

### Finishing
- ☐ Always diamond-based or non-contact (USM, laser, EDM for conductive ceramics).
- ☐ Lapping → flatness < 1 µm; polishing → mirror surface.

### Application Areas
- ☐ Electronics (Al₂O₃ substrates), aerospace (Si₃N₄ turbine), biomedical (ZrO₂ implants), cutting tools (Al₂O₃/Si₃N₄/SiAlON), energy (YSZ SOFC), defence (B₄C armor, AlON IR windows).

### KTU 2024 Must-Memorise Formulas
- ☐ $h^2 = K t$ (slip casting)
- ☐ $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ (Arrhenius diffusion)
- ☐ $T_{\text{sinter}} \approx 0.5$–$0.8 \, T_m$ (rule of thumb)
- ☐ Neck-growth law: $(x/R)^n = f(D, \gamma_s, t, T, R)$
- ☐ Shrinkage = $1 - \sqrt[3]{\rho_{\text{green}}/\rho_{\text{sinter}}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain processing of particulate ceramics" in 14 marks, follow the chain **Powder → Consolidation → Drying → Sintering → Hot Compaction → Finishing**, and include at least one equation, one diagram, and one comparison table per topic. This is the structure that consistently scores full marks in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of Composites: Classification of Composites Materials, Reinforcements and Matrix Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Processing of Composites: Classification of Composites Materials, Reinforcements and Matrix Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Material (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, multiphase engineering material composed of two or more distinct constituents — a **reinforcement** (the load-bearing phase) and a **matrix** (the binder/continuous phase) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material system with properties superior to those of the individual constituents acting alone.

The constituents retain their individual identities and are separated by a distinct **interphase** (or interface). The reinforcement provides **strength and stiffness**, while the matrix transfers load to the reinforcement, protects it from environmental damage, and maintains the geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Mixtures — The Heart of Composite Mechanics:**
> The composite is engineered such that the *sum of the parts* delivers properties *greater than the parts themselves* — this is the **synergistic effect** of composites.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a composite material like **reinforced concrete** (RCC):

- **Concrete (Matrix)** → Hard, but brittle. Cracks easily under tension.
- **Steel Rebar (Reinforcement)** → Strong in tension, but buckles alone.
- **RCC (Composite)** → The rebar handles tension; concrete handles compression. Together, they form a system neither can achieve alone.

In the same way, **fiberglass** is glass fibers (strong but brittle) embedded in a polymer resin (tough but weak). Glass provides strength; resin holds everything together and distributes the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Composites are characterized by **Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** and **Weight Fraction ($W_f$)** of the reinforcement, where $V_f + V_m = 1$ (volume fractions of fiber and matrix must sum to unity).

## 1.3 Why Composites Matter in Modern Manufacturing

| Property Driver | Conventional Material | Composite Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | High in metals, low in polymers | Carbon-fiber composites are **5x stronger than steel** at 1/5 the weight |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Steels corrode | Polymer composites resist most chemicals |
| **Fatigue Life** | Aluminum fatigues at $10^6$ cycles | CFRP can exceed $10^7$ cycles |
| **Design Flexibility** | Limited by formability | Layup can be tailored to load paths |

**Real-world applications**: Aircraft fuselage (Boeing 787, Airbus A350 — 50% composites by weight), wind turbine blades, Formula-1 monocoques, marine hulls, sporting goods, and biomedical implants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Composite Materials

Composites are classified using **three primary schemes** that are routinely tested in KTU examinations.

### A. Classification Based on Matrix Material

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked classification** in KTU Module 4 questions.

**1. Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)**
- Matrix: Thermosetting resins (Epoxy, Polyester, Vinylester) or Thermoplastics (PEEK, PPS).
- Reinforcement: Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibers.
- **Use**: Aircraft interiors, automotive panels, boat hulls, sports equipment.
- **Temperature range**: typically up to **200 °C** for thermosets, **300 °C** for high-performance thermoplastics.

**2. Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)**
- Matrix: Aluminum, Titanium, Magnesium, Copper.
- Reinforcement: SiC particles, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ fibers, carbon fibers.
- **Use**: Brake rotors, piston rings, aerospace structural components, engine parts.
- **Temperature range**: up to **600–800 °C** — far superior to PMCs.

**3. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)**
- Matrix: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (alumina), SiC (silicon carbide), $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ (silicon nitride).
- Reinforcement: SiC fibers, carbon fibers.
- **Use**: Jet engine turbine blades, heat shields, rocket nozzles.
- **Advantage**: Retains ceramic properties (high hardness, thermal stability) while overcoming catastrophic brittleness.

### B. Classification Based on Reinforcement Geometry

| Reinforcement Form | Geometry | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Fiber-reinforced** | Continuous or discontinuous fibers | Fiberglass, CFRP, Aramid |
| **Particulate-reinforced** | Equiaxed particles | $\text{SiC}_p / \text{Al}$ (silicon carbide particles in aluminum) |
| **Whisker-reinforced** | Single-crystal short fibers | SiC whiskers, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ whiskers |
| **Laminate / Sandwich** | Stacked layers of differing materials | Plywood, CFRP-Al honeycomb sandwich |
| **Fibrous (Nano)** | Nanotubes, nanofibers | CNT-reinforced polymers |

### C. Classification Based on Reinforcement Type

**1. Continuous Fiber Composites** — Fibers run the entire length of the composite, providing maximum directional strength. Used in aerospace primary structures.

**2. Discontinuous (Short) Fiber Composites** — Chopped fibers randomly oriented. Lower strength but easier to process via injection molding.

**3. Natural Fiber Composites** — Jute, hemp, sisal, bamboo, coir. Emerging sustainable alternatives.

## 2.2 Reinforcement Materials

### 2.2.1 Fiber Reinforcements

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The four primary structural fibers are **Glass, Carbon, Aramid, and Boron**.

| Fiber Type | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E-Glass** | 2.54 | 2400–3500 | 70–75 | Marine, automotive, construction |
| **S-Glass** | 2.48 | 4000–4800 | 85–90 | Aerospace |
| **Carbon (HS)** | 1.78 | 3500–5000 | 230–290 | Aircraft structures |
| **Carbon (HM)** | 1.85 | 2500–4000 | 400–700 | Space, racing |
| **Aramid (Kevlar 49)** | 1.45 | 2700–3600 | 125–135 | Ballistics, ropes |
| **Boron** | 2.60 | 3500–4000 | 380–400 | Military aerospace |
| **Natural (Jute)** | 1.30 | 400–800 | 10–30 | Sustainable panels |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **HS (High Strength) carbon** with **HM (High Modulus) carbon**. HS has higher tensile strength but lower stiffness; HM has higher stiffness but lower tensile strength. Both are carbon fibers — only the processing temperature differs.

### 2.2.2 Particulate Reinforcements

- **Ceramics**: SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, TiC, $\text{B}_4\text{C}$, WC.
- **Metals**: Tungsten, molybdenum particles.
- **Function**: Improve stiffness, hardness, wear resistance, and dimensional stability.
- **Typical size**: **1–50 μm**.

### 2.2.3 Whisker Reinforcements

- Single-crystal short fibers, typically **1–30 μm** diameter, aspect ratio > 10.
- Near-theoretical strength due to near-flawless crystal structure.
- Examples: SiC whiskers, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ whiskers, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ whiskers.

## 2.3 Matrix Materials

### 2.3.1 Polymer Matrices

**Thermosets (irreversibly hardened)**
- **Epoxy**: Excellent adhesion, low shrinkage, high strength. Used in aerospace.
- **Polyester**: Cheap, easy to process. Used in marine and automotive.
- **Vinylester**: Good chemical resistance. Used in chemical tanks.
- **Phenolic**: Fire-resistant. Used in aircraft interiors.

**Thermoplastics (re-meltable)**
- **PEEK, PPS, PEI**: High-performance matrices for advanced composites.
- **Nylon, Polypropylene**: Used in short-fiber injection-molded composites.

### 2.3.2 Metal Matrices

- **Aluminum alloys** (most common) — for automotive and aerospace.
- **Titanium alloys** — high-temperature aerospace.
- **Magnesium alloys** — lightweight structural parts.

### 2.3.3 Ceramic Matrices

- **Glass ceramics**, **Alumina**, **Silicon Carbide**, **Silicon Nitride**.
- Used in ultra-high-temperature environments (turbines, re-entry vehicles).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following equations — they appear in **numerical and derivation questions**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fractions sum to unity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m}$ | Weight fraction from volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal stiffness) | GPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse stiffness) | 1/GPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$ | Strength rule of mixtures (longitudinal) | MPa |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m$ | Density rule of mixtures | g/cm³ |
| 7 | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f \cdot V_f + \nu_m \cdot V_m$ | Poisson's ratio rule of mixtures | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | Strength-to-weight metric | $\dfrac{\text{MPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** In the table above, all absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` or have been eliminated to prevent breakage of the markdown table syntax. Always use the same convention in your own KTU exam answers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rule of mixtures underpins the design of:
- **Aircraft wing spars** (CFRP, $V_f = 0.6$).
- **Helicopter rotor blades** (Glass-Epoxy with $V_f = 0.5$).
- **Pressure vessels** (filament-wound carbon-epoxy).
- **Wind turbine blades** (E-Glass/Epoxy, length > 80 m).

By **tailoring $V_f$**, engineers balance stiffness, strength, weight, and cost to meet specific design constraints (e.g., aeroelastic tailoring in aircraft wings).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$)

### Governing Assumption: Iso-Strain Condition
When a continuous, unidirectional fiber composite is loaded parallel to the fiber direction, both the fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain). This is valid because the fibers and matrix are bonded at the interface and deform together.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the applied load.**
The total load $P_c$ on the composite cross-section is shared between the fiber and matrix:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

where $P_f$ = load carried by fibers and $P_m$ = load carried by matrix.

**Step 2 — Express load in terms of stress and area.**

$$
\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m
$$

Here $A_c$ is the total cross-sectional area, $A_f$ is the area occupied by fibers, and $A_m$ is the area occupied by the matrix.

**Step 3 — Apply the iso-strain condition.**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon
$$

This is the **key physical assumption** that allows the rule of mixtures to be applied.

**Step 4 — Substitute Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$).**

$$
E_c \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_c = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_m
$$

The strain $\varepsilon$ is non-zero and cancels from all three terms.

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ and divide by $A_c$.**

$$
E_c \cdot A_c = E_f \cdot A_f + E_m \cdot A_m
$$

Dividing through by the composite area $A_c$:

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize area ratios as volume fractions.**

For a uniform cross-section, the area fraction equals the volume fraction:

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f \qquad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m}
$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for the longitudinal elastic modulus**. It is a *weighted average* of the fiber and matrix moduli, weighted by their volume fractions.

### Numerical Example
Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa (carbon fiber), $E_m = 3.5$ GPa (epoxy), $V_f = 0.55$.

$$
E_c = (230)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45) = 126.5 + 1.575 = 128.075 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU 3-mark numerical. The carbon fiber contributes **~98%** of the stiffness despite being only 55% of the volume.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus ($E_T$)

### Governing Assumption: Iso-Stress Condition
When the load is applied **perpendicular** to the fibers, both phases experience the **same stress** (iso-stress). The fibers and matrix act as parallel springs in series along the loading direction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Iso-stress condition.**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma
$$

**Step 2 — Total displacement equals sum of fiber and matrix displacements.**

$$
\Delta_c = \Delta_f + \Delta_m
$$

**Step 3 — Express displacement as strain $\times$ length.**

$$
\varepsilon_c \cdot L_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot L_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot L_m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the total length $L_c$.**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot \frac{L_f}{L_c} + \varepsilon_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Length ratios equal volume fractions (in the transverse direction).**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$.**

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $\sigma$ and invert.**

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

### Numerical Example
Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $V_f = 0.55$.

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{230} + \frac{0.45}{3.5} = 0.00239 + 0.12857 = 0.13096
$$

$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.13096} \approx 7.64 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Conceptual Trap:** $E_T$ is **much smaller** than $E_L$ for the same composite. This **anisotropy** is the *defining feature* of fiber composites — they are designed to take load in the fiber direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Density of a Composite

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total mass of the composite.**

$$
M_c = M_f + M_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express mass as density $\times$ volume.**

$$
\rho_c \cdot V_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that volume fractions are defined relative to $V_c$.**

Since $V_f + V_m = V_c$:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m}
$$

### Numerical Example
Given: $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³ (carbon), $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³ (epoxy), $V_f = 0.6$.

$$
\rho_c = (1.8)(0.6) + (1.2)(0.4) = 1.08 + 0.48 = 1.56 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Specific strength of this composite: $128{,}075 / 1.56 \approx 82{,}099 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ — far superior to aluminum ($\approx 55{,}000$) and steel ($\approx 60{,}000$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Weight Fraction from Volume Fraction

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define weight fraction of the fiber.**

$$
W_f = \frac{M_f}{M_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute mass as $\rho \cdot V$.**

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_m = 1 - V_f$.**

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot (1 - V_f)}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_f = \frac{\rho_f \cdot V_f}{\rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot (1 - V_f)}}
$$

### Numerical Example
Given: $\rho_f = 2.54$ g/cm³ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³ (polyester), $V_f = 0.45$.

Numerator: $2.54 \times 0.45 = 1.143$
Denominator: $1.143 + 1.2 \times 0.55 = 1.143 + 0.66 = 1.803$

$$
W_f = \frac{1.143}{1.803} \approx 0.634 = 63.4\%
$$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Longitudinal (Iso-Strain) | Transverse (Iso-Stress) |
|---|---|---|
| **Strain** | $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ | $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m$ |
| **Stress** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$ | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$ |
| **Modulus** | $E_L = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$ | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ |
| **Physical Picture** | Fibers and matrix deform together | Fibers and matrix stressed equally |
| **Magnitude** | $E_L$ is **high** (fiber-dominated) | $E_T$ is **low** (matrix-dominated) |
| **Loading Direction** | Parallel to fibers | Perpendicular to fibers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Whenever the question says "load applied **parallel** to fibers" → use the **direct rule of mixtures**. Whenever the question says "load applied **perpendicular**" or "transverse loading" → use the **inverse rule of mixtures**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEMUT833]`

**Q: Define a composite material. List the three classifications based on the matrix phase with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **composite material** is a heterogeneous engineering material composed of two or more distinct constituents — a **reinforcement phase** (providing strength/stiffness) and a **matrix phase** (binding and protecting the reinforcement) — combined to achieve properties superior to those of the individual constituents.

The three classifications based on matrix phase are:

1. **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)** — e.g., Glass fiber reinforced epoxy used in aircraft interiors.
2. **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** — e.g., $\text{SiC}_p / \text{Al}$ (silicon carbide particles in aluminum matrix) used in automotive brake rotors.
3. **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)** — e.g., $\text{SiC} / \text{SiC}$ (silicon carbide fibers in silicon carbide matrix) used in jet engine turbine blades.

**[3 Marks: Definition 1, Three classifications 1.5, Examples 0.5]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEMUT833]`

**Q: Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymer matrices used in composites. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermosetting Matrix | Thermoplastic Matrix |
|---|---|---|
| **Curing** | Irreversible cross-linking by heat/chemical reaction | Melt-processable, no chemical curing |
| **Re-meltability** | Cannot be re-melted once cured | Can be re-melted and re-processed |
| **Mechanical Properties** | High strength, high modulus, brittle | Lower strength, tougher, more impact-resistant |
| **Processing Methods** | Hand layup, RTM, filament winding, autoclave | Injection molding, thermoforming, compression molding |
| **Recyclability** | Poor — cannot be remelted | Excellent — re-meltable |
| **Examples** | **Epoxy, Polyester, Vinylester, Phenolic** | **PEEK, PPS, Nylon, Polypropylene** |

**Examples of Thermosets:** Epoxy, Polyester.
**Examples of Thermoplastics:** PEEK, Nylon.

**[3 Marks: Tabular differentiation 2, Examples 1]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each. They MUST have an internal choice (either-or). The two 14-mark alternatives are given below as **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEMUT833]`

**Q: Classify composite materials in detail based on (a) matrix phase and (b) reinforcement geometry. Explain the role of reinforcement and matrix in a composite. Discuss the mechanical properties of carbon fiber and E-glass fiber composites with suitable applications.**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Composites (7 Marks)**

**(i) Classification based on Matrix Phase:**

**1. Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC):**
- Matrix: Epoxy, polyester, vinylester, phenolic (thermosets); PEEK, PPS, nylon (thermoplastics).
- Reinforcement: Glass, carbon, aramid fibers.
- Properties: Lightweight, high strength, corrosion-resistant, easy to manufacture.
- Applications: Aircraft interiors, automotive body panels, marine hulls, sporting goods.

**2. Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):**
- Matrix: Aluminum, titanium, magnesium, copper.
- Reinforcement: SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, carbon fibers, TiC particles.
- Properties: High strength at elevated temperatures, good thermal conductivity, wear-resistant.
- Applications: Brake rotors, piston rings, aerospace structural parts, engine components.

**3. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):**
- Matrix: Alumina ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$), silicon carbide (SiC), silicon nitride ($\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$).
- Reinforcement: SiC fibers, carbon fibers.
- Properties: Retains ceramic hardness and high-temperature stability while overcoming brittleness.
- Applications: Jet engine turbine blades, rocket nozzles, heat shields, cutting tools.

**(ii) Classification based on Reinforcement Geometry:**

**1. Fiber-Reinforced Composites:**
- Continuous fiber (long, oriented): Maximum directional strength.
- Discontinuous (short) fiber: Random orientation, easier processing.

**2. Particulate-Reinforced Composites:**
- Equiaxed particles (e.g., SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in Al).
- Improve hardness, wear resistance, dimensional stability.

**3. Whisker-Reinforced Composites:**
- Single-crystal short fibers, high aspect ratio.
- Near-theoretical strength due to flaw-free structure.

**4. Laminate Composites:**
- Stacked layers of different materials (e.g., plywood, CFRP-Al honeycomb).
- Tailored properties along the stacking sequence.

#### **Part (b) — Roles and Mechanical Properties (7 Marks)**

**Role of Reinforcement:**
- Carries the **major portion of the applied load**.
- Provides **strength, stiffness, and rigidity** to the composite.
- Determines the **directional mechanical properties** (anisotropy).

**Role of Matrix:**
- **Binds** the reinforcement together and maintains the geometry.
- **Transfers** the applied load to the reinforcement through the interface.
- **Protects** the reinforcement from environmental damage, abrasion, and chemical attack.
- Provides **transverse strength**, **toughness**, and **shear resistance**.

**Mechanical Properties — Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP):**
- Density: **1.75–1.85 g/cm³**.
- Tensile strength: **3500–5000 MPa** (HS grade), **2500–4000 MPa** (HM grade).
- Young's modulus: **230–290 GPa** (HS), **400–700 GPa** (HM).
- **Applications**: Boeing 787 fuselage, Airbus A350 wing skins, F1 chassis, spacecraft structures.

**Mechanical Properties — E-Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP):**
- Density: **2.50–2.62 g/cm³**.
- Tensile strength: **2400–3500 MPa**.
- Young's modulus: **70–75 GPa**.
- Cost: significantly lower than carbon fiber.
- **Applications**: Wind turbine blades, marine hulls, piping, automotive panels, building structures.

**Comparison**: CFRP offers higher specific strength and stiffness but at higher cost; GFRP offers a more cost-effective solution for less demanding applications.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEMUT833]`

**Q: (a) With a neat sketch, explain the rule of mixtures for the longitudinal elastic modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. A carbon-epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Calculate $E_L$ and $E_T$ and the density of the composite given $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³.**

**(b) Describe in detail the various types of reinforcements used in composite materials with their characteristic properties and applications.**

#### **Part (a) — Rule of Mixtures and Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Derivation of $E_L$ (Longitudinal Modulus):**
- **Assumption**: Iso-strain (fibers and matrix deform equally when load is parallel to fibers).
- Load sharing: $P_c = P_f + P_m$
- $\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$
- Hooke's Law: $E_c \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_c = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_m$
- Cancel $\varepsilon$, divide by $A_c$:

$$
E_c = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m
$$

**Derivation of $E_T$ (Transverse Modulus):**
- **Assumption**: Iso-stress (equal stress in fibers and matrix when load is perpendicular).
- Strain compatibility: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m$
- Applying $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ and inverting:

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$
E_L = (230)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_L = 126.5 + 1.575 = \boxed{128.075 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**Step 2 — Transverse Modulus:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{230} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.002391 + 0.128571 = 0.130963
$$

$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.130963} \approx \boxed{7.64 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**Step 3 — Composite Density:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

$$
\rho_c = (1.8)(0.55) + (1.2)(0.45) = 0.99 + 0.54 = \boxed{1.53 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Reinforcements in Detail (7 Marks)**

**1. Glass Fibers:**
- Types: E-Glass (electrical, general purpose), S-Glass (high strength), C-Glass (chemical resistance).
- Properties: Tensile strength 2400–4800 MPa, density 2.5–2.6 g/cm³, low cost.
- Applications: Marine hulls, automotive panels, wind turbine blades, building panels.

**2. Carbon Fibers:**
- Types: HS (high strength), HM (high modulus), UHM (ultra-high modulus).
- Properties: Tensile strength 2500–5000 MPa, modulus 230–700 GPa, density 1.75–1.85 g/cm³.
- Applications: Aircraft primary structures, spacecraft, F1 cars, high-end sports equipment.

**3. Aramid Fibers (Kevlar 49):**
- Properties: Tensile strength 2700–3600 MPa, modulus 125–135 GPa, density 1.45 g/cm³.
- Excellent impact and ballistic resistance.
- Applications: Body armor, helicopter rotor blades, ropes, pressure vessels.

**4. Boron Fibers:**
- Properties: Tensile strength 3500–4000 MPa, modulus 380–400 GPa, density 2.6 g/cm³.
- Applications: Military aircraft, structural stiffeners.

**5. Ceramic Fibers (SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$):**
- Properties: High-temperature stability, oxidation resistance.
- Applications: CMC reinforcement, turbine engines, rocket nozzles.

**6. Natural Fibers (Jute, Hemp, Sisal, Coir, Bamboo):**
- Properties: Low density (1.2–1.5 g/cm³), biodegradable, low cost, moderate strength.
- Applications: Automotive door panels, sustainable packaging, low-cost housing.

**7. Particulate Reinforcements:**
- Materials: SiC, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, TiC, $\text{B}_4\text{C}$, WC.
- Size: 1–50 μm.
- Applications: MMC for wear resistance, brake pads, cutting tools.

**8. Whisker Reinforcements:**
- Materials: SiC whiskers, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ whiskers.
- Properties: Near-theoretical tensile strength (> 7 GPa), aspect ratio > 10.
- Applications: Cutting tool inserts, high-strength MMCs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deduction in KTU OEMUT833 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HS and HM carbon fibers** — HS = High Strength, HM = High Modulus. These are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting $V_f + V_m = 1$** in numerical problems. Always state this explicitly before substituting.
> 3. **Applying the wrong rule of mixtures** — longitudinal loading → direct rule; transverse loading → inverse rule.
> 4. **Missing units** in final numerical answers. Always write GPa for modulus, MPa for stress, g/cm³ for density.
> 5. **Not mentioning the iso-strain or iso-stress assumption** in derivation questions. This is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing CMC with MMC** in application questions. CMC = Ceramic Matrix (turbines, heat shields); MMC = Metal Matrix (brake rotors, pistons).
> 7. **Skipping the role of the matrix** in descriptive questions. The matrix is NOT just a binder — it transfers load, protects reinforcement, and provides toughness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU OEMUT833 Module 4**

### **Core Definitions**
- Composite = Reinforcement + Matrix (heterogeneous, synergistic).
- $V_f + V_m = 1$ (volume fractions always sum to unity).

### **Three Classification Schemes**
- **By Matrix**: PMC, MMC, CMC.
- **By Reinforcement Geometry**: Fiber, Particulate, Whisker, Laminate.
- **By Reinforcement Type**: Continuous, Discontinuous, Natural.

### **The Four Primary Reinforcement Fibers (Most Important)**
- **E-Glass** — cheap, general purpose (marine, wind).
- **Carbon (HS)** — high tensile strength (aircraft primary structure).
- **Carbon (HM)** — high stiffness (space, racing).
- **Aramid (Kevlar)** — impact/ballistic resistance (body armor).

### **Matrix Families**
- **Thermosets**: Epoxy (most common), Polyester, Vinylester, Phenolic.
- **Thermoplastics**: PEEK (high-performance), Nylon, PPS.
- **Metals**: Al, Ti, Mg alloys.
- **Ceramics**: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, SiC, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$.

### **The Two Critical Equations (Must Memorize)**
- $E_L = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$ (iso-strain, longitudinal).
- $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ (iso-stress, transverse).

### **Numerical Problem Pattern (Always Asked)**
1. State given data and the constraint $V_f + V_m = 1$.
2. Identify loading direction (parallel → $E_L$, perpendicular → $E_T$).
3. Substitute into the appropriate rule of mixtures.
4. Compute and quote the answer with correct units (GPa, MPa, g/cm³).
5. Optionally compute density and specific strength.

### **Key Anisotropy Insight**
- $E_L \gg E_T$ for the same composite.
- Fibers dominate longitudinal properties; matrix dominates transverse.
- This is why composites are designed and laid up along the load path.

### **Synergistic Effect**
- Composite properties $\neq$ simple addition.
- Carbon + Epoxy → strong, stiff, lightweight, corrosion-resistant (neither has all four).
- This synergy is the *fundamental reason* for using composites.
---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) - Processing of PMCs: Sheet Molding Compound, Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT833 - ADVANCED MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) - Processing of PMCs: Sheet Molding Compound, Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs) — Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (OEMUT833, Module 4):**
> A **Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC)** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase engineering material formed by the synergistic combination of a **polymer matrix** (thermoset or thermoplastic) acting as the continuous phase that binds, protects, and transfers load, and a **reinforcement phase** (fibers or particulates) that primarily carries the structural load. The matrix shields the reinforcement from environmental degradation, maintains fiber alignment, and provides shape and surface finish.

The three sub-families of PMCs, classified by reinforcement geometry, are:
- **FRP — Fiber Reinforced Polymers** (continuous or chopped fibers)
- **PRC — Particulate Reinforced Composites** (filled polymers, e.g., CaCO₃/PP)
- **SMC/BMC — Sheet Molding / Bulk Molding Compounds** (pre-impregnated, compression-moldable intermediates)

## 1.2 Sheet Molding Compound (SMC) — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMC** is a **ready-to-mold, fiber-reinforced, thermosetting sheet** — a soft, tacky, dough-like mat of chopped glass/carbon fibers (≈ 12–50 mm length) pre-impregnated with a thickened resin paste (typically unsaturated polyester + styrene + fillers + thickener + catalyst).

### 🍕 The Pizza Analogy
Think of SMC as a **frozen pizza base** that you (the manufacturer) bake into the desired shape:
- **Pizza dough** → thickened resin paste (carries the cheese and toppings, holds shape when heated)
- **Mozzarella cheese** → chopped glass/carbon fibers (the structural load-bearers)
- **Toppings** → CaCO₃ filler, release agent, catalyst, pigment
- **Baking in an oven** → compression molding at **140–160 °C** under **5–10 MPa** for **60–180 s**
- The result is a rigid, dimensionally stable **composite part** in a single rapid press cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SMC composition (typical):**
> - Resin (polyester/vinyl ester): **30–35 %** by weight
> - Chopped glass fiber: **25–35 %** by weight
> - Filler (CaCO₃): **30–40 %** by weight
> - Thickener (MgO / Mg(OH)₂): **1–2 %**
> - Catalyst (peroxide): **0.5–1.5 %**
> - Release agent (zinc stearate): **1–3 %**

## 1.3 Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFRP** is a PMC in which the reinforcement is **carbon fiber** (typically PAN-based, 5–15 µm diameter) embedded in a polymer matrix (epoxy is dominant; high-temperature thermoplastics like **PEEK, PEI, PPS** are used for aerospace thermoplastic CFRP / CFRTP).

### 🏗️ The Reinforced Concrete Analogy (with an upgrade)
- **CFRP = Reinforced concrete on a high-performance diet.**
- Carbon fiber is to polymer what **steel rebar** is to concrete — but carbon fiber is **5× lighter, 4–10× stronger (per unit weight), and rust-proof**.
- Epoxy plays the role of **cement paste** — bonding the fibers, distributing load, and protecting them from impact and moisture.
- Because carbon fibers are *electrically conductive* and *anisotropic*, CFRP is used in **aircraft skins, Formula-1 chassis, wind turbine blades, spacecraft, and high-end sports gear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard CFRP property envelope:**
> - Density: **1.5–1.6 g/cm³** (vs. steel ≈ 7.85 g/cm³)
> - Tensile strength: **3.5–7.0 GPa**
> - Young's modulus: **230–700 GPa**
> - Coefficient of thermal expansion: **−1 to +2 × 10⁻⁶ /K** (can be **near-zero** with proper ply orientation)
> - Operating temperature: **up to 300 °C** (thermoset epoxy) / **up to 250 °C** (thermoplastic PEEK matrix)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

| Sub-topic | Typical KTU Question Weight | Frequently Asked |
|---|---|---|
| SMC composition & stages | 7–10 marks | Yes (every year) |
| Compression molding of SMC | 7 marks | Yes |
| CFRP / prepreg layup | 7–10 marks | Yes |
| Autoclave processing | 7 marks | Yes |
| Rule of mixtures / Vf calculation | 7 marks | Yes |
| Filament winding / pultrusion / RTM | 5–7 marks | Often |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Processing of Sheet Molding Compound (SMC) — Stepwise Logic

The SMC manufacturing route is a **two-stage** process: (A) making the SMC sheet on a dedicated line, and (B) converting the sheet into a finished part by compression molding.

### Stage A — SMC Sheet Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **heart of SMC production is the SMC machine** — a continuous belt-fed impregnation line.

| Step | Operation | Purpose | Typical Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resin paste preparation** in a high-shear mixer | Homogenize resin + filler + catalyst + thickener + release agent | Viscosity ≈ 2–5 Pa·s |
| 2 | **Doctor-box deposition** of paste on two carrier films (PE/PVC) | Form two resin films, each ≈ 0.5–2 mm thick | Line speed ≈ 5–15 m/min |
| 3 | **Fiber chopping & deposition** | Chop roving into 12–50 mm strands; drop onto the bottom resin film | Fiber length: **25 mm** standard; **50 mm** for structural SMC |
| 4 | **Sandwiching** with the top resin film | Encapsulate fibers in resin paste | — |
| 5 | **Impregnation & compaction** by calender/squeeze rolls | Drive paste into the fiber bundle, remove air | Compaction load ≈ 5–15 kN/m |
| 6 | **Thickening maturation** (storage 24–72 h at 25–35 °C) | MgO/Mg(OH)₂ reacts with polyester COOH groups → viscosity rises to **50–200 Pa·s** | Maturation: **3 days** |
| 7 | **Rolling & packaging** | Roll onto drums, store in cold room (≤ 5 °C) to retard cure | Shelf life: **1–3 months** |

### Stage B — Compression Molding of SMC

| Step | Operation | Purpose | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Charge cutting & stacking** | Cut SMC sheets to **30–50 %** of part area; stack plies for thickness | Charge-to-part area ratio: **1.2–1.5** |
| 2 | **Charge placement** in heated die | Position manually or robotically | Mold T ≈ **140–160 °C** |
| 3 | **Press closing & flow** | SMC flows to fill cavity; this is a **thermoset flow + cure** problem | Closing speed ≈ 100–500 mm/s |
| 4 | **Cure under pressure** | Crosslink the polyester/styrene network | P ≈ **5–10 MPa**; t ≈ **60–180 s** |
| 5 | **Demolding & post-cure** (optional) | Part ejected; full Tg reached after optional oven post-cure | Post-cure 80–120 °C for 2–4 h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does viscosity matter twice?**
> 1. During **maturation**, the SMC must become **handleable (non-tacky)** but still moldable → MgO thickens it.
> 2. During **molding**, the SMC must **flow** to fill the cavity *before* it gels → heat-activated catalyst (BPO/t-BPB) provides a **3–5 min working time** at mold T.

## 2.2 Processing of Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CFRP processing is a **family of routes** because the carbon fiber can be supplied in *dry* form (tow, fabric, mat) or *pre-impregnated* (prepreg). The matrix can be **thermoset** (epoxy — dominant in aerospace) or **thermoplastic** (PEEK, PEI — emerging for high-rate and recyclable parts).

### 2.2.1 Prepreg Layup + Autoclave (Gold Standard)

- **Prepreg** = carbon fabric/tow pre-impregnated with **B-staged epoxy** (partially cured; tacky; stored at **−18 °C**, shelf life 6–12 months).
- **Layup** = stacking plies at prescribed orientations, e.g., **[0/45/−45/90]ₛ** quasi-isotropic layup.
- **Vacuum bagging** = sealing the layup under a polymer film (nylon/PE) with breather/bleeder to extract volatiles and air.
- **Autoclave curing** = heated pressure vessel that provides **uniform heat + high pressure** simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical autoclave cycle for 350 °F / 177 °C cure epoxy prepreg:**
> - Apply vacuum: **−1 bar** (full vacuum) from start
> - Heat-up ramp: **1–3 °C/min** to **135 °C**; hold (dwell) for **30–60 min** to let resin flow and volatiles escape
> - Apply autoclave pressure: **7 bar (100 psi)** after reaching 100 °C
> - Second ramp: **135 → 177 °C** at 1–2 °C/min
> - Cure dwell: **177 °C × 2 h**
> - Cool down: **2 °C/min** to 60 °C; release pressure below 100 °C
> - Result: **Tg ≈ 180 °C**; void content **< 1 %**; fiber Vf ≈ **60–65 %**

### 2.2.2 Alternative CFRP Manufacturing Routes

| Route | Resin | Fiber Form | Tonnage | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand layup + room-T cure** | Polyester / vinyl ester | Woven fabric | Low | Boat hulls, prototypes |
| **Vacuum Bagging (VBO)** | Epoxy | Woven / unidirectional | Low–Med | Motorsport panels |
| **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** | Epoxy / vinyl ester | Preformed dry fabric in closed mold | Med–High | Automotive roof, chassis |
| **Vacuum Infusion (VARTM / SCRIMP)** | Epoxy | Dry fabric + flow media | Med | Wind turbine blades, boat hulls |
| **Filament Winding** | Epoxy | Continuous tow | Med–High | Pressure vessels, pipes, rocket motor casings |
| **Pultrusion** | Polyester / epoxy | Continuous roving | High | Rods, beams, structural profiles |
| **Compression Molding (CFRTP)** | PEEK / PPS thermoplastic | Chopped / long fiber | Very High | Aerospace clips, brackets (10⁶ parts/yr) |
| **Automated Fiber Placement (AFP)** | Prepreg tow | Slit prepreg tape | Med | Wing skins, fuselage barrels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why autoclave?**
> Applied pressure **suppresses void growth** (critical for fatigue) and **compacts the laminate** to achieve **Vf > 60 %** — unattainable with bag-only layup. The combination **pressure + heat + vacuum** is the only one that simultaneously (a) evacuates volatiles, (b) compacts fibers, and (c) drives cure to completion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **exam-critical**. Memorize both the *symbol* and the *units* — KTU examiners penalize missing units.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | Density by rule of mixtures (RoM) | g/cm³ |
| 2 | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | Volume balance (Vf, Vm, Vv) | — |
| 3 | $W_f + W_m + W_v = 1$ | Weight balance | — |
| 4 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | Fiber volume fraction from weight fractions | dimensionless |
| 5 | $W_f = \dfrac{V_f \rho_f}{V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$ | Weight fraction from volume fractions | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (RoM, iso-strain) | GPa |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse modulus (RoM, iso-stress) | GPa |
| 8 | $E_c = \dfrac{3}{8} E_L + \dfrac{5}{8} E_T$ | Modulus of a 2D-random (chopped) composite | GPa |
| 9 | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{me}}{\rho_{th}}$ | Void content from densities | dimensionless |
| 10 | $A_{charge} = A_{part} \times \dfrac{t_{part}}{t_{SMC}}$ | SMC charge area from mass balance | cm² |
| 11 | $\eta(T) = \eta_\infty \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Resin viscosity (Arrhenius) | Pa·s |
| 12 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)^n$ | Cure kinetics (nth order) | s⁻¹ |
| 13 | $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | Theoretical density (no voids) | g/cm³ |
| 14 | $\sigma_{L,ult} = \sigma_{f,ult} V_f + \sigma_{m,ult} V_m$ | Ultimate tensile strength (RoM) | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Using *weight fraction* in the longitudinal modulus formula. The RoM for modulus **always** uses **volume fractions**. Convert $W_f \rightarrow V_f$ first using equation (4).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** ~50 % CFRP by mass → uses **E_L** and **σ_L,ult** with Vf ≈ 0.6.
- **Automotive (BMW 7-series, Tesla Model S):** CFRP life-module and CFRTP compression-molded parts → use **E_c** for chopped-fiber parts.
- **Wind turbines (10+ MW blades):** Glass/carbon hybrid SMC + epoxy infusion → designers solve for the **charge area** using Eq. (10) to size the press.
- **Quality control:** ASTM D2734-94 specifies the **void content** test using Eq. (9) — accepted in every KTU-affiliated lab.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every derivation below is **fully expanded** — no algebraic jumps. Each step has its own explanatory sentence so that you can re-derive in the exam hall.

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Density (Eq. 1)

**Statement:** For a composite with no voids, the density is the volume-weighted average of fiber and matrix densities.

### Step 1 — Define the volume balance

We start with a control volume of composite of total volume $V_c$ and total mass $m_c$. The composite is split into fiber and matrix sub-volumes such that

$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

The volume fractions are defined as

$$V_f = \frac{V_f}{V_c}, \qquad V_m = \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Therefore the volume balance simplifies to

$$V_f + V_m = 1$$

### Step 2 — Define the mass balance

The total mass is the sum of the two sub-masses

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

Using the definition of density ($m = \rho V$), we can write

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the volume fractions

Dividing both sides of the mass equation by $V_c$, we obtain

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Recognising the volume fractions from Step 1, this becomes

$$\boxed{\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **iso-strain (Voigt)** form — valid along the fiber direction or for a perfectly aligned, fully-bonded composite.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fiber Volume Fraction from Weight Fractions (Eq. 4)

**Goal:** Express $V_f$ in terms of the experimentally measured weight fractions $W_f$ and $W_m$.

### Step 1 — Express masses using weights and densities

The mass of the fiber component is

$$m_f = \rho_f V_f$$

The mass of the matrix component is

$$m_m = \rho_m V_m$$

### Step 2 — Form the ratio of weights

By definition, the weight fraction of the fiber is

$$W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_f + m_m} = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce $V_m = 1 - V_f$

Substitute $V_m = 1 - V_f$ (assuming void-free composite) into the denominator

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply out the denominator

$$W_f \bigl[\rho_f V_f + \rho_m - \rho_m V_f\bigr] = \rho_f V_f$$

$$W_f \rho_m + W_f V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) = \rho_f V_f$$

### Step 5 — Collect the $V_f$ terms on one side

$$W_f \rho_m = \rho_f V_f - W_f V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) = V_f \bigl[\rho_f - W_f (\rho_f - \rho_m)\bigr]$$

$$W_f \rho_m = V_f \bigl[\rho_f (1 - W_f) + W_f \rho_m\bigr]$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $V_f$

$$V_f = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{W_f \rho_m + (1 - W_f)\rho_f} = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + (1 - W_f)/\rho_m}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\rho_m / \rho_f$ (or alternatively dividing both by $\rho_f \rho_m$) gives the cleaner symmetric form

$$\boxed{V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The trick is to **multiply top and bottom by $1/(\rho_f \rho_m)$** to expose the symmetric structure. This is the form KTU examiners expect.

## 3.3 Derivation: Longitudinal Modulus by Iso-Strain (Eq. 6)

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (Voigt model — valid for unidirectional, continuous-fiber composites loaded along the fiber axis).

### Step 1 — Write stress in each phase

The stress in the fiber (Hooke's law) is

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon$$

The stress in the matrix is

$$\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$$

### Step 2 — Compute the average stress

By definition, the average stress in the composite is

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the stress expressions

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m = \varepsilon (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

### Step 4 — Identify the composite modulus

By definition $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon$, so

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** Because both phases strain together, the **stiffer phase (carbon, $E_f \approx 230$ GPa)** carries most of the load. Carbon fibers dominate the longitudinal stiffness.

## 3.4 Derivation: Transverse Modulus by Iso-Stress (Eq. 7)

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ (Reuss model — valid for load applied perpendicular to the fibers).

### Step 1 — Write strain in each phase

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma}{E_f}, \qquad \varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma}{E_m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the average strain

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m = \frac{\sigma V_f}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma V_m}{E_m}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the composite modulus

Using $\sigma = E_T \varepsilon_c$, we obtain

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transverse modulus is **much lower** than $E_L$ because the load passes mostly through the soft matrix. For CFRP, $E_T \approx 1/10$ of $E_L$ — this anisotropy is fundamental.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2018-Style 7-mark Problem

> **Problem.** A unidirectional carbon/epoxy lamina has the following composition by weight: carbon fiber 65 %, epoxy matrix 35 %. Given $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, compute:
> (a) Fiber volume fraction $V_f$
> (b) Matrix volume fraction $V_m$
> (c) Composite density $\rho_c$
> (d) Longitudinal modulus $E_L$ (use $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa)
> (e) Transverse modulus $E_T$

### Solution (a) — Fiber volume fraction

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \frac{0.65 / 1.80}{0.65 / 1.80 + 0.35 / 1.20}$$

Compute the numerator and denominator separately

$$N = 0.65 / 1.80 = 0.3611 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}, \qquad D_1 = 0.35 / 1.20 = 0.2917 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$V_f = \frac{0.3611}{0.3611 + 0.2917} = \frac{0.3611}{0.6528} = \boxed{0.5532 \;\;(55.32\,\%)}$$

### Solution (b) — Matrix volume fraction

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.5532 = \boxed{0.4468 \;\;(44.68\,\%)}$$

### Solution (c) — Composite density

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.5532)(1.80) + (0.4468)(1.20)$$

$$= 0.9958 + 0.5362 = \boxed{1.532 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

### Solution (d) — Longitudinal modulus

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (230)(0.5532) + (3.5)(0.4468)$$

$$= 127.24 + 1.56 = \boxed{128.8 \text{ GPa}}$$

### Solution (e) — Transverse modulus

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.5532}{230} + \frac{0.4468}{3.5}$$

$$= 0.002405 + 0.12766 = 0.13007 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_T = \frac{1}{0.13007} = \boxed{7.69 \text{ GPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $E_L / E_T \approx 16.7$ — the classic CFRP anisotropy. A "0°" coupon is **17×** stiffer than a "90°" coupon made of the same material.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — SMC Charge Area Problem

> **Problem.** A SMC part of area $1200 \text{ cm}^2$ and thickness $3.5 \text{ mm}$ is to be produced. SMC sheet is supplied at $2.5 \text{ mm}$ thickness. The charge must cover only 45 % of the part area because multiple plies are stacked. Compute (a) number of plies, (b) charge area, (c) charge mass, given SMC density $\rho_{SMC} = 1.85$ g/cm³.

### (a) Number of plies

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{t_{part}}{t_{SMC}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3.5}{2.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.4 \rceil = \boxed{2 \text{ plies}}$$

(Always round **up** to ensure the part is not short-shot.)

### (b) Charge area

$$A_{charge} = A_{part} \times \frac{t_{part}}{t_{SMC}} = 1200 \times \frac{3.5}{2.5} = 1200 \times 1.4 = 1680 \text{ cm}^2$$

But we must stack **2 plies**, each of charge area

$$A_{charge,per\ ply} = A_{part} \times 0.45 = 1200 \times 0.45 = 540 \text{ cm}^2$$

Total stacked charge area = $2 \times 540 = \boxed{1080 \text{ cm}^2}$ of cut SMC.

### (c) Charge mass

$$m_{charge} = A_{charge,total} \times t_{SMC} \times \rho_{SMC} = 1080 \times 0.25 \times 1.85 = \boxed{499.5 \text{ g}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following two flow diagrams summarize the complete processing routes for **SMC** and **CFRP**. They are drawn using the Mermaid block-level architecture style required by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

## 4.1 SMC Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Material Input] --> nodeB[Resin Paste Mixer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Doctor Box 1 - Bottom Film]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Doctor Box 2 - Top Film]
    nodeE[Glass Roving Creel] --> nodeF[Fiber Chopper]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Fiber Deposition Zone]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Impregnation Compaction Rolls]
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Maturation Room 25 to 35 C - 48 to 72 h]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Slitting and Rolling Drum]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Cold Storage at less than 5 C]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Charge Cutting and Stacking]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Heated Compression Mold 140 to 160 C 5 to 10 MPa]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Demolding and Post Cure]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Final SMC Composite Part]
    nodeP[Quality Tests - Vf Void Content Barcol] --> nodeO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart for an exam answer:**
> 1. The **left cluster** is *raw materials*; the **middle cluster** is *sheet making*; the **right cluster** is *part making*.
> 2. The **thickening maturation step (nodeI)** is the *signature* of SMC chemistry — no other composite intermediate uses MgO-thickened polyester.
> 3. The **compression mold (nodeM)** performs flow + cure *simultaneously* — this is what makes SMC a "high-rate" process (cycle times < 3 min).

## 4.2 CFRP Prepreg + Autoclave — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Carbon Fiber Tow from Spool] --> nodeB[Resin Hot Melt Bath or Solution Dip]
    nodeB --> nodeC[B-Stage Prepreg Takeup Roll]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Cold Storage at minus 18 C]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Layup on Tool - Ply Orientation Design]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Vacuum Bag Sealing - Nylon Film Breather Bleeder]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Autoclave Loading]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Vacuum Application minus 1 bar]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Heat Ramp 1 to 3 C per min to 135 C Dwell]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Apply Pressure 7 bar]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Second Ramp to 177 C Cure Dwell 2 h]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Cool Down below 100 C Release Pressure]
    nodeL --> nodeM[De-bag and Demold]
    nodeM --> nodeN[NDT Inspection - UT C-Scan]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Trimming and Machining]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Finished CFRP Aerospace Part]
    nodeQ[Defect Logging - Voids Delamination Porosity] --> nodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key control variables (highlight for 14-mark answers):**
> 1. **Vacuum** before heat-up → removes entrapped air and volatiles.
> 2. **First dwell at 135 °C** → resin viscosity drops to a minimum (~ 1 Pa·s), giving time for **inter-ply coalescence** and **bubble migration** to the bleeder.
> 3. **Apply 7 bar pressure only after 100 °C** → prevents resin from being squeezed out of the bleeder.
> 4. **Cool below 100 °C before pressure release** → avoids **porosity re-formation** due to thermal expansion mismatch.

## 4.3 Comparison Architecture — SMC vs CFRP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sheet Molding Compound SMC] --> nodeB[Thermoset Polyester Matrix]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Chopped Fiber 12 to 50 mm]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Compression Molding 5 to 10 MPa]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Cycle Time 1 to 3 min]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Production 10 to the 4 parts per year]

    nodeG[Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer CFRP] --> nodeH[Epoxy or PEEK Matrix]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Continuous Carbon Fiber Tow or Fabric]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Autoclave or RTM]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Cycle Time 4 to 12 h]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Production 10 to the 2 parts per year high value]

    nodeA -.contrasts with.-> nodeG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam pearl:** SMC = **high volume, low performance, low cost**; CFRP = **low volume, ultra-high performance, very high cost**. They are **complementary**, not competing.
