# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define ceramic matrix composites. List and briefly state the purpose of any three processing routes used for CMCs. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Ceramic Matrix Composite (CMC) is a multi-phase material in which a **ceramic matrix** (e.g., Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄) is reinforced with **ceramic fibres, whiskers, or particles** (e.g., C, SiC, BN, Al₂O₃) to obtain a combination of high-temperature strength, hardness, and improved **fracture toughness** compared with monolithic ceramics.

**Three Processing Routes (2 Marks):**

1. **Cold Pressing + Sintering** — Powder consolidation at room temperature followed by high-temperature diffusion bonding; economical, suitable for simple shapes.
2. **Hot Pressing (HP/HIP)** — Simultaneous application of heat and pressure; yields near-theoretical density (> 99 %) at lower temperatures.
3. **Reaction Bonding (RBSN/RBSC)** — In-situ chemical reaction forms the matrix; gives **near-net-shape** capability with negligible shrinkage.

---

### Q2. What is the driving force for sintering of ceramic powders? Write the expression and explain how particle size affects it. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Driving Force (1.5 Marks):** The driving force for sintering is the **reduction in total surface free energy** of the powder compact. At the atomic level, this manifests as a **Laplace / capillary pressure** at curved particle-neck surfaces:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sv}}{r}
$$

where $\gamma_{sv}$ is the solid-vapour surface energy (~ 1–2 J/m²) and $r$ is the particle radius.

**Particle-Size Effect (1.5 Marks):** Because $\Delta P \propto 1/r$, **smaller particles generate a much higher driving force**. Halving the particle size doubles the capillary pressure, accelerating densification by orders of magnitude. This is the metallurgical reason why nano-powders (sub-100 nm) can be sintered at 500–800 °C lower temperatures than conventional micron-scale powders.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Mark Distribution:** Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice — **exactly ONE question** to be attempted. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Sub-parts may contain *part (i)* subdivisions.

---

### QUESTION A

**(a)** With the help of a schematic flow diagram, describe the **cold pressing and sintering route** for fabricating ceramic matrix composites. Mention the role of sintering aids. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Steps (with brief explanation — 5 Marks):**

1. **Powder preparation** — Matrix powder (e.g., α-Al₂O₃) is attrition-milled to ≈ 1 µm; reinforcement (SiC whiskers, 5–20 vol %) is added.
2. **Wet mixing** — Ball milling in alcohol with a dispersant to break agglomerates and achieve homogeneous dispersion of reinforcement.
3. **Cold compaction** — Powder is pressed in a hardened steel die at 100–200 MPa (uniaxial) or in an elastomeric bag at 200–400 MPa (CIP) → green body of ≈ 55–65 % theoretical density.
4. **Debinding** — Heating to 400–600 °C to burn out organic binders.
5. **Sintering** — Heating in a furnace (1500–1700 °C for Al₂O₃; 1900–2100 °C for SiC) under controlled atmosphere (air, Ar, or N₂).

**Role of Sintering Aids (2 Marks):**

* Sintering aids (typically **Y₂O₃, Al₂O₃, MgO, or rare-earth oxides**, added at 2–10 wt %) form a **transient liquid phase** at the sintering temperature.
* This liquid enhances **diffusion rates**, enables **particle rearrangement**, and **fills residual pores** through capillary action.
* Example: Si₃N₄ cannot be sintered to high density without Y₂O₃ + Al₂O₃ aids because of its high covalency and low self-diffusion coefficient.

**Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the flow diagram.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **three stages of solid-state sintering** of ceramics, indicating the dominant mass-transport mechanism in each stage. Use the appropriate densification equation in your explanation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Initial Stage (Density 60 → 65 %) (2 Marks):**
* Particle contacts flatten to form **necks** of radius $x$.
* **Dominant mechanism:** surface diffusion and evaporation–condensation.
* Material redistributes from convex to concave surfaces without bulk shrinkage; densification is therefore *minimal* even though necks grow.

**Stage 2 — Intermediate Stage (Density 65 → 90 %) (2.5 Marks):**
* Pores form a **continuous, cylindrical channel network** along grain edges.
* **Dominant mechanism:** **grain-boundary diffusion** and **volume diffusion**.
* The Kingery–Bhatkar equation describes neck growth:

$$
\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{3 \gamma_{sv} \, \delta^{3} \, D_{gb}}{r^{4} k T} \, t\right)^{1/4}
$$

* Significant **bulk shrinkage** (~ 5–10 %) occurs in this stage.

**Stage 3 — Final Stage (Density 90 → 99 %) (2.5 Marks):**
* Pores become **isolated, near-spherical** and located at grain boundaries or triple junctions.
* **Dominant mechanism:** **volume diffusion** along grain boundaries; eventually grain growth competes with densification.
* Coble's equation:

$$
\dfrac{d \rho_{rel}}{dt} = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sv} \, \Omega \, D_{gb}}{G^{3} \, k T} \cdot \dfrac{(1 - \rho_{rel})}{\rho_{rel}}
$$

* Grain size $G$ has a strong inverse cubic effect: finer grains densify much faster.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **hot pressing** process for ceramic matrix composites with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** over pressureless sintering. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 Marks):** Hot pressing is a **pressure-assisted densification** technique in which ceramic powder (with reinforcement) is simultaneously heated (1400–2200 °C) and uniaxially compressed (10–50 MPa) inside a graphite die. The combined action of temperature and pressure accelerates **plastic flow, grain-boundary sliding, and diffusion creep**, enabling densification at lower temperatures and shorter times than pressureless sintering.

**Apparatus (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for block diagram) (1.5 Marks):**
* Hydraulic press with water-cooled rams.
* Graphite die, punches, and susceptors.
* Induction or resistance heating.
* Controlled atmosphere (vacuum / Ar / N₂).

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
1. Final density > 99 % theoretical.
2. Sintering temperature reduced by 200–300 °C.
3. Suppresses decomposition of Si₃N₄ and SiC.
4. Finer grain size → higher strength and toughness.
5. Reduced cycle time and superior mechanical properties.

**Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
1. Limited to **simple shapes** (HP); HIP adds cost.
2. Graphite die wear and oxidation above 2000 °C.
3. Anisotropic properties due to uniaxial pressing.
4. Slow throughput; high capital cost.

---

**(b)** Describe the **reaction bonding** processes for **(i)** silicon nitride (RBSN) and **(ii)** silicon carbide (RBSC). State the governing chemical reactions and the key engineering advantage of each. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) RBSN — Reaction Bonded Silicon Nitride (3.5 Marks):**

* **Starting material:** compacted Si powder (with sintering aids optional).
* **Process:** nitridation in N₂ or NH₃ atmosphere at 1100–1400 °C.
* **Reaction:**

$$
3 \, \text{Si (s)} + 2 \, \text{N}_{2} \, \text{(g)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Si}_{3}\text{N}_{4} \, \text{(s)}; \quad \Delta H = -742 \, \text{kJ/mol}
$$

* **Mechanism:** Nucleation of α-Si₃N₄ whiskers at Si particle surfaces, followed by inward diffusion of N₂ and outward diffusion of Si.
* **Key Engineering Advantage:** **Near-zero dimensional change** during processing → ideal for **complex, net-shape turbine rotors** and large components that would warp if sintered.

**(ii) RBSC — Reaction Bonded / Reaction Formed Silicon Carbide (3.5 Marks):**

* **Starting material:** porous preform of **SiC particles + carbon** (or C-fibre preform).
* **Process:** capillary infiltration by **molten Si** at 1500–2000 °C in vacuum.
* **Reaction:**

$$
\text{Si (l)} + \text{C (s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SiC (s)}; \quad \Delta H = -73 \, \text{kJ/mol}
$$

* **Mechanism:** Molten Si wicks into the preform and reacts exothermically with carbon to form a continuous SiC matrix that bonds the original SiC grains (or fibres).
* **Key Engineering Advantage:** Ability to infiltrate **fibre preforms (C-fibre, SiC-fibre)** without damaging them → enables fabrication of **SiC/SiC turbine shrouds** for jet engines. Residual Si (5–15 %) is the main drawback above 1400 °C.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HP with HIP.** HP is uniaxial (graphite die, simple shapes, 10–50 MPa); HIP is isostatic gas pressure (100–200 MPa, complex shapes, requires encapsulation). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for interchanging the two.
> 2. **Writing "hot pressing increases density because of heat"** — this is *not* a complete answer. You MUST mention **simultaneous pressure + creep + diffusion** as the mechanism.
> 3. **Forgetting sintering aids in Si₃N₄** — monolithic Si₃N₄ has a self-diffusion coefficient so low that it will **NOT densify without Y₂O₃/Al₂O₃ aids**. Examiners will not award full marks if this point is missing.
> 4. **Stating that RBSN is fully dense.** RBSN is typically **15–25 % porous**; it almost always requires a post-HIP step for structural use.
> 5. **Skipping the stoichiometric calculation** in reaction-bonding questions. Always show the **balanced chemical equation** AND a numerical mass/volume calculation.
> 6. **Using $\gamma$ instead of $\gamma_{sv}$** — always specify the interface (solid-vapour, solid-solid, solid-liquid) for surface energy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list. It is the KTU-premier-engine "last-night-before-exam" cheat sheet for this topic.**

* [ ] **CMC = ceramic matrix + ceramic reinforcement**; goal = combine high-T strength with fracture toughness.
* [ ] **Three principal processing routes:** Cold press + sinter, Hot press / HIP, Reaction bonding (RBSN / RBSC).
* [ ] **Cold pressing** is at room temperature; produces a **green body** of 50–65 % theoretical density.
* [ ] **Sintering** consolidates the green body by atomic diffusion at 1500–2200 °C; **3 stages** = initial (neck), intermediate (channel pores), final (isolated pores).
* [ ] **Driving force for sintering** = reduction in surface free energy → Laplace pressure $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{sv}/r$; **smaller particles** ⇒ larger $\Delta P$ ⇒ faster sintering.
* [ ] **Sintering aids** (Y₂O₃, Al₂O₃, MgO) form a transient liquid phase that accelerates densification; **mandatory for Si₃N₄**.
* [ ] **Hot pressing** = simultaneous heat + uniaxial pressure in graphite die → density > 99 %, finer grains, lower T.
* [ ] **HIP** = isostatic gas pressure (100–200 MPa) → complex shapes, requires encapsulation.
* [ ] **Reaction bonding** forms matrix *in situ* by chemical reaction; **shrinkage ≈ 0 %**; ideal for net-shape parts.
* [ ] **RBSN reaction:** $3\text{Si} + 2\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$; nitrided at 1100–1400 °C; porosity 15–25 %.
* [ ] **RBSC reaction:** $\text{Si(l)} + \text{C(s)} \rightarrow \text{SiC}$; silicon-infiltrated at 1500–2000 °C; residual Si is the main drawback.
* [ ] **Key equations** (memorise the form, not the constant): Laplace pressure, Kingery neck-growth, Murray hot-pressing, Coble final-stage.
* [ ] **Selection rule of thumb:**
  * **Cheap + simple shape** → Cold press + sinter.
  * **High density + structural** → Hot press / HIP.
  * **Complex net shape + low residual stress** → Reaction bonding.
* [ ] **Always mention** matrix-reinforcement **chemical compatibility** and **CTE mismatch** as the two principal design constraints.
* [ ] **Cofiring** is *not* covered here — do NOT confuse it with co-sintering, which is a separate advanced topic.
* [ ] **Residual thermal stress** around reinforcement: $\sigma_{res} = E_m \Delta T (\alpha_r - \alpha_m)$ → use to estimate **safe reinforcement volume fraction**.
